File.Name: c428a_c428-027.jpg
Notes:
7123239467 This is a fantastic Dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) that I found today on the hillside above our home this morning. I believe it's a Flame Skipper (Libellula saturata). Look at those amazing structural struts on it's wings! (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2012)

I got this photo just after my mushroom shot a few photos back, and my camera was still set on f/20. *Sigh* But I took lots of photos, and this one came out well - with a boost from the extra DoF.

I had work to do after noon today, but I got some nice photos in the morning!

File.Name: c428b_c428-033.jpg
Notes:
7123202231 This is amazing Dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) that I found today on the hillside above our home this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2012)

Flipping through my book on Dragonflies and Damselflies of the West (Dennis Paulson, 2009), I believe it is a Flame Skipper (Libellula saturata). This one is some ways from standing water, but it was attracted to the alate termites in the air. It let me get fairly close - this photo is almost full frame.
Locality: Libellula saturata). This one is some ways from standing water
CommonNames: Dragonfly; Dragonflies and Damselflies of the West; Flame Skipper

File.Name: c428c_c428-009.jpg
Notes:
7123118645 This is a remarkable tiny Parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) with its abdomen thrust forward depositing an egg on an unfortunate little aphid. Maybe the wasp is family Braconidae? (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2012)

This action is taking place on a native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family.

This is an 800x800 crop from my original 4288x2848 pixel original photo taken at 1:1 magnification with my 105 mm macro lens. I need extension tubes to get closer!

File.Name: c428d_c428-013.jpg
Notes:
7123073027 Look close, there's a lot happening in this photo!

There's a pair of large aphid "fundatrices" for this colony - the first aphids that produce new aphids parthenogenetically and viviparously - and you can see the lower one squeezing out a new baby aphid. There's also a tiny Parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) on the right, though I think she's more interested in the small, blurry aphid below the large one. Maybe the wasp is family Braconidae? (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2012)

This action is taking place on a native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family.

File.Name: c428e_c428-040.jpg
Notes: 6976961050 There's no mistaking the bottle-neck shape of this spider's body! This is a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae) on the end of a grass blade today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2012)

File.Name: c428f_c428-022.jpg
Notes: 7123021595 I believe this is a brilliant Boletus flaviporus mushroom growing in the woods morning under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It has a viscid cap and yellow pores that don't stain blue. It also has a "somewhat reticuate" stem. It's said to be "edible, but mediocre". Sure is pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2012)

File.Name: c428g_c428-025.jpg
Notes: 7122988295 This is a wonderful new flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the in the Rosaceae plant family. It makes a prefect mandala with those outer sepals and inner petals and lollipop stamens. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2012)

File.Name: c428h_c427-079.jpg
Notes:
7122920181 These are interesting new insect galls on the leaves of the different native Scrub Oak (Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens) in the Fagaceae plant family. The leaves of this scrub oak are thinner and shiny underneath, quite different from Q. berberidifolia. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2012)

I'm not sure what kind of gall this is. The best match in my book is the Succulent Gall Wasp (Neuroterus fragilis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but that wasp is said to cause galls on "white oaks" and this is a "black oak".
CommonNames: galls; Scrub Oak; Succulent Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; Q. berberidifolia; Neuroterus fragilis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; Q. berberidifolia; Neuroterus fragilis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c429a_c429-035.jpg
Notes:
6980852486 This is the precious developing seed pod of a rare Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2012)

It was breezy today - enough to make photographing insects impossible. I suspect it was just "fog wind" caused by the instability between the marine layer and the dry upper layer of the atmosphere, but wind is wind. I gave up trying to get photos at home, and went for a drive up into the mountains along East Camino Cielo road.

File.Name: c429b_c429-033.jpg
Notes:
7126903777 These are the last two flowers of the rare Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in our mountains. Only this one plant had flowers this year. The flowers are now falling apart. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2012)

See my photo of a flower at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7089257141/

File.Name: c429c_c429-052.jpg
Notes: 7126874241 These are the elegant new flower buds of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It won't belong until we have flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2012)

File.Name: c429d_c429-013.jpg
Notes: 7126828327 This is the cute little whole plant of Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's only a couple of inches high, growing on bare shale soil where nothing else can grow. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 April 2012)

File.Name: c429e_c429-006.jpg
Notes:
6980673208 These are new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 April 2012)

They might be the longest-flowering plant in our mountains - at least four months! See my photo from October 29:
   http://us.fotolog.com/treebeard/68150218/

File.Name: c429f_c429-019.jpg
Notes:
6980630314 These are the elegant flowers of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The shiny leaves are pretty too. They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 April 2012)

I was surprised to find these flowers still going strong higher in the mountains today, but here they are. It's been an interesting weather year, and the late-season March/April rains have given some native shrubs a second life.

File.Name: c429g_c429-011.jpg
Notes:
7126650757 These are spiky fruit of Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Compare my photo of Ribes Amarum which is quite glandular. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 April 2012)

The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. The trick is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside.

File.Name: c429h_c429-065.jpg
Notes: 7126627615 This is a pretty cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms in the woods today. They are growing from an old stump of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. They fruited here earlier this year, as early as November. It's odd to see them again this year, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2012)
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: c430a_c430-035.jpg
Notes:
6984450204 Love play among the butterflies!

These are some kind of Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterflies doing their foreplay on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2012)

I assume that's a "she" at the bottom of the photo, since "he" was doing the courting. She was rapidly vibrating her tail, while he was flying back and forth. In a few minutes they flew away.

I had some photo-work for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society this afternoon. Six photos are more than enough tonight!

File.Name: c430b_c430-028.jpg
Notes:
7130489211 Here is another impossibly thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Hemiptera perched on the new flower of a native Phacelia in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It's a hard flower group to to be sure of, but this might be the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida). The flowers look pale and washed out. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2012)

Look close - you can clearly see the bug's proboscis! It was working the flower before I disturbed it.
ScientificName: Berytidae; Hemiptera; Phacelia; Hydrophyllaceae; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Hemiptera; Phacelia; Hydrophyllaceae; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida

File.Name: c430c_c430-020.jpg
Notes: 7130465293 I call these "Lupine Aphids" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera. These are on a stem of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. They are quite large for aphids. They pack the stems and do some damage to the plants. I haven't found any ants tending them - they manage on their own! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2012)

File.Name: c430d_c430-012.jpg
Notes: 6984314700 This is a little Buprestid beetle (family Buprestidae in the order Coleoptera) on a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. I guess it is a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. There are lots of species. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2012)
CommonNames: Buprestid; Bush Monkey Flower; Spotted Flower Buprestid

File.Name: c430e_c430-003.jpg
Notes:
7130357277 Our newly hatched spiderlings have moved up to an old red fruit on this Prickley Pear (Opuntia sp.) cactus in the garden. They've been busy spinning new webs, but they still like to clump together. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2012)

I believe the mama spider is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae - maybe the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) or kin. See my photo from a few days ago at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7114515377/
CommonNames: Prickley Pear; Orb-Weaver Spider; Cat-Faced Spider

File.Name: c430f_c430-039.jpg
Notes:
6984235690 I'm pretty sure this the fantastic seed head of the Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. The seed head is quite large, 2+ inches, and the seeds have straight ribs. It's a native perennial in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2012)

Does that sound right, Lynn? (I know you're working on this.)

File.Name: c501a_c501-008.jpg
Notes:
7134186755 I believe this is a new flower of Slender Tarweed (Madia gracilis) in the Asteraceae plant family - with fog drip. The pretty composite flowers usually close early in the morning, so I rarely see them - but they stay open on drizzly days. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

It was gloomy and drizzly all day today. We never saw the sun. But the low light was just right for a few flowers, except for a nagging breeze.

File.Name: c501b_c501-010.jpg
Notes:
6988033532 These are new flowers of Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla) in the woods- with fog drip. The whorled flowers look like lupines at first, but they are really quite different. They are in the Scrophulariaceae plant family, related to penstemon and paintbrush. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

The flowers are variable - the color varies from deep violet (like these) to pure white. Some plants are quite pale, almost another species.
Locality: like these) to pure white.  Some plants are quite pale

File.Name: c501c_c501-039.jpg
Notes: 7134098855 This is the Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) in the Fabaceae plant family. It took a drizzly day to get the colors right! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

File.Name: c501d_c501-047.jpg
Notes:
7134033687 These are pretty new flowers of (probably) native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It took a drizzly day to get the colors right! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

There's some confusion about what species our plants really are. The real S. mollis is said to only grow a foot or two tall, and our plants (more like S. albus) are 2-4 feet tall. It definitely some kind of Snowberry!
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus

File.Name: c501e_c501-020.jpg
Notes:
7133987783 I believe this "Little Brown Mushroom" (LBM) is the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox). It has a smooth brown cap that cracks, adnate gills, and a faint ring on the stem. It's said to be edible, but it's not recommended because there are too darn many LBMs! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

This one is growing on the ground with (mostly) California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family.

File.Name: c501f_c501-025.jpg
Notes:
7133938989 This "Little Brown Mushroom" is large! It's open-hand size, growing on wood chips along the road. The mature cap on the right looks like a hot cross bun! I don't know what they are, but I suspect they come with the wood chipper. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

These mushrooms have brown spores and a partial veil that leaves an evanescent ring. I have no idea what they are or if they are edible. If you know what it is, please share!

File.Name: c501g_c501-002.jpg
Notes: 6987800454 I found this archetypal "Little Brown Mushroom" (LBM) in the woods this morning growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It has thin flesh and brown spores. It don't know what it is, and won't try to guess. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

File.Name: c501h_c501-021.jpg
Notes:
6987777840 Here is another beautiful dark-spored Inky Cap (genus Coprinus) mushrooms coming up along our road. This one is hiding under a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. They are quite large - maybe 2 inches across. The caps are thinner than tissue paper and easier to tear. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

Mushrooms in this genus "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime.

File.Name: c502a_c502-023.jpg
Notes:
6991433190 These are fantastic tiny wasp galls on a staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe they are the galls of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? The galls are only a few mm long. Catkins seem such a temporary place to build a house! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2012)

I've been at a disadvantage tonight, because TCM has been showing one of my all-time favorite movies - Preston Sturges' Sullivan's Travels (1942). It's a perfect blend of screwball comedy and serious, with maybe the best last line of any movie:

"There's a lot to be said for making people laugh. Did you know that that's all some people have? It isn't much, but it's better than nothing in this cockeyed caravan.
Locality: 1942).  It's a perfect blend of screwball comedy and serious
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp; Sullivan's Travels
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; There's a lot to be said for making people laugh.  Did you know that that's all some people have?  It isn't much, but it's better than nothing in this cockeyed caravan.
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; There's a lot to be said for making people laugh.  Did you know that that's all some people have?  It isn't much, but it's better than nothing in this cockeyed caravan.

File.Name: c502b_c502-021.jpg
Notes:
6991399788 This caterpillar has clown colors!

Here are the psychedelic colors and form of a Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin. It's working a new leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2012)

The adults of these colorful caterpillars are dull moths. The female is just a flightless stubby-winged bag that never crawls far from her cocoon. I don't know that I've ever noticed them.
ScientificName: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: c502c_c502-006.jpg
Notes: 6991338850 I found this odd little fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. I think from its round head and flattened proboscis that it is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae. Compare this photo of the genus Rhamphomyia at BugGuide.net. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2012)
ScientificName: Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Empididae; Rhamphomyia
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Empididae; Rhamphomyia

File.Name: c502d_c502-008.jpg
Notes:
6991254616 This is a perfect little Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Rhagio. It's perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. Their abdomens remind me of those collapsible cups I used to carry when backpacking. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2012)

We also have biting snipe flies (genus Symphoromyia) that bother us later in the summer, but these flies are benign.
Locality: genus Symphoromyia) that bother us later in the summer
ScientificName: Rhagionidae; Diptera; Rhagio; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Symphoromyia
associatedTaxa: Rhagionidae; Diptera; Rhagio; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Symphoromyia

File.Name: c502e_c502-018.jpg
Notes: 6991219910 These are well-camouflaged little mushrooms in the woods. I walked right past them twice without noticing, but I found them on the third trip. I'm not sure what they are, but I guess genus Clitocybe with those decurrent gills growing down the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2012)

File.Name: c502f_c501-042.jpg
Notes: 6991203292 I believe this is a large Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom in the woods. They have a distinctive thick ring that stands out from the stalk. The cap tastes mild and they look delicious, but they are poisonous to many people. I don't trust any of our woodland agaricus mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2012)

File.Name: c502g_c502-026.jpg
Notes:
7137264949 This is a pretty new flower cluster of native Tomcat Clover (Trifolium willdenovii) in the Fabaceae plant family. I got this photo on the hillside by our mailbox today. It's hard to show both flowers and leaves in the same photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2012)

*Sigh* I used to know all our native clovers years ago, but memory fails if you don't use it. This clover is quite distinctive with those pretty flowers and long narrow leaflets.

File.Name: c502h_c502-016.jpg
Notes: 6991137558 These are new flowers of Allophyllum glutinosum in the Polemoniaceae plant family. They're interesting because they are usually one of our summer wildflowers, and these seem early to be flowering. But I also love that blue pollen! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2012)

File.Name: c503a_c503-039.jpg
Notes:
7140742341 These are the fantastic new flowers of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Did you notice the tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) on the left-most petal? (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

These are the endemic var. inezianus in our mountains. The flowers are larger and have greenish glands instead of yellow like the species. They seem distinctive.

The flowers do a little dance as they mature. They start with both stamens and pistils held up. Then the stamens lower like merry-go-round carousel horses to transfer pollen. Then they rise and the pistils lower to receive pollen.

Lynn, I know that they are now genus Toxicoscordion in TJM2, but I'll need some time to make the change. It took me years to get used to Jepson after Munz, and I still first think Happlopappus when I see Ericameria or Isocoma!
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas; wasp
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Hymenoptera; inezianus; Toxicoscordion; Happlopappus; Ericameria; Isocoma
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Hymenoptera; inezianus; Toxicoscordion; Happlopappus; Ericameria; Isocoma

File.Name: c503b_c503-033.jpg
Notes: 6994608344 This is the whole plant of a beautiful Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's only about one foot tall. This is the only plant I've found in flower in this drought year. See my [Newer] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: c503c_c503-048.jpg
Notes:
7140665653 These are the small flowers of native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's the first of our "Farewell-to-Spring" wildflowers that I've seen this year. The nodding buds are diagnostic of the species. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

This seems to be an uncommon plant in our mountains. I only find it in this one canyon - where it is quite common.

File.Name: c503d_c503-031.jpg
Notes:
6994551426 These are fantastic new native flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family - the first I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

I mostly find skipper butterflies and small bees on the flowers. I wonder what insect they are made for?

File.Name: c503e_c503-027.jpg
Notes: 7140607135 This is a beautiful male Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly perched on a grass seed head in a place that was mostly covered with Deerweed (Lotus scoparius). It's showing that these Blue butterflies really are blue! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

File.Name: c503f_c503-007.jpg
Notes:
6994479982 Here is another photo of the interesting late-season "infundibular" (funnel-shaped) mushrooms in our woods. I'm pretty sure they are genus Clitocybe with those decurrent gills growing down the stem. They are growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

It's curious - I've seen quite a few of these this year, but I don't remember them from past years. Maybe it's my failing memory, or maybe this is a mushroom that thrives in drought years?

File.Name: c503g_c503-014.jpg
Notes:
6994450552 The Grateful Dead had the "wall of sound", and we have a wall of mushrooms!

This is another photo of the cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms in the woods today. They are growing from an old stump of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see that they are brown-spored. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

They fruited here earlier this year, as early as November. It's odd to see them again in May, but here they are.
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: c503h_c502-001.jpg
Notes:
6994436438 I have no doubt that these are the late-season Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox) mushroom. It has a smooth brown cap that cracks as it dries, adnate gills, and a faint ring on the stem. These are growing along the road in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2012)

It's said to be edible, but it's not recommended because there are too darn many Little Brown Mushrooms!

File.Name: c504a_c504-024.jpg
Notes: 6997706750 Here are a mating pair of Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterflies. They are "doing it" on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. It's spring! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2012)

File.Name: c504b_c504-007.jpg
Notes: 6997689408 I found this small "micro-moth" (Lepidoptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family by the creek today. I usually try to get insect eyes sharp, but looking at my photos, the wings steal all the attention. It has hackles, like a rooster! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2012)

File.Name: c504c_c504-003.jpg
Notes:
7143742261 This is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a new leaf of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2012)

These are real "hover flies" that hover in the air and rarely perch. *Sigh* I had my exposure turned down 1.3 stops, and it's still over-exposed.

File.Name: c504d_c503-029.jpg
Notes: 7143732943 I found this handsome Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera on my trail yesterday. I'm sorry Alice, I didn't see it's wings when it flew away. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

File.Name: c504e_c503-064.jpg
Notes:
7143716409 I found another clutch of newly hatched spiderlings in the woods yesterday. I don't know what kind of spider they (or at least a few of them) will become. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

Looking at this mass of spiderlings, it's interesting to think that every male and female couple have to produce just two offspring in their lifetime to replace them to maintain a stable population...

File.Name: c504f_c503-046.jpg
Notes:
6997573486 This is the nest of an unusual (though locally common) spider in our woods. I believe it's a Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. They decorate their inverted-thimble nests with the husks of their prey, like pirates! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

Steve Lew's Spiders Of California lists six of these interesting spiders in California.

File.Name: c504g_c504-015.jpg
Notes:
6997543208 Could these pure white "mushrooms" be Fairy Fingers (Clavaria vermicularis) in the Clavariaceae? They have unbranched stems that are very brittle. I found them in the woods today growing from a seep on a sandstone outcrop. They are said to be edible but insubstantial. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2012)

I expect to find these in Oregon in the fall, but here they are in a drought-year spring. I've never found them before in our mountains. On the other hand, I'm not usually looking for mushrooms at this time of year.

File.Name: c504h_c503-050.jpg
Notes:
6997496208 Here are the first new flowers I've found of the endemic native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. These white-leaved shrubs are a fire-follower that lingers. They are good butterfly flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2012)

This shrub is named for the naturalist Blanche Trask who studied the botany of the channel islands 100 years ago. Interesting lady! There's a collection of her writing, well worth reading, on the web at:
   http://naturespeace.org/blanchetraskanthology.htm

File.Name: c505a_c505-006.jpg
Notes:
7001036450 Well blow me down...
(as Popeye used to say)

I was surprised to find this perfect new Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom growing under a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains today. There are also Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees nearby. It's beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2012)

Despite the Italian name, the Coccora is only known from the west coast of the U.S. It has a thick white "volva" - the cup at the base of the stem - and an equally thick volval patch on the cap. It also has a distinct partial veil ring on the stem.

This mushroom is said to be edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. But this really is a distinctive mushroom that I should try. My book describes it as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't encouraging.

Julie and I are heading east for a few days of camping and exploring. We'll be back in a few days, hopefully with new photos!
CommonNames: Coccora; Fir; Coast Live Oak; Madrone
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Amanita
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Amanita

File.Name: c505b_c505-002.jpg
Notes:
7147072359 This battle-scarred Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) posed for me this morning under the native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2012)

Mentally draw a line between its eyes, and draw another line from the front of its face and along its back... behind where they cross, you can see the mysterious "third eye" of this lizard!

If you do a Google search on "western fence lizard third eye" you'll get lots of hits. It's so interesting that Descartes picked the pineal gland (our third eye, somewhat buried) as the seat of the soul.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Wild Blackberry

File.Name: c505c_c505-047.jpg
Notes:
7000925016 It's my custom to come home from the day's hike, and drink a cold beer on the front porch while I flip through the day's photos on my camera screen. Today was different...

Suddenly, the air was full of winged Termites (order Isoptera)! They were emerging from a sawed-off log with pots on it about two feet from where I was sitting. I moved my camera and got a few quick photos without checking my settings. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2012)

The whole emergence was over in a minute or two. Birds and lizards moved in to grab them! If you look on the left below the termites, you can see sandy-looking tunnels with new termites emerging. I'm glad this isn't the foundation of our house!

File.Name: c505d_c505-003.jpg
Notes:
7000911818 These are new flowers of the native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family in our mountains. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They seem almost too beautiful to be native chaparral plants, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2012)

See my earlier photo for more info.

File.Name: c505e_c505-030.jpg
Notes:
7000876166 Here is a shrub of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family, full of the fantastic white flowers. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They seem almost too beautiful to be native chaparral plants, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2012)

See my earlier photo for more info./

File.Name: c505f_c505-016.jpg
Notes:
7000845634 The native plants in our mountains are confused by the weather this year - and so am I. We had a pretty wet fall, then a very dry winter, followed by a pretty wet spring. Despite our recent March-April rains, we still only stand at about 50% normal rainfall.

This is native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Look - it has developing seed pods from its earlier flowers, and now it has new flowers again! I haven't see this behavior before, but I reckon it's a good thing these native shrubs have a new trick up their sleeves! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2012)

File.Name: c505g_c505-040.jpg
Notes:
7146857635 This photo should make you pause if you know the oak trees in Santa Barbara County. You can see from the catkins that it's definitely an oak (genus Quercus, family Fagaceae) - but which oak? It's a small tree with thin, mostly entire leaves, which are shiny on both sides - not like a local oak at all!

I believe this is an unusual "Shreve Oak" (Quercus shrevei) - though it's also been called Q. parvula; or Q. parvula var. shrevei; or maybe Q. wislizenii var. frutescens, or even Q. wislizenii! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2012)

These unusual trees grow in small groves that are maybe clones. Once you learn to recognize them, you can spot the groves here and there in the mountains. They are mostly on West Camino Cielo, but there also a grove along Fremont Ridge to the east.
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Q. wislizenii
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Q. wislizenii

File.Name: c505h_c505-022.jpg
Notes: 7000721518 This is a new flower of native annual Brewer's Red Maids (Calandrinia breweri) in the Portulacaceae plant family growing in a recently cleared place in the mountains. It usually grows as a fire follower, but sometimes clearing the chaparral is enough to trigger these plants. It is different from the much more common C. ciliata, and not as common. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2012)

File.Name: c510a_c510-038.jpg
Notes:
7174350910 Today's photo always seem best, even when I just got back from a trip with many new photos!

This is an elegant new flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's kind of an upside-down Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2012)

It's a "tender trap" for pollinators - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those hairs without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. This is the first one I've seen this year at home.

We got back from our trip on Tuesday night, but Wednesday was my "busy day" with my DMS class and a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting.
CommonNames: Fairy Lantern; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society
ScientificName: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Mariposa Lily; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Mariposa Lily; Liliaceae

File.Name: c510b_c507-135.jpg
Notes:
7174266074 I first thought (and hoped) this little snake was a Night Snake (Hypsiglena torquata), but I'm wrong. Ron Wolfe and Aliceinwl1 both agree that it is a young Pacific Gopher Snake (Pituophis catenifer catenifer). One difference is that night snakes have vertical pupils, and they are quite round in this photo. Thanks for the help Ron and Alice - there's always something to learn!

This little snake came down a tree at our KCL Campground on the Carrizo Plains near sundown when light was fading. Fortunately, it stayed very still for this 3/4 second exposure. After a few minutes, it went back up the tree. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

These little snakes look a bit like rattlesnakes, but they lack rattles and the typical triangular head. They are no threat.

File.Name: c510c_c508-005.jpg
Notes:
7174150128 Here is a barely-fledged Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus) perched in the morning in a Eucalyptus tree at KCL Campground on the Carrizo Plains. It's still fuzzy, with no horns. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 8 May 2012)

I think it flew away for it's very first hunt the night before. We saw it in its nest in the evening. Other birders in the campground said it had never left the nest before. It's a great moment!

File.Name: c510d_c508-003.jpg
Notes:
7174061234 This is an adult Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus) perched in the morning in a Eucalyptus tree at KCL Campground on the Carrizo Plains. It has very large horns. It's large - like a football balanced in the tree! (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 8 May 2012)

This adult was keeping its eye on its young. See my next photo.

File.Name: c510e_c508-019.jpg
Notes: 7174007652 This is a shockingly beautiful Bullock's Oriole (Icterus bullockii) in a desert shrub near our campsite at KCL Campground on the Carrizo Plains. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 8 May 2012)

File.Name: c510f_c508-012.jpg
Notes: 7173971260 I believe this is a male Brewer's Blackbird (Euphagus cyanocephalus) bird in a non-native Eucalyptus tree at KCL Campground on the Carrizo Plains. They are slender and long-tailed, quite beautiful. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 8 May 2012)

File.Name: c510g_c508-024.jpg
Notes: 7173891514 This is a family group of Pronghorn Antelope (Antilocapra americana) on the Carrizo Plains, with the Temblor Mountains in the background. If you look close, you can see that the second antelope from the left has horns. Dry as it's been this year, there is a good bit of grass. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 8 May 2012)

File.Name: c510h_c508-027.jpg
Notes: 7173821180 Here is Julie on the edge of dry Soda Lake in the Carrizo Plain National Monument. She didn't know I took this photo. As the great Lord Buckley once said, "Dig infinity" - and she dug it! (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 8 May 2012)

File.Name: c511a_c511-002.jpg
Notes:
7179735176 Today's photos always seem best, even when I just got back from a trip with *many* new photos!

This is a brilliant Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working new flowers of the Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. Its wings look like panels of stained glass! I got this photo on the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River today, which still has a bit of flow. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 May 2012)

The rest of today's photos are all interesting native plants that seemed to need more than one photo to show them.

It's been hard to concentrate on comments tonight when TCM is showing "The Wrong Box" (1966). I don't know how I missed this film, but it's hilarious - a bit of Monty Python and one of Peter Sellers best roles!
Locality: 1966).  I don't know how I missed this film
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Bicolored Yerba Santa

File.Name: c511b_c507-063.jpg
Notes:
7179697852 These are new flowers of the remarkable "Poodle Dog Bush" (Turricula parryi) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It's not common - or rather, rarely common - as a fire-follower after (I believe) the La Brea wildfire in August, 2009. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

When I first saw these plants without flowers, I thought Yerba Santa (genus Eriodictyon). I was close!

File.Name: c511c_c507-061.jpg
Notes: 7179639280 Here are new flowering plants of "Poodle Dog Bush" (Turricula parryi) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - somewhat obscured by chamise and a barb-wire fence. The only plants we found flowering were on private land in the lower parts of the canyon. It's not common - or rather, rarely common - as a fire-follower after (I believe) the La Brea wildfire in August, 2009. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c511d_c507-007.jpg
Notes: 7179550214 This is new growth of "Poodle Dog Bush" (Turricula parryi) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It's not common - or rather, rarely common - as a fire-follower. These are growing near the Bates Canyon Campground off Highway 166 in the Cuyama Valley, where it is quite common. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c511e_c507-043.jpg
Notes: 7179512382 This is a remarkable new flower of native Golden Eardrops (Dicentra chrysantha) in the Papaveraceae plant family. They look like penguins! The plants are growing as fire-followers after (I believe) the La Brea wildfire in August, 2009. They have poppy-like foliage. See my [Older] photo for a more distant shot. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c511f_c507-038.jpg
Notes:
7179416660 These are amazing new flowers of native Golden Eardrops (Dicentra chrysantha) in the Papaveraceae plant family. They have poppy-like foliage. See my [Newer] photo for a close shot. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

There were several of these plants growing as fire-followers after (I believe) the La Brea wildfire in August, 2009. The woods are recovering as they should, and there are lots of fire-follower wildflowers to prove it!
Locality: I believe) the La Brea wildfire in August

File.Name: c511g_c507-113.jpg
Notes: 7179346680 This is a closer shot of the flowers of native Pallid Larkspur (Delphinium parishii ssp. pallidum). These interesting natives grow 3-4 feet tall. See my [Older] photo for the whole plant. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c511h_c507-110.jpg
Notes: 7179303530 We didn't find fields of wildflowers in the Carrizo Plains in this dry year, but I did find some interesting plants. This is almost all of a native Pallid Larkspur (Delphinium parishii ssp. pallidum) hiding in the non-native grasses along my trail. These interesting natives grow 3-4 feet tall. See my [Newer] photo for a close shot. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c512a_c512-041.jpg
Notes:
7185550434 Today's photos always seem best, even when I just got back from a trip with *many* new photos!

This shy Coast Horned Lizard aka "horny toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii or P. coronatum) decided to pose for me today! I actually saw three of these lizards today, but this is the only one who stayed around for a photo shoot. Sure, they could get work in Hollywood posing for monster movies! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

*Too many photos* - what a dilemma! I have many more wildflower photos from our zigzag trip through Cuyama and the Carrizo Plains last week, but I've also been getting new photos every day since we've been back. It's time to move on...

File.Name: c512b_c507-074.jpg
Notes: 7185478532 I found this handsome Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera on the ground along my trail at the Carrizo Plains last week. It has brilliantly colored hind legs. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c512c_c506-009.jpg
Notes:
7185385088 Thanks again Lynn, this sure does look like Whiskerbrush (Linanthus ciliatus).

Original comment:
"Maybe these are the same Common Linanthus aka False Babystars (Linanthus androsaceus) flowers in the Polemoniaceae plant family as my [Older] photo, but they sure look different! I don't know. (Bates Canyon, 6 May 2012)

File.Name: c512d_c507-013.jpg
Notes: 7185300310 I believe these are pretty Common Linanthus aka False Babystars (Linanthus androsaceus) flowers in the Polemoniaceae plant family. They are quite variable, and these flowers are quite pretty. They are growing as fire-followers after (I believe) the La Brea wildfire in August, 2009. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c512e_c507-101.jpg
Notes: 7185235382 I found these beautiful flowers of the native Crinkled Onion (Allium crispum) in the Liliaceae plant family hiding in a patch of Farewell-to-Spring (Clarkia) flowers on a hillside along the trail from KCL Campground. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c512f_c507-089.jpg
Notes: 7185178188 This is a beautiful flower head of native Thistle Sage (Salvia carduacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I didn't really expect to find these on the Carrizo Plains last week, but here they are on the trail from KCL campground. The beetles sure are enjoying the flowers. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c512g_c508-037.jpg
Notes:
7184998876 There were lots of these Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working some composite flowers in the Asteraceae plant family. They were camera shy, but I got this one. It looks like its wearing goggles, with a neat little WWI trimmed mustache! (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispo County, 8 May 2012)

On ouy way home last week, we made a point to stop where the Shell Creek Road meets Highway 58 in the San Luis Obispso County backcountry, about 20 miles from Santa Margarita. The flowers were mostly done. Compare my photo from 2005 at:
   http://us.fotolog.com/treebeard/9834492/

File.Name: c512h_c508-035.jpg
Notes: 7184919736 I guess this beetle-infested flower is a Tidy Tips (Layia platyglossa) flower in the Asteraceae plant family , though there are other species like the Smooth Layia (L. chrysanthemoides). (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispo County, 8 May 2012)

File.Name: c513a_c507-052.jpg
Notes:
7193847924 Here is a beautiful spring flower of the Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family. I've seen a few at home too. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

Did you notice the tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the right?

I really wanted to get back to my new photos today, but these photos were too good to delegate to the archives. New photos tomorrow!

File.Name: c513b_c507-027.jpg
Notes:
7193782558 This is the real Woolly Morning-glory (Calystegia malacophylla ssp. pedicellata) in the Convolvulaceae plant family, without the wavy leaves. It seems to be growing as a fire-follower. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

Compare my photo of the South Coast Range Morning-glory (Calystegia collina ssp. venusta) which does have wavy leaves.

File.Name: c513c_c507-035.jpg
Notes: 7193729678 I guess this is the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. They have pale, washed-out flowers, but they make it up with those fantastic hairy seed heads. (Bates Canyon, 6 May 2012)

File.Name: c513d_c507-022.jpg
Notes:
7193633976 This is the real California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) in the in the Papaveraceae plant family. You can recognize it by the disk below the flower. So many of these flowers are spread from seed packs, that you have to go to a remote place like this to see the real thing! The red-tipped leaves are a bonus. (Bates Canyon, 7 May 2012)

The background is a mass of non-native Red Brome (Bromus rubens) grass. *Sigh*

Compare my photo of Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) which lacks the disk below the flower.
ScientificName: Eschscholzia californica; Papaveraceae; Bromus rubens; Eschscholzia caespitosa

File.Name: c513e_c506-008.jpg
Notes: 7193581354 These are new flowers of the native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. They are also starting to flower at home. The flowers are too sparse to be considered pretty, but the "scorpioid inflorescence" - coiled like a scorpion's tail or the neck of a violin - sure is nice. (Bates Canyon, 6 May 2012)

File.Name: c513f_c507-120.jpg
Notes: 7193404422 This is the gall of a Woolly Stem Gall Midge (Asphondylia floccosa) growing on some kind of Saltbush (Atriplex) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family on the Carrizo Plains. I'm not sure, but maybe it's Mohave Saltbush (Atriplex spinifera)? (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c513g_c507-081.jpg
Notes:
7193268592 This is an unusual patch of native grassland with (I guess) Purple Needlegrass (Nasella pulchra) in the Poaceae plant family, growing on the Carrizo Plains. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

California used to look very different - not the "golden rolling hills of California" - as Kate Wolf used to sing about so eloquently, bless her. The golden hills come from the non-native annual grasses. The natives grasses are different.

File.Name: c513h_c507-065.jpg
Notes: 7193194692 I guess this is the Diablo Milkvetch (Astragalus oxyphysus) in the in the Fabaceae plant family, growing in Cottonwood Canyon in the Cuyama River valley. I think the same plant was common on the Carrizo Plains. (Cottonwood Canyon, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c513i_c507-093.jpg
Notes:
7192977826 I'm pretty sure these are seed pods of a Freckled Milkvetch (Astragalus lentiginosus var. nigricalycis) in the Fabaceae plant family, growing on the Carrizo Plains. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

They are also known as "Locoweed" because some species, including maybe this one, contain swainsonine, a phytotoxin harmful to livestock. Sorry kids, you can't get high from it. For more info, start at Wikipedia:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Locoweed

File.Name: c513j_c507-124.jpg
Notes: 7192907684 This is a distinctive little Lupine (genus Lupinus) in the Fabaceae plant family, growing on the Carrizo Plains. I guess it is a Chick Lupine (Lupinus microcarpus var. microcarpus). The crowded flowers are stacked in whorls on the stem, it's quite hairy, and the seed pods are +/- erect. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, 7 May 2012)

File.Name: c514a_c512-016.jpg
Notes:
7200480402 This is an unusually brilliant white Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working white flowers of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It must be newly emerged. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

These large butterflies are often more yellowish. Sometimes they are hard to distinguish from the Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus) butterflies.
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; California Thistle; Western Tiger Swallowtail

Notes: 7200422308 Here is the first Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that I've managed to photograph this year. It's perched on a pure white flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I hope there will be many more! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c514c_c511-013.jpg
Notes: 7200356100 These are mating Robber Flies (family Asilidae) in the order Diptera. It's a little hard to make out the photo, but think that "she" (on the bottom?) has her long abdomen up behind "his" (on top?) hind legs, and their abdomens are joined on the left, towards the camera. It's quite sensual. I only got this one photo - somehow they flew away together for their privacy. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 May 2012)

File.Name: c514d_c512-044.jpg
Notes: 7200267846 This is a large Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae ) in the insect order Hemiptera. Probably it is the Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus). You can see a bit of its mighty proboscis. It's perched for the moment on a flowering stem of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

File.Name: c514e_c512-066.jpg
Notes:
7200132482 This is a fantastic little Wasp on an unknown gall of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. See my [Older] photo. I think it might be a Chalcid Wasp (family Chalcidoidea) - maybe Torymidae? - but I'm not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

This isn't the wasp that induces the gall, but it's a parasite looking to lay an egg on the different wasp larvae inside the gall. Galls are complicated like that.
CommonNames: Wasp; gall; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Chalcid Wasp

File.Name: c514f_c512-070.jpg
Notes:
7200026112 I'm not sure what kind of galls these are on stems of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family - but there sure are lots of them in this dry year, more than I've ever seen before. The galls are quite large, an inch or more long, and they cause a bend in the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

Maybe they are the gall of a Kidney Stem Gall Wasp (Andricus reniformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera?

I almost forgot about this photo from our road trip last week, but It's too good to skip!

7207179246 A gathering of (small) minds...

This is a group meeting of tiny ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) under a grass blade. I have no idea what they were discussing! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2012)

I'm pretty sure these ants are genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. Look close at the ants on the left and bottom. They have two spines on their thorax and an articulated attachment to their abdomen.

File.Name: c515d_c512-025.jpg
Notes:
7207131112 These are new flowers of Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Papaveraceae plant family. These flowers always remind me of Holy Rollers in white robes having a meeting in the woods with arms raised for glory! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

They are another one of those curious fire-follower plants that sprout after wildfires and flower for a few years, and then disappear into the seedbank - not to be seen until the next wildfire. I've only found this one plant this year, and it's probably the end of them - hopefully for a while!

File.Name: c515e_c512-021.jpg
Notes:
7207064430 This is one of our strangest fire-follower plants in the mountains - Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Papaveraceae plant family. See my [Newer] photo to appreciate how strange they are! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

These plants act as typical fire-follwers in our mountains - they flower like crazy for a few years after a wildfire, and then they disappear into the seedbank to wait for the next wildfire. I didn't expect to find any this year, but here they are.

This place never actually burned, but it was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break - a few years ago. For some of these fire-followers, it's enough to clear the ground.

File.Name: c515f_c513-036.jpg
Notes: 7206966294 This is a native flower of the Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. This is another of the fire-follower plants that flower in great profusion for a few years after a wildfire, and then disappear into the seedbank to wait for the next wildfire. I didn't expect to find any this year, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2012)

File.Name: c515g_c513-016.jpg
Notes: 7206909708 I believe this is California Mustard (Thelypodium lasiophyllum aka Guillenia lasiophylla) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a real native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. It has long seed-pods that spread out from the stem. It's not common. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2012)

File.Name: c515h_c513-041.jpg
Notes:
7206853796 This is a tiny native flower of Mimulus floribundus in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing in a spring-fed wet place in the chaparral on ground burned by the Gap Wildfire four years ago. The glandular leaves feel distinctly slimy when you grasp them. It's uncommon in our mountains, like the habitat.

These flowers are especially uncommon in this drought year. I only found one clump, barely hanging on. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

File.Name: c516a_c516-002.jpg
Notes:
7213281310 This is a remarkable Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera). It looks a bit like the Common Blow Fly or Blue Bottle Fly. But these are trash flies, and I found this critter in the woods, perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. And what gives with those brilliant red eyes and that yellow beard? (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2012)

I did some browsing at BugGuide, and I now suspect this fly is the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria). See the images at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/36836/bgimage

If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. This fly is native to North America. It's larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feed on already dead tissue.

It actually has some uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. Visit Wikipedia for a start, and follow the links. Interesting stuff!
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cochliomyia
Locality:
Cochliomyia macellaria). See the images at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/36836/bgimage

If I'm right
CommonNames: Blow Fly; Common Blow Fly; Blue Bottle Fly; Wild Blackberry; Secondary Screwworm
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae

File.Name: c516b_c514-025.jpg
Notes: 7213220692 I guess this is a small Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera. Looking around at BugGuide, I think it might be genus Thevenetimyia in the subfamily Ecliminae, but I really don't know. One distinctive trait is that it always bobbed up and down a few times before landing. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2012)

File.Name: c516c_c513-008.jpg
Notes: 7213133498 This is an odd dusty-looking fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of (I think) native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's larger than a house fly. I have no idea what kind of fly it is, so if you recognize it, please leave a comment! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2012)

File.Name: c516d_c512-045.jpg
Notes: 7213023042 This is a remarkable beetle (Coleoptera) larvae with a iridescent green sheen, like metal. Compare Aliceinwl1's photo of a Trirhabda flavolimbata larva in the Chrysomelidae family. It sure looks like the same critter, though her larva is munching Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) and mine is munching Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It's my impression from BugGuide that the host plant matters with this genus, so maybe it's a different species? It's doing some real damage to the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)
CommonNames: beetle; Coyote Bush; Trask's Yerba Santa
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Trirhabda flavolimbata; Chrysomelidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Trirhabda flavolimbata; Chrysomelidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: c516e_c511-038.jpg
Notes:
7212899658 Here is another late-season amanita mushroom - the Springtime Amanita (Amanita velosa). It has a tan cap - "the color of a brown hen's egg" - with a white patch of volval tissue on top, and white gills that turn pink in age, and a large sack-like volva that sheathes the base of the stalk. It's a beautiful mushroom that comes out at a time when few other mushrooms are out. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 May 2012)

This specimen is not fresh - it smells like a diaper pail! But fresher specimens are said for be sweet and delicious - IF you dare to eat any amanita.

File.Name: c516f_c510-026.jpg
Notes:
7212864890 This is another photo of "The Blusher" - a well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods a few days ago. It develops reddish colors on its cap and stem as it ages, though not always as clear as this It's a late-season mushroom that I've been waiting to see in this dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2012)

See my [Older] photo of the whole mushroom out of the ground. This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. No thanks!

File.Name: c516g_c510-027.jpg
Notes:
7212784082 Here is a photo of the whole mushroom that I show in situ in my [Newer] photo. This is "The Blusher" - a well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods a few days ago. It's an interesting amanita that has a very fragile ring that may disappear, and basically no volva at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2012)

It gets its name from the reddish colors it develops in age. This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. No thanks!

File.Name: c516h_c510-020.jpg
Notes:
7212711132 This sure look like a worm standing up and wiggling in its burrow!

In fact, it's a "mushroom" - at least it's the spore-bearing fruiting body of a fungus. This one is growing from a crack in the sandstone along our road, where there is a seep to provide moisture. Maybe it is Fairy Fingers (Clavaria vermicularis) in the Clavariaceae? (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2012)

For more info, see my photo from May 4 at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6997543208

File.Name: c517a_c517-020.jpg
Notes:
7219175168 This is a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) ovipositing on a flower of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. But it's really watching that "Lupine Aphid" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera on the left! If you know what to look for, you can clearly see the fly's "spurious vein". (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

The flies lay their eggs, which hatch into larvae that consume the aphids. These are good flies to have around!

Today started breezy, and got downright windy this afternoon, with gusts to 40 mph. Fire weather! There was the threat of a real "sundowner wind" event, but I don't think it happened.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Canyon Lupine; Lupine Aphid
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: c517b_c517-035.jpg
Notes:
7219116836 Here is a perfect Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly in the woods today. It has chocolate-brown wings instead of the darker, almost black wings of the Mournful Duskywing (E. tristis) butterfly, which is our other black skipper with a white trailing edge on its hindwing. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

This one is perched on the dead stem of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family - chasing away all other butterflies.
CommonNames: Funereal Duskywing; Mournful Duskywing; California Buckwheat

File.Name: c517c_c517-008.jpg
Notes:
7219038582 This is another photo of a fanciful caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

It's probably the same caterpillar on the same leaf that I showed a couple of days ago. If you compare images, you can see that its done some damage to the leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family.
ScientificName: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: c517d_c517-026.jpg
Notes:
7218993988 This is another impossibly thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

That lens flare behind the antenna was an accident, but its perfect.

File.Name: c517e_c517-037.jpg
Notes:
7218947740 Alice may well be right that this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. The photos at BugGuide are a good match. I guess I have to rethink all the spiders I've placed in Diguetidae. Thanks Alice!

Original comment:
"I believe this is a Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae hiding in its nest. They decorate their inverted-thimble nests with the husks of their prey, like pirates! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers; Coneweb Spider; Desertshrub Spider

File.Name: c517f_c516-004.jpg
Notes:
7218871572 Here are more new flowers of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's kind of an upside-down Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

*Sigh* As fast as these elegant new flowers come out, our family of deer come along and very selectively munch them off. They've also been munching the garden. Julie has a couple of motion-sensor sprinklers set up, and they sure startle the hell out of me in the morning when I forget about them!
ScientificName: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Mariposa Lily; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Mariposa Lily; Liliaceae

File.Name: c517g_c517-007.jpg
Notes:
7218820362 This is another beautiful photo of "The Blusher" - a different well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods today. It develops reddish colors on its cap and stem as it ages, though not always as clear as this. It's a late-season mushroom that I've been waiting to see in this dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. I'm more interested in how these mushrooms on the opposite sides of our canyon know to emerge at the same time. I have the same question with flowering plants like Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). How do they know when to flower? "Environmental conditions" seems too generic for an answer.

File.Name: c517h_c517-031.jpg
Notes:
7218742098 These are more "Wood Chipper Mushrooms" (my name) growing on wood chips from a neighbor's clearing project along the road. I don't know what they are, but I suspect they come with the wood chipper. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

These mushrooms have brown spores and a partial veil that leaves an evanescent ring. I have no idea what they are or if they are edible. Maybe some Agrocybe other than A. praecox? If you recognize them, please share!

File.Name: c518a_c518-014.jpg
Notes:
7224625566 This is a different tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) with a red-tipped abdomen. It's working a new flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2012)

It was windy again today. I was lucky to get any photo, though the flower was close to the ground where the wind wasn't so bad.

The wind has been dying down this evening as I type. I hope it will be better tomorrow.

File.Name: c518b_c518-013.jpg
Notes:
7224540800 Here is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2012)

It was windy today. I was lucky to get this photo, though the flower was close to the ground where the wind wasn't so bad.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c518c_c518-010.jpg
Notes:
7224502088 These are ripe fruit of Galium nuttallii in the Rubiacae plant family. The leaves are whorled, mostly in 4's. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2012)

I have a photo of the flowers at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6974606919/

Galium plants are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds.

File.Name: c518d_c518-006.jpg
Notes:
7224436198 These are native woodland grasses in the garden. Julie's been doing good work with these grasses, and they're doing well. We have a native grassland!

The thin spikes are Western Rye (Elymus glaucus) and the nodding grasses are California Brome (Bromus carinatus). Both grasses are in the Poacea plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2012)

They are beautiful and NOT stickery!

File.Name: c518e_c518-005.jpg
Notes:
7224365850 This is a "Live Oak Witches' Broom Fungus" infection of Powdery Mildew, very likely Cystotheca lanestris, on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2012)

It's an Ascomycete fungal infection. This powdery mildew seems to attack this same small tree every year. It doesn't spread, and it doesn't seem to harm the tree, except for the galled branches, which will ultimately thin and die.

For more info about this interesting Powdery Mildew, see "A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks" (page 82ff.) which is available as a PDF from:
   http://www.fs.fed.us/psw/publications/documents/psw_gtr197/psw_gtr197.pdf
CommonNames: Live Oak Witches' Broom Fungus; Powdery Mildew; Coast Live Oak; Powdery Mildew

File.Name: c518f_c517-017.jpg
Notes:
7224260538 This is interesting. This is a new flower head of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's infested with tiny aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

The aphids will ultimately gall the flowers so that they open late and deformed, and stay on the stem way longer than they should. The aphids take them over and change them. They must be specialized aphids, but who can tell just by looking at them?

I reckon this is a plant gall in the works. Give them a few thousand generations, and the aphids might produce a real structure. I'll keep posting photos as they develop.
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera; gall
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera; gall

File.Name: c518g_c513-050.jpg
Notes:
7224182862 This is native Whispering Bells (Emmenanthe penduliflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. It's a fire-follower growing in a place that burned a few years ago during the Gap Fire which threatened homes on San Marcos Pass in the summer of 2008. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2012)

Did you notice the long-horned Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) visiting the flower in the upper right?

Fire-follower plants - at least the herbs - have a way of flowering like crazy for a few years, and then disappearing to wait in the seed bank until the next wildfire. I only found this one plant that forgot to wait.
ScientificName: Emmenanthe penduliflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Emmenanthe penduliflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c518h_c517-018.jpg
Notes:
7224153508 Here is another photo of the little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) ovipositing on a flower of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - while watching the "Lupine Aphid" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera on the other flower! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2012)

These aphids are quite large. The flies lay their eggs, which hatch into larvae that consume the aphids. These are good flies to have around!
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Canyon Lupine; Lupine Aphid
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: c519a_c519-021.jpg
Notes:
7230728984 Here is a beautiful new Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly. It's perched on a leaf of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family - but it was really interested in the Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family nearby. (I had to chase it from plant-to-plant for a while!) (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)

This is a female, with those brown upper wings. The males really are blue above.

Today was a beautiful day, though still a bit breezy. I have hopes for tomorrow to be a perfect photo day!
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c519b_c519-010.jpg
Notes: 7230702758 This is a perfect little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)

File.Name: c519c_c519-016.jpg
Notes: 7230627028 This is a perfect little Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. These flies are predators. They have amazing iridescent metallic colors! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)

File.Name: c519d_c519-014.jpg
Notes: 7230593724 Here is the tiny nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. It's only about 1/4 inch long! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)

File.Name: c519e_c519-032.jpg
Notes: 7230462916 This is a small immature Katydid in the family Tettigoniidae of the suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera. It appears to be interested in the throat of this native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family by the creek today. Beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: c519f_c519-020.jpg
Notes:
7230372668 "Mother of nations"

This herd of "Lupine Aphids" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera is on a stem of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. They seem to be following the large aphid "fundatrice" on the right - maybe the mama for this whole aphid colony! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)

I have to admit that I really enjoy sitting by a colony of aphids with my camera. There is always something happening.

File.Name: c519g_c519-006.jpg
Notes:
7230293242 Is that egg on your face?
No, it's mushed up aphids!

This is the looking close at the larva of a Lady Beetle in the (Coccinellidae) family of the Coleoptera. It's been munching the "Lupine Aphids" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera on a stem of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Bon appÈtit! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; Lupine Aphids; Canyon Lupine
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c519h_c519-018.jpg
Notes: 7230247506 I was trying to photograph little flies on the leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today, when this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) suddenly appeared. There was no time to adjust exposure or anything. Here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)

File.Name: c520a_c520-002.jpg
Notes:
7238523224 This is a native "Timema" insect that I found inside the bathroom window screen this morning. It's a chunky member of the Walkingstick clan in the family Timematidae, of the suborder Timematodea, of the unusual (for us) order Phasmatodea. It's about an inch long. I caught it in a glass and took it outside to get a more "natural" photo, but it squirmed away from me. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2012)

I don't find them very often, so they are always interesting. I really don't know how common they are in our woods. I rarely find them, though they are hard to spot in the field.

Eclipse night! I didn't try to get photos of the actual eclipse, but the shadows through the oak trees were very cool. I'll have a photo tomorrow.

File.Name: c520b_c520-015.jpg
Notes:
7238373724 This is solitary Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (genus Eumeninae, family Vespidea, order Hymenoptera). It's got one pot done, and its working on the other - on the radio antenna of my truck! Well, I decided I didn't really have to go anywhere today, and hopefully it will be done tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2012)

Wikipedia says that "the adult wasp typically collects beetle larvae, spiders or caterpillars and, paralyzing them, places them in the cell to serve as food for a single wasp larva. As a normal rule, the adult wasp lays a single egg in the empty cell before provisioning it."

I'm impressed that this photo came out as good as it is. I was sitting on my truck hood, and the antenna wouldn't stop wiggling back and forth. Fortunately, it was sunny, and I bumped up my ISO a notch.

File.Name: c520c_c520-008.jpg
Notes: 7238327424 These are winged "alate" Termites (order Isoptera) preparing to take off into the unknown. Most of them will end up in the mouths of birds and lizards! We had a hatch this morning from a small crack in our driveway - not from the foundation of our house! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2012)

File.Name: c520d_c520-013.jpg
Notes:
7238104588 This is another fantastic little Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2012)

They have iridescent metallic colors that seem to change from moment to moment, like a male hummingbird's head.

File.Name: c520e_c519-009.jpg
Notes:
7238015600 This is the larva of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) approaching it's food - a "Lupine Aphid" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2012)

Whenever you're at a loss for a photo, try sitting by an aphid-infested plant, and just wait for some action!

File.Name: c520f_c520-023.jpg
Notes:
7237870844 These are new flowers of California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae) plant family. It's not common in our mountains, but I find it here and there - and it has an interesting common name. I've never tried to make tea from this plant, but it does have a fragrance. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2012)

The gland-spotted flowers remind me of Turpentine Broom (Thamnosma montana) in the desert. I have an old photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9762769

This is one of those native plants that could probably disappear from the woods without a trace and make no difference - though it does add some diversity to the woods, and it is diversity that keeps the woods interesting year after year!
Locality:
Thamnosma montana) in the desert. I have an old photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9762769

This is one of those native plants that could probably disappear from the woods without a trace and make no difference - though it does add some diversity to the woods
