File.Name: c520g_c520-031.jpg
Notes:
7237787010 This is an interesting Clarkia (genus Clarkia) in the Onagraceae plant family growing on the dry hillside behind our home. I suspect it is the native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides), though these flowers are speckled with violet unlike the pure white flowers I usually find. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2012)

Compare this photo. This flower almost looks like a hybrid with Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica) in the Onagraceae plant family.
ScientificName: Clarkia; Onagraceae; Clarkia epilobioides; Clarkia cylindrica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Clarkia; Onagraceae; Clarkia epilobioides; Clarkia cylindrica; Onagraceae

File.Name: c520h_c519-029.jpg
Notes:
7237616420 I found this impressive late-season Amanita mushroom in the woods yesterday, and I'm confused. I really think it might be the Pine Cone Amanita(Amanita magniverrucata aka A. strobiliformis), though there is no sign of the exaggerated warts on the cap which usually identify this species.

I've found (A. magniverrucata) in the same place before, at the same time of year. When I went to pick this one, I inadvertently broke the stem - so it has a long tapered stem below what you can see here. That's another sign of (A. magniverrucata). (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2012)

I suppose A. baccata is another possibility, though it doesn't fruit in the spring. If you can help me with this, please leave a comment.
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita magniverrucata; A. strobiliformis; A. magniverrucata; A. magniverrucata; A. baccata
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita magniverrucata; A. strobiliformis; A. magniverrucata; A. magniverrucata; A. baccata

File.Name: c521a_c521-069.jpg
Notes:
7246637140 Here is the gorgeous nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. The tiny bejeweled nymphs have no green, but the large dull adults are all green. The nymphs remind me of a little pony decked out with jeweled finery. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)

Today was a beautiful day - until the wind picked up in the afternoon. We are apparently in for a "wind event" with prolonged gale force winds for the next few days. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: c521b_c521-075.jpg
Notes:
7246605594 This is a beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower head of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)

It seems to have a bit of spider web stuck to its wing.

File.Name: c521c_c521-043.jpg
Notes:
7246550612 This is a fantastic Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working new flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. Look at the pollen on its proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)

This is the first of these diurnal (daytime) moths that I've seen this year. It's a deep 1:1 crop. I hope there are many more to photograph better this year!
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Trask's Yerba Santa

File.Name: c521d_c521-036.jpg
Notes: 7246480240 Here is another beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched on a flower of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's not a food or host plant. This butterfly was looking to mate, and chasing away any other butterflies, regardless of species. That makes it hard to get a photo! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)

File.Name: c521e_c521-047.jpg
Notes:
7246382148 I suspect this beautiful iridescent creature is a Sweat Bee (family Halictidae, Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera), but I'm not sure. It's perched on a leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family that is starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)

We have Metallic Leafcutter Bees, Metallic Sweat Bees, and Cuckoo Wasps that all have metallic green colors. It's confusing!
CommonNames: Sweat Bee; Trask's Yerba Santa; Metallic Leafcutter Bees; Metallic Sweat Bees; Cuckoo Wasps
ScientificName: Halictidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Halictidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: c521f_c521-070.jpg
Notes: 7246281188 This critter is a rather large - at least an inch long. It's a Cricket in the family Tettigoniidae of the suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera. I found it hiding in the shade under a leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californicain the Rhamnaceae plant family. I tried to move it into the sun for a better photo, and it fell away. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: c521g_c521-012.jpg
Notes:
7246139894 This is interesting... These are new green fruit of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in the mountains.

But look how the cherries are all different sizes, and there are still new flowers. I've seen this in a few other natives like Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It quit flowering early in this dry year, but then started flowering again after the late spring rains. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)

I guess it take a very dry year to learn these new tricks that the native shrubs have.
ScientificName: Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: c521h_c520-032.jpg
Notes: 7245944084 I left it to others to get real photos of the solar eclipse last night, but I did get a photo of the very cool "eclipse shadows" through the oak leaves on our west-facing wall as we prepared our BBQ. The gaps between the leaves act like little pinhole cameras and make images of the sun. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2012)

File.Name: c522a_c522-004.jpg
Notes:
7252954374 Do grasses experience "Empty Glume Syndrome" when almost all the seeds have left home?

This is a native bunch grass, I guess the Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua) in the in the Poacea plant family. There are a couple of seeds left on the stems, and they have characteristic bent awns that are also twisted. It was almost hidden by the annual weedy grasses. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 May 2012)

The wind came up as advertised today, but it gave me some time in the morning before it got too bad. I hope it does the same tomorrow!

File.Name: c522b_c522-007.jpg
Notes:
7252891184 This is a beautiful new flower of Elegant Clarkia (Clarkia unguiculata) in the Onagraceae plant family. The petals look like they've been cut with scissors. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 May 2012)

This is one of our "Farewell-to-Spring" flowers. I hate to see Spring leave, with just 54% normal rainfall so far. The woods are drying out fast, and we can't expect any more rain for 6 long, hot months. It will be an interesting summer...

File.Name: c522c_c522-023.jpg
Notes:
7252795884 Have you ever been crawling under chaparral, when it suddenly felt like your hands were on fire? This native plant is probably the reason!

This is a small native Western Nettle (Hesperocnidae tenella) in the Urticacea

File.Name: c522d_c522-018.jpg
Notes:
7252691900 I'm not sure what kind of gall this is on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It's like a blister on the leaf. You can see a hole where something got out - or did something get in? (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 May 2012)

If you look close, you can also see a couple tiny pistillate acorn "flowers" above the gall to the right.

File.Name: c522e_c522-009.jpg
Notes: 7252634882 These are tiny flowers of the Phlox-leaved Bedstraw (Galium andrewii) in the Rubiacae plant family. The 4-merous flowers are really tiny, a mm across or less. It's one of my personal favorite native plants, as is the whole genus. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 May 2012)

File.Name: c522f_c522-011.jpg
Notes:
7252546292 These are new flowers of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) in the Convulvulaceae plant family. It has bracts below the calyx of the flower rather than overlapping the calyx. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 May 2012)

Did you notice the tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) flying into the lower flower?
ScientificName: Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Convulvulaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Convulvulaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c522g_c521-064.jpg
Notes: 7252499046 I believe this is the fantastic dandelion-like seedhead of Silver Puffs (Uropappus lindleyi) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains. The flowers aren't much, but the seed heads are wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)

File.Name: c522h_c521-045.jpg
Notes:
7252369736 Spittle Bugs aka Froghoppers (family Aphrophoridae of the order Homoptera) have made these frothy masses to protect their larvae. These are on a flowering stem of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. Other tiny insects get stuck in the slime. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2012)

It looks like someone hocked a big loogie on the stem - but this is all natural.
CommonNames: Spittle Bugs; Froghoppers; Trask's Yerba Santa

File.Name: c523a_c523-005.jpg
Notes:
7259697612 This is a beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working flowers of a Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2012)

This butterfly is a bit yellow, like the Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus) - but those are usually very yellow.

It's been hot, dry, and very windy today. We're under a "Red Flag Warning" for wildfire danger. The cars on the highway are like rolling fire-bombs...
Locality:
P. rutulus) - but those are usually very yellow.

It's been hot
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Geranium; Western Tiger Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Pelargonium; Geraniaceae; P. rutulus; very
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Pelargonium; Geraniaceae; P. rutulus; very

File.Name: c523b_c523-009.jpg
Notes:
7259664364 This is a beautiful Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family in flower on the pass. I found many of these on one hillside along StageCoach Road today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2012)

It was very windy today, and it took quite a few tries to get this one keeper photo.

File.Name: c523c_c523-012.jpg
Notes: 7259607328 This is looking down on a native violet Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family - with a Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working the flower. It's probably a Leafcutter Bee (family Megachilidae) because it's collecting pollen on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2012)
CommonNames: Cobweb Thistle; Wild Bee; Leafcutter Bee
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Megachilidae

File.Name: c523d_c523-011.jpg
Notes:
7259517784 These are beautiful flower heads of a native violet Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. It took quite a few photos to get this one keeper in the wind today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2012)

(I'll pretend the varieties don't exist - I'm always confused by these!)

File.Name: c523e_c523-015.jpg
Notes:
7259435212 Look in the upper-right of this photo. There is the nymph of a brown Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera). This one is not a katydid. It's perched on a flower head of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2012)

It was very windy when I got this photo today. I waited for relatively calm moments, and took 10-30 photos to get this one keeper. I'm thankful for digital - I couldn't afford to do this with film!

File.Name: c523f_c523-021.jpg
Notes:
7259399778 These are the waxy flowers of Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family, just starting to open today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2012)

It was very windy today, but these thick flowers don't move too much.

File.Name: c523g_c523-004.jpg
Notes:
7259312016 This is the pot of a solitary Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (genus Eumeninae, family Vespidea, order Hymenoptera). It made two of these well-crafted mud nests on the radio antenna of my truck! That's a red Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2012)

What to do? Well, I took the radio antenna off my truck and planted it in the garden nearby. I can live without radio for awhile. See my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7238373724/

File.Name: c523h_c512-073.jpg
Notes:
7259232556 This is a non-native grass in the Poaceae plant family showing its "goodies" - fluffy white pistils and yellow stamens. I first thought this was Rye Grass (Secale cereale), but after looking at CalFlora photos, I'm not sure. I am sure that it was seeded by the Forest Service in this place for erosion control, darn it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2012)

I've been saving this photo for a windy day - here it is.

File.Name: c524a_c524-007.jpg
Notes:
7265949488 Here is another beautiful nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. The tiny bejeweled nymphs have no green at all, but the large dull adults are all green. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2012)

The Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society hosted a lecture tonight by Dr. Gordon Frankie from UC Berkeley on California native bees. It was a very interesting lecture, but I got home late and I'm tired. Two photos will do just fine tonight.

File.Name: c524b_c524-003.jpg
Notes:
7265897454 These are pretty new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. In the back, you can see some of the uppermost pair of leaves which are fused around the stem, like an umbrella. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2012)

These aren't the first flowers I've seen this year, but they're the first I can reach with my tripod. Often they flower high in the trees and shrubs.

File.Name: c525a_c525-012.jpg
Notes:
7271080574 These are new flowers of our native Stream Orchid (Epipactis gigantea) in the Orchidaceae plant family. I found it growing near the Santa Ynez River this morning. Note how the inferior ovary is twisted to present the flower upside down - a trait of all orchids. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2012)

These flowers are quite uncommon, at least north of the Santa Ynez Mountains. I only know them from a couple of places. They are more common along the streams that go to the coast.

The weather changed last night from hot to cold. I expected the wind would change too, but if anything it got worse with 45+ mph gusts according to a neighbor's weather station - with a 70 mph gust! The power has been flickering. It's been a windy week.

I did what I could for photos, but nothing today is quite sharp. And I took 20-30 photos for each of these keepers!

File.Name: c525b_c525-047.jpg
Notes:
7271000532 These are new flowers of False Indigo (Amorpha californica) in the (Fabaceae) plant family. It's an uncommon shrub in our mountains that's growing on a sandy flat along the river. It has long odd-pinnate leaves with many leaflets. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2012)

Many sources repeat that this is the only food plant of the California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly, but I'm sure that's wrong. This shrub is uncommon, and the butterflies are quite common. They must have another host plant.

File.Name: c525c_c525-030.jpg
Notes:
7270962414 These are beautiful native flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing along the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2012)

You can hardly tell that it very windy when I took this photo - but it was, and it took many photos to get this one keeper.

File.Name: c525d_c525-041.jpg
Notes: 7270912370 Here is a mass of flowering Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family flowering near the river today. I tried to get a closer photo, but the wind didn't agree. This will do! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2012)

File.Name: c525e_c525-043.jpg
Notes:
7270872618 Doesn't it refresh you just to look at this sweet-smelling Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2012)

I appreciate that "yerba buena" means any local plant that smells like peppermint, but in California this is THE yerba buena, the original namesake of the city of San Francisco. It has a spicy taste and makes great peppermint tea when dried. It makes a nice ground cover in our garden.

This native plant is not common, but there are colonies along the river and in our canyon at home.

File.Name: c525f_c525-017.jpg
Notes:
7270829380 This is a new flower of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family growing in a sea of non-native Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) in the Poaceae plant family down by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2012)

Actually, the Toloache is non-native too - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!
ScientificName: Datura wrightii; Solanaceae; Bromus diandrus; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Datura wrightii; Solanaceae; Bromus diandrus; Poaceae

File.Name: c525g_c525-036.jpg
Notes:
7270709864 Fall colors in (late) spring!

The leaves of this native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family are already turning red and starting to shut down. I'm not surprised - this has been a very dry year with just 53% normal rainfall so far. Those plants that can would do well to shut down early. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2012)

File.Name: c525h_c525-015.jpg
Notes:
7270611870 Julie jokes that I can find mushrooms anywhere - she might be right!

This is a thoroughly dried out black-spored mushroom growing on the sandy flood plain of the Santa Ynez River. I think it might be the Gastroid Coprinus (Montagnea arenarius), but I'm not sure. If so, there is a small volva at the base of the the stem. I didn't dig this one up. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2012)

File.Name: c526a_c526-029.jpg
Notes:
7276866800 This perfect female Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly is ovipositing on a leaf of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. You can see her squeezing out an egg! See my [Older] photo for a look at her upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2012)

It was still windy all day today, but not as bad as yesterday. I have hopes for tomorrow.

File.Name: c526b_c526-038.jpg
Notes: 7276838268 These are the upper wings of a female Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly in the canyon today. You can see that she really does have some blue color! See my [Newer] photo for a view of the lower wings. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2012)

File.Name: c526c_c526-018.jpg
Notes:
7276676322 This is a handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly - the first I've managed to photograph this year. It's resting on the chip seal road in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2012)

I figure that half a butterfly, sharp - is better than a whole butterfly, not quite sharp!

File.Name: c526d_c526-020.jpg
Notes: 7276613770 I found this unfortunate Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly caught in a spider web today. I flipped it over for a photo of its underside. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2012)

File.Name: c526e_c526-035.jpg
Notes: 7276573144 This is a small Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens, cp. P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae. It has prey in its chelicerae which looks like a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). It's perched on a flower calyx of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2012)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Syrphid Flower Fly; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Syrphidae; Diptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Syrphidae; Diptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: c526f_c526-012.jpg
Notes: 7276549596 Here is yet another beautiful nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. The tiny bejeweled nymphs have no green at all, but the large dull adults are all green. You can probably tell that I really like these little insects! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2012)

File.Name: c526g_c526-004.jpg
Notes: 7276492282 Here are mating Tephritid Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) on a flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden this morning. They have such beautiful eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2012)

File.Name: c526h_c513-031.jpg
Notes: 7276459284 This is a new flower stalk of our other native orchid - the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. I agree that it looks more like an asparagus than an orchid, but just wait. It will be a few more weeks before there are flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2012)

File.Name: c527a_c527-016.jpg
Notes:
7284226698 I was excited to find this flower cluster of the uncommon Chaparral Broomrape (Orobanche bulbosa) in the Orobanchacea plant family on my hike today. Flowers are all there are to this plant, at least all you can see. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2012)

This is a parasitic plant without chlorophyll that grows from the roots of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. There's a shrub a foot or two away on the right. Maybe there is a fungal connection between the plants? It has a thick root and a bulbous, scaly stem base - like a real plant that grows underground without the sun. The flowers look like they belong in the Scrophulariaceae plant family.

I won't show the exact location of this rare plant, but I'll show you where to start looking!

It was still breezy today, but nothing like it's been this windy week. I hope we're back to normal now.
Locality: Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. There's a shrub a foot or two away on the right. Maybe there is a fungal connection between the plants? It has a thick root and a bulbous
ScientificName: Orobanche bulbosa; Orobanchacea; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Orobanche bulbosa; Orobanchacea; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: c527b_c527-024.jpg
Notes:
7284111140 The things we do to get a photo!

This is a biting Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera) on the index finger of my left hand. (I took the photo with my right hand, stretching to get focus.)

Look - it's drawn blood! I believe this is a "Rocky Mountain Bite Fly" (or kin) in the genus Symphoromyia. They have a painful bite. This one's life is just about over. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2012)

These dark flies are bad biters, but they are easy to swat. The orange biting snipe flies are much worse because they are faster and harder to swat. I haven't seen them yet this year... but I'm sure I will.
CommonNames: Snipe Fly; Rocky Mountain Bite Fly

File.Name: c527c_c527-040.jpg
Notes:
7284059990 Here is a different tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in our mountains. It is very dark, with pure white contrasting pedipalps. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2012)

This is an 800x800 crop from my original 4288x2848 pixel original photo taken at 1:1 magnification with my 105 mm macro lens. I need extension tubes to get closer!

File.Name: c527d_c527-046.jpg
Notes:
7283955852 This is a gorgeous Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly working a flower head of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family today. It was some ways from the willows and cottonwoods in the creek below that it usually favors. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2012)

These butterflies look a lot like the California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border.
CommonNames: Lorquin's Admiral; Trask's Yerba Santa; California Sister
ScientificName: Basilarchia lorquini; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Adelpha bredowii; californica
associatedTaxa: Basilarchia lorquini; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Adelpha bredowii; californica

File.Name: c527e_c527-009.jpg
Notes:
7283812308 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly curled around a flower head of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It appears to be feeding with its proboscis out, but its a sham - its really looking out for other butterflies to chase away, or hopefully arrange a "hook up". (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2012)

I was up on the ridge above the headwaters of Paradise Creek today looking for hill-topping butterflies. These butterflies seemed to be having turf wars with the Pale Swallowtails, chasing each other away in a circle game! (See my [Older] photo.)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Black Mustard; Pale Swallowtails

File.Name: c527f_c527-010.jpg
Notes:
7283744402 This is another beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly perched on a stem of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It wasn't interested in the flowers, but in finding a mate. When this butterfly found swallowtail, they took off straight into the air, like hummingbirds. It's thrilling to watch. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2012)

I was up on the ridge above the headwaters of Paradise Creek today looking for hill-topping butterflies. These butterflies seemed to be having turf wars with the Variable Checkerspot butterflies, chasing each other away in a circle game! (See my [Newer] photo.) I also saw Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterflies. I'm still waiting for a photo of them.
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Deerweed; Variable Checkerspot; Gabb's Checkerspot
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Chlosyne gabbii
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Chlosyne gabbii

File.Name: c527g_c524-014.jpg
Notes:
7283697622 This is a another beautiful Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek. It was perched on a high leaf to chase all the other butterflies away from its territory. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2012)

I took this photo a few days ago, but its too good to lose in the archives!

File.Name: c527h_c527-027.jpg
Notes:
7283603460 Moths are Lepidoptera just like butterflies, but they get no respect. *Sigh* I might need another lifetime to understand them.

This little moth is diurnal - out in the daytime. I saw it land on this leaf and got the photo, then it flew away. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2012)

File.Name: c528a_c528-022.jpg
Notes:
7291810428 These are delicate new flowers of Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family - getting taken over by the serpent-like tendrils of Dodder - probably California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae plant family. I really like this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2012)

It was warmer today, and not too breezy. We're close to summer, and it felt like it today!
ScientificName: Erigeron foliosus; Asteraceae; Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae
associatedTaxa: Erigeron foliosus; Asteraceae; Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae

File.Name: c528b_c528-003.jpg
Notes:
7291655940 This is like an anatomical drawing from your Botany 101 class...

Julie sliced this immature flower of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden down the middle this morning to send the other half to a neighbor who is an authority on cacti.

You can see the developing flower petals and stamens and pistil, and the inferior ovary full of ovules. If you look close, you can see a little critter inside the ovary! See my [Older] photo for the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2012)

File.Name: c528c_c528-036.jpg
Notes:
7291548912 This is the gorgeous new flower of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden. Julie thinks she got it from a friend who got it in Baja many years ago. It seems quite happy here. This is the first flower we've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2012)

A neighbor is an authority on local cacti, and he thinks this may be a hybrid. Julie sliced a cactus bud in half today so he could have a look inside. See my [Newer] photo!

File.Name: c528d_c528-037.jpg
Notes: 7291498352 These are the new flowers of Chalk Lettuce (Dudleya pulverulenta) in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family in my [Older] photo. This one is happily growing in our garden, but it's native across the valley on Figueroa Mountain. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2012)

File.Name: c528e_c528-008.jpg
Notes:
7291371146 This is a wonderful native Chalk Lettuce (Dudleya pulverulenta) in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family. It's growing in our garden, raised (as I recall) from seed by Ron and Diane Griffin at Manzanita Nursery in Solvang. That flower stalk is 2 or 3 feet long! See my [Newer] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2012)

The plants are native, but on the south-facing slopes across the valley from us. It's time for another trip to Figueroa Mountain!

File.Name: c528f_c528-016.jpg
Notes:
7291251142 These are new flowers of Hemp Dogbane aka Indian Hemp (Apocynum cannabinum) in the Apocynaceae plant family. That's hemp as in fiber. It's an uncommon plant in our mountains - I only know it from two places. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2012)

This was one of the most important native plants for the native American Chumash in our mountains - for string, rope, nets, and clothing. For more info, see Jan Timbrook's Chumash Ethnobotany (2007).

File.Name: c528g_c528-024.jpg
Notes:
7291171336 These are more native flowers of the Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. The large flowers are about an inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2012)

This is another fire-follower plant that flowers in great profusion for a few years after a wildfire, and then disappears into the seedbank to wait for the next wildfire. I found a few more in a place that was cleared (not burned) last year. Sometimes clearing the ground is enough to bring these plants out.

File.Name: c528h_c528-001.jpg
Notes: 7291075106 I found this well-camouflaged little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) on the edge of the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2012)

File.Name: c529a_c529-047.jpg
Notes:
7299464574 I found this impressive large Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) by the Santa Ynez River today. It's perched on a new flower cluster of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family, but it's not interested in the flowers, rather the pollinators! It's at least an inch long. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2012)

These large robber flies look a bit like dragonflies, and the hunt like them too - capturing prey in the air between their stiff-haired front legs. They also have a hollow between their eyes. It's an example of convergent evolution.

It was a beautiful day today - a bit breezy, but manageable.

File.Name: c529b_c529-070.jpg
Notes:
7299357932 This is a male Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera in the air over our porch this afternoon. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2012)

This male (with light-colored hairs) has been hovering here for days - chasing females and fighting other males. Mostly it's been fixated by our shiny rain gauge, but my camera lens also seems to also work to get its attention.

Sure, this is a 1:1 crop from my original 4288x2848 pixel original photo. It helps to back up a bit from your monitor.
Locality: with light-colored hairs) has been hovering here for days - chasing females and fighting other males.  Mostly it's been fixated by our shiny rain gauge

File.Name: c529c_c529-025.jpg
Notes: 7299305104 This is a different little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) with its nest in a Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family shrub. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2012)

File.Name: c529d_c529-040.jpg
Notes: 7299245426 This is a magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family on a dry flat by the river. The new flowers make perfect mandalas as they unwind. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2012)

File.Name: c529e_c529-010.jpg
Notes:
7299180520 Black sage, Purple Sage, White Sage - what's next?

I believe this is a hybrid between White Sage (Salvia apiana) and Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla), both in the Labiatae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2012)

It has leaves like white sage, with long wand-like flower stems and those interesting flowers in ball-like clusters. The hybrids are actually fairly common, once you notice them.

File.Name: c529f_c529-002.jpg
Notes:
7299040096 These are new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2012)

The flowers do an interesting dance as they mature. At first, they stand straight up like the flower on the left, and the pistil stays off to the side. It takes a large bee like a Carpenter Bee to unlock these flowers. Then they lay open like the flower on the right and the pistil moves in to the center. It's a fascinating process to watch.

File.Name: c529g_c529-011.jpg
Notes:
7298980194 These are new flowers of Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla) in the Labiatae plant family growing along the Santa Ynez River today. I don't believe this shrub grows near our home in the mountains. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2012)

This is NOT the purple sage as in "New Riders of the Purple Sage" - that's sagebrush, totally different.

File.Name: c529h_c529-015.jpg
Notes:
7298862624 The incoming Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) in the upper-right is flying towards flower heads of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Labiatae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2012)

I wanted a photo of Black Sage to complete a photo series today, but I almost waited too long. The flowers are just about done for the year.

File.Name: c530a_c530-016.jpg
Notes:
7305809914 Here is another fantastic little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working a flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. Look close at the other flowers to see little insects inside! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 May 2012)

If you compare my [Older] photo, you can see that this little bee fly is a lot browner than the other little bee fly. Maybe it's a gender difference?

I drove up East Camino Cielo road into the high mountains today - mostly looking for new butterflies. I found surprisingly few butterflies and other insects. Maybe they all got blown out to sea last week?

File.Name: c530b_c530-020.jpg
Notes:
7305677044 This is a fantastic little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera about to insert its long proboscis into a flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. I didn't realize these flowers were so deep! You can see a bit of pollen on its proboscis. Thus pollination happens. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 May 2012)

This photo is not quite in focus, but its still very cool. See my [Newer] photo.

File.Name: c530c_c530-056.jpg
Notes: 7305641282 Here is a large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera hanging on the cedar wall of our bathroom. What an amazing face! These large flies always remind me of wise old philosophers contemplating the infinite. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2012)

File.Name: c530d_c530-038.jpg
Notes:
7305602332 Here is a rather large Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) thorough engaged in a new flower of native Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing in the mountains. I waited several minutes for it to emerge, but it didn't. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 May 2012)

You can see the upper stamens patting its back. Thus, pollination happens!
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Penstemon heterophyllus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Penstemon heterophyllus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: c530e_c530-035.jpg
Notes:
7305513746 These are new flowers of native Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing in the mountains. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 May 2012)

This plant is called a "pen-stemon" for a reason - there is something strange about its fifth stamen. You can see the interesting four normal stamens, and the mysterious namesake "fifth stamen" laying along the lower petal like a Rolling Stones tongue. It's a non-fertile "staminode". The receptive pistil is visible on top of the flower.

File.Name: c530f_c530-041.jpg
Notes: 7305477366 This is a mass of new flowers of native Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. This plant is not common in our mountains, but I find it in this one place along East Camino Cielo road every summer. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 May 2012)

File.Name: c530g_c530-026.jpg
Notes: 7305398406 This is the beautiful native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are summer flowers - this is the first one I've seen this year. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 May 2012)

File.Name: c530h_c530-042.jpg
Notes: 7305360354 These are odd flowers of native Buck Lotus (Lotus crassifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. This interesting bushy perennial only grows along the summits of the mountains. It has characteristic flat sprays of leaves. (La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 May 2012)

File.Name: c531a_c531-026.jpg
Notes:
7311880670 I found this perfect little female Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera) insect posed on a twig today. They seem intelligent to me, and they make eye contact. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

Today was a great photo day - dead calm for much of it. Tomorrow is the Dunn Middle School graduation which I must attend as the retired teacher to see my kids off. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: c531b_c531-020.jpg
Notes:
7311780466 Here is a beautiful little Convergent Lady Beetle - aka "ladybug" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera perched on a grass stem. They have a variable number of white spots, and sometimes none, but they always have those two white lines on their thorax. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

There were hundreds of these beetles in the air today. I guess they are converging for a mating rave.

File.Name: c531c_c531-037.jpg
Notes: 7311734426 This is the first California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly that I've seen this year. It's resting on a leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica - or is it R. tomentella?) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - that is just starting to flower. It has pretty little tails. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)
ScientificName: Satyrium californica; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Satyrium californica; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: c531d_c531-030.jpg
Notes:
7311680214 This is a beautiful Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly ovipositing on a leaf of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

The females have brown wings on top, but the males are quite blue. You can see a couple of large "Lupine Aphids" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera on the leaves. Maybe they're lost?
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: c531e_c531-008.jpg
Notes: 7311618440 Here is another perfect Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

File.Name: c531f_c531-001.jpg
Notes:
7311569114 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil by our garden. It looks pretty tattered for this early in the year. I rarely find these butterflies visiting flowers, yet they fly constantly through the oak trees. I wonder how they do it? (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

You can see that this butterfly has a brown border around its orange wing tips - unlike the Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini). See my photo

File.Name: c531g_c531-016.jpg
Notes: 7311466426 I don't know what butterflies these little caterpillars will become, but here they are on a leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - with a few "lupine aphids". You can see some webbing around the caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

File.Name: c531h_c531-004.jpg
Notes:
7311417520 "Snug as a bug in a ... Coffee-Berry leaf?"

This is the neat little nest of some caterpillar or other larvae in a rolled-up leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica - or is it R. tomentella?) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I've seen several of these nest recently. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

File.Name: c601a_c601-004.jpg
Notes:
7317810208 I found this handsome little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) on the edge of the garden after I got got home today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2012)

It's summer - at least that's what my internal calendar says. I went to the Dunn Middle School graduation today - not as a speaker (after 30 years), but in the audience. Yes, it was emotional. I wish you well little wing's!

File.Name: c601b_c531-029.jpg
Notes: 7317775694 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a flower of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Labiatae plant family. It seems to be offering a gift to potential prey in its screen-left toes! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

File.Name: c601c_c531-036.jpg
Notes: 7317751712 This is a beautiful female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly resting for a moment after ovipositing on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

File.Name: c601d_c531-012.jpg
Notes: 7317722922 This is a beautiful native violet Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. (I'll pretend the varieties don't exist - I'm always confused by these!) They are just starting to flower in our canyon at home. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2012)

File.Name: c601e_c529-033.jpg
Notes: 7317664952 Here is a gorgeous new flower of native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei in the Liliaceae) plant family. The plants have hundreds or thousands of these flowers that rise on 6-10 foot tall stalks from a basal rosette of sword-like leaves. They die after flowering, but usually leave lots of pups around them. Beautiful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2012)

File.Name: c601f_c530-006.jpg
Notes:
7317626626 Natty Dread comes to the woodlands?

These are new dreadlock-like catkins of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. My photo also shows the female acorn cups. The male catkins aren't quite ready to release pollen, but the female acorns look ready to receive it. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 May 2012)

The long catkins look wind-pollinated, but they also attract a buzzing swarm of bees, so there must be more to the story. I'll have more photos in a week or two.

File.Name: c601g_c527-017.jpg
Notes:
7317544532 I guess this is the Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - but I'm not sure. It seems to be perennial, and it has wider calyx lobes than the plant in my [Older] photo that surround the new seeds below the flowers. Lynn? (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2012)

Whatever it is, it's different.

File.Name: c601h_c524-025.jpg
Notes:
7317401330 These are flower heads of the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. They have pale, washed-out flowers, but they make it up with those fantastic hairy seed heads. Notice the little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the left flower - they like these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2012)

Now look close at the calyx lobes that surround the new seeds below the flowers. The calyx lobes of this species are narrow. Compare the Phacelia flowers of my [Newer] photo.
ScientificName: Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c602a_c602-029.jpg
Notes:
7324190296 This is a not-so-tiny ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) getting *something* from new pistillate flowers/acorns of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. I watched it for some time, and it kept going back and forth between two groups of these baby acorns. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2012)

I seems strange to think of bitter scrub oaks making sweet honey, but they really do! See my old photo at:
   http://us.fotolog.com/treebeard/37845974

Tomorrow I have a final school commitment at Dunn School for the high school graduation - and to wish my good friend Charley well. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: c602b_c602-001.jpg
Notes: 7324104806 This is an odd insect that I found in a spider web this morning. It's a nymph, not an adult. My first thought looking at its legs was a Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera, but the long antennae seem wrong. I don't know what it is! (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2012)

File.Name: c602c_c602-003.jpg
Notes:
7324022304 I guess this is an atypical little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. It has gold flecks on its abdomen and thorax. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2012)

BugGuide is down tonight, so I can't do better for an ID.

File.Name: c602d_c602-005.jpg
Notes: 7323954542 Here is another tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. This one is collecting pollen - though it happens to be on a pistil. It's the size of a grain of rice. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2012)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: c602e_c602-011.jpg
Notes:
7323892860 Here is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It's hugging the stamens and going for the nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2012)

I can't make out "pollen hairs" on its hind legs, so this might be in the family Megachilidae.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: c602f_c602-024.jpg
Notes: 7323822342 This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) on a flower head of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia 19mar13) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2012)

File.Name: c602g_c602-015.jpg
Notes:
7323779584 Here are fully-open new flowers of native California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae) plant family. It's not common in our mountains, but I find it here and there - and it has an interesting common name. I've never tried to make tea from this plant, but it does have a fragrance. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2012)

This is one of those native plants that could probably disappear from the woods without a trace and make no difference - though it does add some diversity to the woods, and it is diversity that keeps the woods interesting year after year!

File.Name: c602h_c602-017.jpg
Notes: 7323673664 This is a another beautiful native flower of Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family in flower on the pass. You can see a couple of little beetles snuggling in the lower-right. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2012)

File.Name: c603a_c603-029.jpg
Notes:
7332640584 "Like catching an elephant in a mouse trap..."

This unfortunate Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly got snared in a spider web on a shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family by the creek. I guess it's the web of a small Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae). You can see the spider in the upper-right. It must be wondering - what the hell do I do next? (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2012)

My graduation duties are done, and it's the real beginning of summer!
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Linyphiidae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Linyphiidae

File.Name: c603b_c603-038.jpg
Notes:
7332543180 This is a another beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly perched on a flower cluster of Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden this morning. Look in the lower-right - there's a large female Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera flying into my photo! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2012)

I'm pretty sure we got this geranium as a cutting from the old "Casa Vieja" adobe at Christy Ranch on Santa Cruz Island. It's a real old-fashioned geranium!
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Pelargonium; Geraniaceae; Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Pelargonium; Geraniaceae; Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c603c_c603-006.jpg
Notes:
7332356682 Update - I think Aliceinwl1 nailed it - this is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, genus Thereva. And it is in the superfamily Asiloidea which contains the robber flies. See the photos in Alice's link. Thanks again Alice, I didn't feel right with either Rhagionidae or Asilidae.

Original comment:
My first thought was "snipe fly" (Rhagionidae) - because of the abdomen like those old-fashioned collapsible drinking cups for backpacking, if you remember. Then my second thought was "robber fly" (Asilidae) - because of the long abdomen that extends beyond the wings and those hairy front legs. *Sigh*

I'll go with my first thought that this is a Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Rhagio. But that large abdomen still gives me pause. Maybe it's a gravid female full of eggs? (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2012)

It's perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning.

The "June gloom" fog started rolling in this morning as the fog bank got to 2500+ feet to spill over the mountains on the pass. (We live on the north side.) One good thing about the fog is that some insects (especially flies) get out in the sun to warm up - and they're still slow, so I can get photos!
Locality: especially flies) get out in the sun to warm up - and they're still slow
CommonNames: Stiletto Fly; Snipe Fly; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Therevidae; Thereva; Asiloidea; Rhagionidae; Asilidae; Rhagionidae; Asilidae; Rhagionidae; Diptera; Rhagio; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Therevidae; Thereva; Asiloidea; Rhagionidae; Asilidae; Rhagionidae; Asilidae; Rhagionidae; Diptera; Rhagio; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
Families: Therevidae; Rhagionidae; Asilidae; Rhagionidae; Asilidae; Rhagionidae; Rosaceae

File.Name: c603d_c603-002.jpg
Notes: 7332259090 This is another little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) trying to warm up on a succulent leaf in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2012)

File.Name: c603e_c603-019.jpg
Notes: 7332149860 This Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) has a large head! It's perched on a leaf of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae), trying to get warm on a cold morning. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2012)

File.Name: c603f_c603-023.jpg
Notes:
7332100954 Here is another beautiful nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. The tiny bejeweled nymphs have no green at all, but the large dull adults are all green.

This one is perfectly posed at the center of a leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2012)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c603g_c603-026.jpg
Notes: 7332004968 This is the pupal stage of a "ladybug" in the family Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera. It's wearing it's larval coat like a fur stole! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2012)

File.Name: c603h_c603-003.jpg
Notes: 7331958858 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) on new flowers of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It's not really working the flowers, but trying to warm up in the sun on a cold morning. Did you know bees have such hairy eyes? (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2012)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: c604a_c604-017.jpg
Notes:
7340151442 This is a well-camouflaged Coast Horned Lizard aka "horny toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii or P. coronatum). I actually found four of these today, and probably walked right past many more. Sure, they could get work in Hollywood posing for monster movies! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

It was an odd weather day today. It started sunny, but the fog started coming over the mountains early, and the "fog winds" started blowing from the south. So I tried to get above the winds on the ridge above Paradise Canyon. It worked, until the real wind kicked in from the north... *Sigh* It's predicted to be windy for a couple of days.

File.Name: c604b_c604-013.jpg
Notes:
7154916991 Do you see the Coast Horned Lizard aka "horny toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii or P. coronatum)? Well, your not supposed to. That's camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

Hint: it's head is on the right. It's hiding in a growth of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. See my [Newer] photo.
ScientificName: Phrynosoma blainvillii; P. coronatum; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Phrynosoma blainvillii; P. coronatum; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c604c_c604-038.jpg
Notes:
7154852251 You have to look close at this photo...

At the top are two spotted fawns, and at the bottom is mama deer *in the air* chasing them away from me! I guess these are Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus). I startled them as I came down the steep hill on my trail today. The plants are mostly Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

File.Name: c604d_c604-004.jpg
Notes: 7154806165 This is a male Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly perched on an empty glume of non-native (but ubiquitous) Wild Oat (Avena fatua) grass. The females have a pattern of dark veins above, and are quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

File.Name: c604e_c604-059.jpg
Notes:
7154776347 The colors of summer...

This is a two-fer of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies working flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

The population of these butterflies crashed two years ago, but I hope they are recovering. They are still scarce in the canyon at home.

File.Name: c604f_c604-068.jpg
Notes:
7154742029 This photo is for Alice...
(*Go ask Alice...*)

It's a small Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) that I found on my trail today perched on a leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

File.Name: c604g_c604-048.jpg
Notes: 7339910306 This very dark Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) is getting nectar - or something? - from a new seed pod of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: c604h_c604-047.jpg
Notes:
7154664001 Here is another fantastic little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working a flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. I guess they are genus Bombylius. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

There were lots on these bee working the flowers, creating a high-pitched buzz in the air.
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Bombylius
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Bombylius

File.Name: c605a_c605-006.jpg
Notes:
7343626720 I found this remarkable little Treehopper (family Membracidae in the superfamily Fulgoroidea) of the insect order Homoptera on a leaf of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen. They have remarkable eyes, like all jumpers that have to see where they're jumping. Cool! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2012)

It was windy today, but I had a few moments of relative calm this morning for a few photos. There have gusts to 43 mph according to a neighbors weather station on the web. Not quite as bad as the 60 mph winds forecast - at least not yet.
ScientificName: Membracidae; Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: c605b_c605-010.jpg
Notes: 7343572084 This is a small beetle (Coleoptera) taking off from a native flower of Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. It must have taken wing just as I snapped the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2012)

File.Name: c605c_c604-056.jpg
Notes: 7343541404 This is a well-camouflaged caterpillar that I happened to notice on an aging flower head of native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) shrub in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The white caps on its body look so much like the white petals of the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

File.Name: c605d_c605-002.jpg
Notes:
7343493636 These are new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. They have two long stamens and one blue-tipped pistil. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2012)

They start out folded up tight like the flower on the left, but heavy Carpenter Bees (Anthophoridae) can lever them open, like the flower on the right. Once opened, they stay open. The blue-tipped pistils do a little dance of their own. They start off to the side, but move to the center when the flower is opened.

The leaves have a strong smell of camphor, like Vicks. They are widely used for "smudge sticks" - like natural incense. We rescued this plant from a slide along Paradise Road years ago, and it's doing well in the garden.

File.Name: c605e_c604-073.jpg
Notes:
7158216975 These are fantastic red flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They are not common in our mountains, but I find them here and there. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

This uncommon summer flower only grows in a few places in our mountains - along the Knapp's Castle trail (where I got this photo) and near Arroyo Burro on East Camino Cielo road.

I voted today, so I thought red, white, and blue flowers would be appropriate!
Locality:
where I got this photo) and near Arroyo Burro on East Camino Cielo road.

I voted today

File.Name: c605f_c604-010.jpg
Notes:
7343384364 These are fantastic white flowers of Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Papaveraceae plant family. These flowers always remind me of Holy Rollers in white robes having a meeting in the woods with arms raised for glory! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

They are another one of those curious fire-follower plants that sprout after wildfires and flower for a few years, and then disappear into the seedbank - not to be seen until the next wildfire. I've only found this one plant this year, and it's probably the end of them - hopefully for a while!

File.Name: c605g_c604-027.jpg
Notes: 7158161843 These are fantastic blue flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. You can see the long-exerted stamens and Y-forked pistils. I wonder what insect they are made for? (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

File.Name: c605h_c604-030.jpg
Notes:
7343327948 I went back to the ridge above "Paradise Canyon" yesterday to see what the uncommon Chaparral Broomrape (Orobanche bulbosa) in the Orobanchacea plant family was doing. See my photo from May 27. It's turning brown, but there are still some flowers. I don't think I would recognize it as a flowering plant if I saw it now for the first time. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2012)

This is a parasitic plant without leaves or chlorophyll that grows from the roots of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. There's a shrub a foot or two away on the right. Maybe there is a fungal connection between the plants? It has a thick root and a bulbous, scaly stem base - like a real plant that grows underground without the sun.
Locality: Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. There's a shrub a foot or two away on the right. Maybe there is a fungal connection between the plants? It has a thick root and a bulbous
ScientificName: Orobanche bulbosa; Orobanchacea; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Orobanche bulbosa; Orobanchacea; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: c606a_c606-005.jpg
Notes:
7161791561 I found this furry little fledgling Band-Tailed Pigeon (Columba fasciata) under our bird feeder this morning. It's a wild pigeon, not a city pigeon. Maybe it fell from it's nest in the oak tree above? I left it where I found it, and I hope mama is taking care of it. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2012)

The day started and finished windy, but there was a less-windy time when I look my walk today. The wind seems to be dying down now. I hope we're done with it.

File.Name: c606b_c606-032.jpg
Notes: 7161770065 This is a fantastic little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera hovering before new flowers of Allophyllum glutinosum in the Polemoniaceae plant family. The flowers have blue pollen, and you can see some blue color on the fly's proboscis and head and feet. Thus pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2012)

File.Name: c606c_c606-016.jpg
Notes:
7346941226 This is a remarkable Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) that I found today on a stem of a native violet Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't think I've ever seen this beautiful creature before. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2012)

It was interested in the flowers. After I got this photo, I noticed it under the flower head.

File.Name: c606d_c606-026.jpg
Notes:
7161693015 On the left are Lady Beetle larvae in the (Coccinellidae) family of the Coleoptera. I guess they are settling in to pupate into adults.

Now look on the right... These are the husks of "Lupine Aphids" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera. You can see the pupae of Parasitic Wasps (Hymenoptera) where their innards should be! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2012)

I didn't notice the wasp pupae until I got this photo on my computer screen. I'll try to get a closer photo tomorrow.
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; Lupine Aphids; Parasitic Wasps

File.Name: c606e_c606-013.jpg
Notes:
7346861984 This is another Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a new flower of native violet Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. (I'll pretend the varieties don't exist - I'm always confused by these!) (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2012)

It looks like it's casting a fly-fishing line!

File.Name: c606f_c606-046.jpg
Notes: 7346828164 This is a handsome Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2012)

File.Name: c606g_c606-034.jpg
Notes:
7161581483 Here is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly working new flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's our plainest "Blue" butterfly below, but it is deep blue above. Of course it usually folds its wings up when it perches - just to frustrate us photographers! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2012)

This is our earliest butterfly. I found my first one this year on 31 December 2011. This must be a second brood./

File.Name: c606h_c606-043.jpg
Notes:
7346769846 This is a pretty little California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working (over-exposed) flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has cute little tails. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2012)

The Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) flowers are the main attraction for the butterflies in our canyon right now.
CommonNames: California Hairstreak; Golden Yarrow; Golden Yarrow
ScientificName: Satyrium californica; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum

File.Name: c607a_c607-022.jpg
Notes:
7164996289 Update: I defer to someone who knows - thanks to Alice, I believe this is the Bernardino Blue - Euphilotes bernardino aka Euphilotes battoides bernardino.

This is either a small Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes battoides) OR a Dotted Blue (Euphilotes enoptes) butterfly. I found it today down by the river working new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family. As far as I know, there's no way to tell them apart - and I don't really care. It's a pretty little blue! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 June 2012)

The wind is finally dying down - after a week of blowing. It was still breezy today, but manageable.
CommonNames: Bernardino Blue; Square-spotted Blue; Dotted Blue; California Buckwheat

File.Name: c607b_c607-012.jpg
Notes:
7350160592 Here is a beautiful yellow Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working flowers of an old-fashioned flower cluster of Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2012)

I'm sure they are waiting for our wild Tiger Lily flowers to open. (So am I!)
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Geranium; Tiger Lily

File.Name: c607c_c607-002.jpg
Notes: 7164932081 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family this morning. It doesn't do much for pollination, but its just beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2012)

I almost missed this photo - the bee is way on the bottom edge of my original photo.

Notes:
7164816369 I found this odd Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) perched on a rock today near what's left of the Santa Ynez River. This critter is small - 1/2 inch or less long. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 June 2012)

"Go ask Alice!

File.Name: c607h_c607-029.jpg
Notes: 7164787249 This is a new flower head of Sneezeweed aka Rosilla (Helenium puberulum) in the Asteraceae plant family. There's a little Convergent Lady Beetle - aka "ladybug" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera on top. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 June 2012)

I found this beautiful critter on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) nearby, and saw it jump a foot to the flower!

This is a perfect Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a new flower of native violet Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. The curve of its proboscis always reminds me of fly-fishing. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2012)

Notes:
7353225674 This is another remarkable caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin. It's about to finish off the leaves on this Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stem. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2012)

I like the background of this photo!

Notes:
7167977499 This is making eye-contact with a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on the tip of a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2012)

These cute little spiders have been getting scarce in the woods as we move into summer.

File.Name: c608e_c608-029.jpg
Notes:
7353154848 "The walking dead?"

Actually, these "Lupine Aphids" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera aren't walking any more. They are just husks that have been consumed by tiny Parasitic Wasps (Hymenoptera). You can see the wasp pupae where the aphids' innards should be. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2012)

See my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7123118645/

This one is also on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's larger and faster than the larva in my [Older] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2012)

I must have startled it!

File.Name: c609a_c609-037.jpg
Notes:
7356378332 I found this handsome Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2012)

They are uncommon in our mountains, but I find a few every year. They look like whites, but the hooked antennae and bulldog face gives them away as a skipper.

*Pigeon Update*

We had our fledgling pigeon outside today because other Band-Tailed Pigeons (Columba fasciata) were hanging out. Our little pigeon flew away (like a chicken) into a nearby tree. Good luck little wing!

File.Name: c609b_c609-055.jpg
Notes:
7356311592 I guess that this is a Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly working the flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2012)

It's a small grayish-brown butterfly with white frosting above the postmedian line. There's lots of Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family growing nearby.

But we also have the Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) and the Gold Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) which are very similar.

I'll keep trying to stump her!

Notes:
7356048304 Here is another fantastic little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera dipping into a flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. I guess they are genus Bombylius. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2012)

There were so many of these little flies in the air today over the flowers that they made a real noise, like a high-pitched siren!

Notes:
7175061215 This is a mighty Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae ) in the insect order Hemiptera. You can see it's strong proboscis folded under its head. This one is resting on a flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2012)

I've been distracted tonight by TCM showing "The Wizard Of Oz" and "Meet Me In St. Louis" - as good as it gets!

I've also been distracted by starting to pack for the upcoming Live Oak Music Festival! It will be another fabulous weekend of music just a few miles down the road from where we live.

(flygrl67 and rjadams55 - do you attend? It's more of a SLO thing. Look for me at the NW corner of Mandolin Lane and Live Oak Way. I'm the guy with the long white beard!)

File.Name: c610b_c610-001.jpg
Notes:
7360207456 I noticed this tiny Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae ) in the insect order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. You can see its proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2012)

This little critter looks a lot like an ant, especially when it moves - but less so motionless in a photo. We have better ant-mimic Hemiptera in the mountains, and I hope to get a photo soon.

File.Name: c610d_c610-012.jpg
Notes:
7360120062 Our fledgling Band-Tailed Pigeon (Columba fasciata) flew off to a nearby tree last night, but it came home this morning for more peanut butter and birdseed! Here it is perched on my son Ryan's hand. This is a native mountain pigeon, not a city bird. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2012)

It's flown off again into the trees, but I hope will come home again tomorrow.

File.Name: c610e_c610-035.jpg
Notes:
7360062290 This is the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (This is the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2012)

Botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen in 1831 from the coast, but the inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) is much more common. But his named collection is official.

If you compare my [Older] photo of L. subspicata var. denudata), this one has narrower leaves. It's interesting that this species also has salmon-colored berries, while the variety is red.
Locality:
L. subspicata var. denudata) is much more common. But his named collection is official.

If you compare my [Older] photo of L. subspicata var. denudata)

Notes:
7359949760 These are normal flowers of beautiful native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Note the wide leaves and compare my [Newer] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2012)

This native honeysuckle grows erect - with some vining tendencies - unlike the California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) which grows as a vine. Compare the galled flowers in my [Older] photo.

Notes:
7174629787 This is a deformed flower of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Compare the normal flowers at this photo.

I guess the flower is being galled by these little Aphids in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera. These galled flowers are not only deformed, but they stay on the plant for months. This is Interesting natural history. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2012)

File.Name: c611a_c611-046.jpg
Notes:
7178979043 This is - I kid you not - the "Dog Vomit Slime Mold" - really! If you don't believe me, you can find it at Wikipedia.

This is NOT a fungus - for one reason, it can move. This is the interesting slime mold Fuligo septica in the fascinating Myxomycetes class. This photo shows the mobile plasmodium stage becoming the solid lime-encrusted spore-bearing aethalium stage. It looks and feels like a toasted marshmallow, and it's about the same size. Julie found it this afternoon growing on oak leaves in our compost bin. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2012)

File.Name: c611b_c611-039.jpg
Notes:
7364150674 This is a wonderful new flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - our native Tiger Lily. It looks like it belongs in a garden, but its growing wild and free. These flowers are not rare, but not common either. It's always a surprise to find them. This is the first one I've seen in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2012)

The plants in our canyon at home are huge, 8+ feet tall. They are not flowering yet, but it won't be long.

File.Name: c611c_c611-010.jpg
Notes:
7178887605 This is a handsome Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower head of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on a sandy place along the Santa Ynez River. I've been waiting for these beautiful flowers to show. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 June 2012)

This plant has an intense peppermint smell that is shocking when you walk through a patch of it without knowing the source.

Wildflowers come in the spring in California, but I love our summer flowers!
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Monardella lanceolata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Monardella lanceolata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: c611d_c611-025.jpg
Notes:
7364074970 If you are hiking in the woods and suddenly smell peppermint, it might be this plant. This is sweet-smelling Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family, with new flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 June 2012)

I appreciate that "yerba buena" means any local plant that smells like peppermint, but in California this is THE yerba buena, the original namesake of the city of San Francisco. It has a spicy taste and makes great peppermint tea when dried. It's not common in our woods, but I find it here and there.

File.Name: c611e_c611-028.jpg
Notes:
7364036066 This is a beautiful small Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) munching a flower of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It has striking colors - or rather, the lack of colors. It's quite small, 1/2 inch or so. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 June 2012)

This one's for you, Alice!

File.Name: c611f_c611-005.jpg
Notes: 7178744031 This is an odd butterfly that I found today on flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. My first thought was Bernardino Blue aka Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes bernardino), but it has very faint orange spots on its hindwings. I don't know what this is. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 June 2012)
CommonNames: California Buckwheat; Bernardino Blue; Square-spotted Blue

Notes: 7178678043 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working an equally tiny flower of (I think) Hollyleaf Navarretia (Navarretia atractyloides) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. Notes: 7363873086 I believe this is a skinny little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera - maybe genus Thevenetimyia. It's working (over-exposed) flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2012)
