File.Name: c612a_c612-005.jpg
Notes:
7182209189 These are brilliant little flowers of native Canchalagua (Centaurium venustum - aka Zeltnera venusta) in the unusual (for us) Gentianaceae plant family. If you do a web search, you'll find it is popular in homeopathy for a range of treatments. I would rather find the unusual summer flowers! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 12 June 2012)

I did get some new photos today, but I've been pretty preoccupied with getting ready for the upcoming Live Oak Music Festival.

Tomorrow might be hopeless with shopping to do and a meeting in the evening. And Thursday afternoon, the festival line-up for camping spots starts! This might well be my last post for a week...

File.Name: c612b_c612-007.jpg
Notes: 7367414918 This is small - but rather pretty, if you get down on your hands and knees - flower of native Spanish Clover (Lotus purshianus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It grows in dry, sandy places in our mountains. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 12 June 2012)

File.Name: c612c_c612-012.jpg
Notes: 7182139217 I believe this is a tiny (but proud!) flower of Bishop's Lotus (Lotus strigosus) in the Fabaceae plant family. "Strigose" is one of those botanical words that means "with stiff, straight, sharp, appressed hairs". You can see strigose hairs on the leaves and seed pods. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 June 2012)

File.Name: c612d_c611-013.jpg
Notes: 7367331510 These are odd flowers of the native Thyme-leaved Spurge (Chamaesyce serpyllifolia) in the locally uncommon Euphorbiaceae plant family. It's a very small annual palnt that grows prostrate on sandy flats along the river. Those white flowers are a mm or less across. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 June 2012)

File.Name: c612e_c612-003.jpg
Notes:
7367280324 I believe this is the native Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua) in the in the Poacea plant family with new seeds. The seeds are distinctive - with long characteristic bent awns that are also twisted. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 12 June 2012)

California used to look very different - not the "golden rolling hills of California" - as Kate Wolf used to sing about so eloquently, bless her. The golden hills come from the non-native annual grasses. The native grasses are different - more gray, and perennial.

File.Name: c612f_c611-003.jpg
Notes: 7182040691 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly showing her pretty blue colors. This one is "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil by our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2012)

Notes: 7182020533 This Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) is the same size and shape as a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) - but the colors are all wrong, so it must be something else. This one is working new summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 June 2012)

Notes:
7367198478 These are native tiny ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending a colony of little black Aphids in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera on a grass blade of native Western Rye (Elymus glaucus). (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2012)

I'm pretty sure these interesting ants are genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. Look close at the ant on bottom. They have two spines on their thorax and an articulated abdomen.

These are people I love - from left to right: John on fiddle, Josh on guitar, Jigger shooting video, Ewan on fretless banjo, and Wil on guitar. That red light on the left is my Zoom digital recorder, capturing the action. (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 17 June 2012)

I've had no choice tonight but to use the "New Uploader" for Flickr today. I don't like it. It's slow and it feels claustrophobic. I hope Flickr doesn't go the way of Fotolog in making it look slick at the cost of usability.

Notes: 7405144222 Here are is my son Ewan and my grandkids Kelvin and Otto in our camp at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend. (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 16 June 2012)

File.Name: c619g_c617-001.jpg
Notes: 7405029048 This is our camp at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend - quiet in daytime, noisy at night! The flag reads "Night Owl Camp - kicking out the jams since 1995" - but I think it's been even longer, since 1993, which makes this 20 years! (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 17 June 2012)

File.Name: c619h_c616-003.jpg
Notes:
7404905276 The acorn woodpeckers are also busy at the Live Oak Music Festival!

This is an Acorn Woodpecker (Melanerpes formicivorus) granary tree that's full of acorns. It also happens to be a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family near our campsite, which is appropriate for the Live Oak Music Festival. (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 16 June 2012)

File.Name: c620a_c620-004.jpg
Notes:
Pretty good camouflage!

I saw this large dragonfly fly into a mass of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family today. I went to look for it and almost gave up, when I suddenly saw it right before my eyes. I guess it's a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly, in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2012)

Today is the Summer Solstice - the longest day of the year, and the official start of summer. The days will start getting shorter tomorrow in our mountains north of the Equator, and the sun will rise just a bit lower in the sky tomorrow. I guess the stumper is why the longest day of the year comes at the start of summer rather than the middle?

I found this one yesterday resting on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2012)

Certain other members of this family resemble wasps, but not this one.

Notes: This is a handsome little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera resting on the ground. It's been working the new summer flowers of Allophyllum glutinosum in the Polemoniaceae plant family. (See this photo.) What large eyes it has! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2012)

File.Name: c620d_c619-027.jpg
Notes: I believe this is the uncommon - at least I rarely notice it - Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly perched on flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's a plain brown hairstreak, without the white frosting of the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly or the darker markings of the Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2012)

Notes: This is a beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on new summer flowers of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. Look close to see the little beetle under its proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2012)

File.Name: c620f_c619-059.jpg
Notes: Here is a tattered Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly ovipositing on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2012)

The golden beetles are handsome Metallic Wood-boring Beetles(Buprestidae) in the order Coleoptera. And look at the little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) flying in on the left! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2012)

File.Name: c621a_c621-010.jpg
Notes: 7417946290 Here is a beautiful Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly on bare ground in our garden this morning. I've been seeing them in the woods, but this is the first one I've managed to capture a photo of this year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2012)

File.Name: c621b_c621-015.jpg
Notes: 7417876670 I apologize that this photo isn't quite sharp, but this remarkable bee-wasp (order Hymeoptera) moves rapidly and is hard to follow. It looks like a wasp, but it has flat leg segments with hairs like a bee. It is the reason why the Impatiens flowers in our garden are looking so ratty - the bee-wasps are taking chunks of the flowers, but only the dark colored flowers. I'll keep trying to get a better photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2012)

File.Name: c621c_c621-006.jpg
Notes: 7417839740 I found this gorgeous iridescent green/blue Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) in the garden this morning. These little flies are predators. Note the scooped-out head - it's a trait shared by other predators like robber flies and dragonflies. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2012)

File.Name: c621d_c621-035.jpg
Notes:
7417748968 I guess (using the museum handout) that this is a Julia Heliconian (Dryas iulia) butterfly. It's native from Brazil to Florida and Texas, but I found this one today at the Butterflies Alive! exhibit at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. It seems to be on a white flowered Agapanthus. (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 21 June 2012)

I don't care much for critters in captivity, but I'll admit it was fun and educational. It's a large screen enclosure filled with butterflies and butterfly plants. Tripods are not allowed, but monopods are OK. I got this photo hand-held with my normal lens - I want to go back with my macro lens!

Julie and I took our grandkids to the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History today to see dinosaurs, butterflies, and birds - great fun!
Locality: Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History

7417587588 I believe this is the Pipevine Swallowtail (Battus philenor) butterfly. They feed on plants of Pipevines or Dutchman's pipes in the Aristolochiaceae plant family, which we don't have in Santa Barbara. This butterfly is part of the Butterflies Alive! exhibit at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. I don't much care for critters in captivity, but I'll admit it was fun and educational. I got this photo hand-held with my normal lens - I want to go back with my macro lens! (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 21 June 2012)

Notes:
7417507678 Here are Kachina and Kanati - female (left) and male (right) American Kestrel (Falco sparverius) falcons. Males and females are quite different. Both birds are part of the Eyes In The Sky program of the Santa Barbara Audubon Society. Both were hit by cars, but they are doing good work introducing many kids to native hawks. Beautiful birds! (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 21 June 2012)

Julie and I took our grandkids to the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History today to see dinosaurs, butterflies, and birds - great fun!
Locality: Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History

File.Name: c621g_c621-044.jpg
Notes:
7417448984 This is Kisa, a Peregrine Falcon (Falco peregrinus) that is part of the Eyes In The Sky program of the Santa Barbara Audubon Society. It's a rescued falcon with a bum wing. It will never fly again, but it's doing good work introducing many kids to native hawks. It's giving me the eye! (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 21 June 2012)

Julie and I took our grandkids to the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History today to see dinosaurs, butterflies, and birds - great fun!
Locality: Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History

File.Name: c621h_c621-049.jpg
Notes:
7417339748 This is Puku, a Western Screech Owl (Otus kennicottii) that is part of the Eyes In The Sky program of the Santa Barbara Audubon Society. It's a rescued owl with cataracts. It can't see well enough to hunt, but it's doing good work introducing many kids to native owls. Cute little guy! (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 21 June 2012)

Julie and I took our grandkids to the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History today to see dinosaurs, butterflies, and birds - great fun!
Locality: Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History

File.Name: c622a_c622-045.jpg
Notes:
7423378672 I found this Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the insect order Diptera working flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Maybe its Physocephala burgessi in the subfamily Conopinae? It's a remarkable wasp mimic, but it really is a fly - just count the wings. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

Note the up-turned proboscis and down-turned abdomen, and compare My photo of a different Conopidae fly. There is a family resemblance.

I had many photos of interesting flies today, so I decided to add a few from the archive to make this an all-fly day. I love photographing flies - they have so much structural detail!

File.Name: c622b_c622-047.jpg
Notes:
7423272656 This is another atypical little Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera. At least this one has some bristles on its abdomen. File.Name: c623a_c623-002.jpg
Notes:
7429271366 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working non-native Zinnia flowers in the garden. The females are pretty enough, but capturing a male with it's wings open is my ongoing quest! Single-flowered Zinnias and other composites seem to be the favorite butterfly flowers in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2012)

I've been preoccupied today. The bad news is that my son Ewan went to the hospital last night for an emergency appendectomy. The good news is that we get to keep Roz and the grandkids for a few extra days before they fly back to Portland!

OR is it a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium)? The two native butterflies are very similar, but I reckon the white "frosting" on its hindwings and short tails and pointy forewings makes it the former. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

File.Name: c623c_c622-006.jpg
Notes: 7429123198 This is a brilliant yellow Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working new flowers of a well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. This butterfly is missing its tails - the birds take a toll. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

File.Name: c623d_c622-010.jpg
Notes: 7429064984 Here is a black and white Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a flower head of non-native Scabiosa atropurpurea "Black" in the garden. The flowers have such a deep red color that they almost look black. They are hard to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

File.Name: c623e_c622-022.jpg
Notes: 7429032312 This is a beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. I dig its punk hair! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

File.Name: c623f_c619-039.jpg
Notes: 7428960696 These are new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - one of my favorite summer flowers. They are not so pretty as the speckled flowers of Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in my [Older] photos, but they make up for it by being tougher - they flower much of the summer in our summer-dry mountains. The plant has a musty smell that is not quite pleasant. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2012)
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mustang Mint

File.Name: c623g_c623-004.jpg
Notes: 7428923768 This is a flower head of a native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) plant in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in a sandy place along the Santa Ynez River. The flowers are beautiful with those speckled petals that have little balls on the tips. This plant has an intense peppermint smell that is shocking when you walk through a patch of it without knowing the source. It seems to be uncommon. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 June 2012)

File.Name: c623h_c623-010.jpg
Notes: 7428863690 This is most of a native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) plant in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in a sandy place along the Santa Ynez River with weedy grasses. This plant has an intense peppermint smell that is shocking when you walk through a patch of it without knowing the source. It looks wilted. Did you notice the little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly on the top flower? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 June 2012)

File.Name: c624a_c624-031.jpg
Notes:
7437293700 Even butterflies know the art of "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering under the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen!

I guess this is a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) - it seems too yellow - practicing "nectar theft" on a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly" in the Lamiaceae plant family) in the garden. See my [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2012)

I had 8 photos ready to go tonight, but after posting my first one, we got a call to visit my son in the hospital. He's OK, it's late, and four photos will do just fine tonight.

File.Name: c624b_c621-020.jpg

7437217786 This large black Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) is perched on a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly" in the Lamiaceae plant family) in the garden. It's also engaged in the act of "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering under the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. Many insects seem to know this trick - see my [Older] and [Newer] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2012)

This bee is carrying a heavy load of mites - those pale spots on its back.

"Everyone does it so it must be alright!"
There's an interesting entry about nectar theft on Wikipedia.

File.Name: c625a_c625-010.jpg
Notes:
7445248378 "Who ordered the crane fly?"

This is one of the pair of busy House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) that occupied a bird house on our front porch. It has a large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera. If you look close in the dark shadows, you can see a waiting open mouth! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2012)

File.Name: c625b_c625-008.jpg
Notes:
7444951734 What we do for a photo!

This is our worst Biting Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera) filling up with blood from my left hand. I got the photo with my right hand. Maybe it's a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia, but I can't find it at BugGuide. These flies have a distinct brown-orange color, even when they're not full of my blood. They are the worst biters in our mountains because they are so fast and alert - they fly away before you can swat them. Fortunately they are only on the wing for a few weeks. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2012)

Compare my photo at:
http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7284111140/
CommonNames: Biting Snipe Fly; Rocky Mountain Bite Fly

File.Name: c625c_c625-005.jpg
Notes: 7444882394 I found this tiny nymph in the order Orthoptera inside an orange flower of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family today. After I found this one, I looked around and found a few more inside other flowers. Maybe they are Tree Crickets (genus Oecanthus)? (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2012)

File.Name: c625d_c624-015.jpg
Notes: 7444557494 I found this perfect Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly perched on new flower heads of (I think) Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see the iridescent eyespots on its hind wings. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2012)

File.Name: c625e_c624-008.jpg
Notes:
7444452566 This is a single flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - our native Tiger Lily. They are quite large - the size of my open hand. It looks like it belongs in a garden, but it's growing wild and free. These flowers are not rare, but not common either. It's always a surprise to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2012)

I believe they are pollinated by Swallowtail (genus Papilio) butterflies which hand down from the flowers and get pollen on their wings which is rubbed off on the pistil of another flower. See this old photo of mine.

File.Name: c625f_c624-007.jpg
Notes: 7444285050 This is the beautiful whole flower head of a native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - our native Tiger Lily. This one has 14 buds, and the plants in our canyon have even more. It looks like it belongs in a garden, but it's growing wild and free. These flowers are not rare, but not common either. It's always a surprise to find them. The tree in the background is Incense Cedar (Caalocedrus decurrens) in the Cupressaceae plant family. I don't believe it is native here, but there are large trees that look native with successive younger generations in Kinevan Canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2012)
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Caalocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae
associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Caalocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae

This is an unusual wasp-like Clearwing Moth (family Sesiidae) in the order Lepidoptera, and it seems worth posting even though it's not quite sharp. These wasp-mimic moths are diurnal - out in the daytime. This one has clear wings and a flat tail like a fish! I found It resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2012)

File.Name: c626d_c626-042.jpg
Notes:
7451401760 Maybe you can tell by the backlit pattern on its wings that this is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - one of my favorite summer flowers. Of course it has it wings closed. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2012)

The holy grail of local butterfly photographers must surely be to capture the iridescent "dog face" on the upper wings of this butterfly. It's a beautiful butterfly, with a brilliant iridescent violet sheen that is only revealed in the air when the butterfly takes wing. This is the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists and a few butterfly photographers know why!

Notes:
7458479300 These are mating Damselflies (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) in what's left of the Santa Ynez River today - with reflections. I had grandpa duties today - taking my son to the airport and my grandkids to the river - not too bad!

File.Name: c628a_c628-016.jpg
Notes:
7464439334 These are "tent caterpillars", but they are not the bad kind.

This is the tent of little web-spinner Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly caterpillars on Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. You can see the tiny caterpillars on the web. They may kill a branch or two of the plants, but they do not do too much damage. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year!

File.Name: c628b_c628-022.jpg
Notes: 7464353480 This is a natural candelabra of new flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. The stems hang down and the flowers look up. They are classic hummingbird flowers - the reason why the hummers haven't been much interested in my feeder lately. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

File.Name: c628c_c628-031.jpg
Notes:
7464257694 Here is a mature Katydid in the family Tettigoniidae of the suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera. It's perched in a large shrub of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family - recognizable because of the golden fuzz under the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

It may well be an Angle-wing Katydid in the genus Microcentrum - possibly the California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). These are gentle leaf-like creatures which do no harm. The Bush Katydids (genus Scudderia) that I've been showing as nymphs look different.
CommonNames: Katydid; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Angle-wing Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid; Bush Katydids

File.Name: c628d_c628-034.jpg
Notes: 7464215470 This is a tiny Wasp in the insect order Hymenoptera perched on a small new seed of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I suspect it's been ovipositing. I have no idea what kind of wasp it is. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

File.Name: c628e_c628-037.jpg
Notes: 7464143780 This is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on a leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The individual eggs look like little salt shakers with a circle of pores around the top. I have no idea what kind of insect they contain. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

File.Name: c628f_c628-011.jpg
Notes: 7464115318 This is one of the last flowers of annual native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. I'll say it's "hispida"! You can see where it gets its name with those very hairy seed pods. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

File.Name: c628g_c628-004.jpg
Notes:
7464056070 Here are insect galls on a leaf of a native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I don't know what critter live inside these galls. It has "leaves of three (let it be)" like poison oak, but the leaves are different. (See my [Older] photo.) The middle leaf does not have a separate stalk; it has berries rather than dry seeds; it has a distinctive "skunk-like" smell; and it does not make you itch. The berries are tart and tasty if you "suck and spit" - but don't suck too long or you notice the skunk! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

The Chumash/Spanish name for this plant is Chiquihuite. It was an important plant for the native Americans, who used the stems for the "warp" of their baskets.

File.Name: c628h_c628-007.jpg
Notes:
7463913156 This is native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family - with new seeds. The leaves seem to be turning red for "fall colors" early this year, as it's barely summer. I'm not surprised - it's been a very dry year, with only about 50% of normal rainfall. Not good for summer flowers or insects, but it is a chance to see some summer drought adaptations - like this. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

The leaves contain the oil urushiol that causes an allergic skin reaction in many people. It's an allergen, not a poison. Enjoy, but don't touch - at least know your limits! I'm one of the lucky ones who doesn't usually get the rash. It's a good thing - considering how much time I spend in the woods!

File.Name: c629a_c629-055.jpg
Notes:
7470045438 This photo shows how native Humboldt Lilies (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family get pollinated.

They are pollinated by large Swallowtail (genus Papilio) butterflies - Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus) or in this case a Pale Swallowtail (P. eurymedon) butterfly. Look how the butterfly hangs from the flower and gets its wings dusted with pollen from those large stamens! The female stigma of the flower hangs down in the center, but it gets more curved as time goes on until it meets the butterflies wings to transfer pollen. It's a beautiful process to watch. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2012)
CommonNames: Humboldt Lilies; Swallowtail; Western Tiger Swallowtails; Pale Swallowtail
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio; P. rutulus; P. eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio; P. rutulus; P. eurymedon

File.Name: c629b_c629-035.jpg
Notes: 7470022998 This is a beautiful female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - one of my favorite summer flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2012)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: c629c_c629-031.jpg
Notes: 7469986232 This is a small Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly on new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - one of my favorite summer flowers. I don't try to identify skippers - I just choose to "skip her". (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2012)

File.Name: c629d_c629-034.jpg
Notes:
7469898018 This is a mÈnage ‡ trois of pretty little golden Metallic Wood-boring Beetles(Buprestidae) in the order Coleoptera on a new flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - one of my favorite summer flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2012)

The beetles have - uh - impressive sclerotised genitalia!
CommonNames: Metallic Wood-boring Beetles; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: c629e_c629-019.jpg
Notes: 7469848714 Here is another fantastic nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It's perched on a branch of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. They remind me of a little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2012)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: c629f_c629-025.jpg
Notes:
7469780398 These are tiny ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. The flat filaments of the stamens remind me of those wide matches that restaurants used to give out. The ants are really tiny, only a couple of mm long. They have a double nodal bump. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2012)

There were many flowers on this plant, but the ants picked only this one to work. Why?

File.Name: c629g_c629-044.jpg
Notes:
7469690942 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae resting on a native shrub of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2012)

The skinny abdomen of the spider looks amazing, like a lava lamp! It also looks strange. This spider is quite large, so I reckon it's female. Maybe the skinny abdomen fills out as the spider eats and develops eggs?
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: c629h_c629-003.jpg
Notes:
7469636536 This small spider has captured a small Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera). I guess from the eyes that it's an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae, but you can't tell it from the web. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2012)

I haven't seen too many spiders lately, but their time is coming. Insects start disappearing as our dry summer goes on into fall, and the spiders are there to put them to good use - making more spiders!

File.Name: c630a_c630-025.jpg
Notes:
7476198630 Here is an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - the first I've seen this year. It's sharing this flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family with a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. This is our third swallowtail - after the Pale Swallowtail (P. eurymedon) and the Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus). It's different because the black on it forewing runs along the wing rather than across it. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

The common name is doubly wrong, since this native butterfly usually lays eggs on non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family, and it really should be laying eggs of other native plants in the Apiaceae plant family.

I've been looking for these butterflies, or their caterpillars. We always keep some fennel plants just for them - why don't you come by and visit!
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Variable Checkerspot; Pale Swallowtail; Western Tiger Swallowtail; Fennel
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; P. eurymedon; P. rutulus; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; P. eurymedon; P. rutulus; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae

File.Name: c630b_c630-027.jpg
Notes: 7476159224 This is a brilliant male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I didn't capture the iridescent "dog face" on the upper wings,but you can see a hint of it on the upper wings. This is the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists and a few butterfly photographers know why! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: c630c_c630-031.jpg
Notes: 7476093956 This is another fantastic Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - one of my favorite summer flowers. I took many photos of this critter in the air to get this keeper. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: c630d_c630-043.jpg
Notes: 7476008998 This is a beautiful Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working new flowers of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I especially like that little orange patch on its head. They seem to be early this year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c630e_c630-054.jpg
Notes: 7475779166 I believe this is a beautiful female Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly perched on a weedy thistle flower head. The females look very different from the males, see my photo here. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c630f_c630-055.jpg
Notes: 7475704918 I guess this is another Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly perched on a spent flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has a pointed forewing, very short tails, and a frosted white postmedian line on the hindwing. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c630g_c630-013.jpg
Notes: 7475665400 This is a closer shot of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on dreadlock-like catkins of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Bees, flies, and even butterflies seem to be interested in the catkins. Do they produce some nectar as well as pollen? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c630h_c630-011.jpg
Notes:
7475586782 Natty Dread comes to the woodlands?

These are new dreadlock-like catkins of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. But look close - there is a in the upper left. Bees, flies, and even butterflies seem to be interested in the catkins. Do they produce some nectar as well as pollen? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c701a_c630-007.jpg
Notes:
7484022016 All summer flowers are remarkable in our summer-dry climate - especially in this drought summer. But this native summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now maybe renamed as C. fimbriata? - in the Liliaceae plant family blows away all my expectations. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. This species is listed as "rare, threatened or endangered in California and elsewhere" and "fairly endangered in California" (CNPS: 1B.2). It's doing fine in our mountains, even in this drought year. The flowers are just beginning. Look at all the insects inside! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

My photos today are all mid-summer flowers that come out during the harshest dry time in our mountains. They are tough critters who can take the heat. Maybe the benefit is that there are more pollinating insects?

My son Ewan came home today after being in the hospital for 9 days!
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: c701b_c630-052.jpg
Notes: 7483973454 Here are fantastic red summer flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They are not common in our mountains, but I find them here and there. They've been flowering all the month of June and are just about done for the year. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c701c_c630-056.jpg
Notes:
7483861292 These are summer flowers of native Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - though they've been flowering all the month of June and are just about done for the year. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

It's strange - the flowers LOOK blue, but they come out magenta in my photos. Is it something to do with UV?

File.Name: c701d_c701-005.jpg
Notes:
7483780226 These are summer flowers of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. There's a small Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) in the throat of the flower on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2012)

These flowers have a neat trick. See that little white flap on the flower on the right? It's the stigma - the female receptive part of the flower, with the yellow stamens down lower. If you take a grass stem and touch it, you can watch it close in a few seconds, like the flower on the left. This is a fun trick to do with grandkids!
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c701e_c630-009.jpg
Notes: 7483696084 These are new summer flowers of native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a small plant, less than one foot tall. It is rather pretty, with those violet stamens and pistils. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c701f_c701-029.jpg
Notes: 7483608002 These are new summer flowers and buds of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed (Hiericium argutum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on a rock ledge in the canyon. It's a plant that is just starting to flower in our summer-dry mountains. It favors cool rocky places. It's hard to photograph because the flowers are spread out on long stalks that sway in the slightest breeze. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2012)

File.Name: c701g_c630-047.jpg
Notes: 7483576160 Here are characteristic flower heads of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's just beginning to flower in our summer-dry mountains, and it will flower for much of the remaining summer. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c701h_c630-038.jpg
Notes: 7483399392 These are new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family, with campus point and UCSB and the Santa Barbara channel in the background. I got this photo near the FAA radio tower at the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro saddle. The flowers are unexpected but welcome in our dry summer season. Butterflies like them - see my [Older] photos. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c702a_c701-024.jpg
Notes: 7491259878 Here is a small fierce-looking Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) with a tiny beetle (Coleoptera) for prey. This bug is perfectly camouflaged in the new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. They are wicked predators - I'm glad they're tiny! This is the first one I've noticed this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2012)
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Coleoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Coleoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: c702b_c701-028.jpg
Notes: 7491147632 This is a handsome little beetle (Coleoptera) munching a stamen of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I guess from its shape that it's a small Longhorn Beetle in the family Cerambycidae, but I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2012)

File.Name: c702c_c701-007.jpg
Notes:
7491002632 This is a tiny "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera hiding in the throat of a Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. I think it might be Closterocoris amoenus or kin in the family Miridae, though I really can't tell from this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2012)

Many insects use the Bush Monkey Flowers for shelter, but few actually seem to be pollinators. I sometimes find the remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - maybe Eulonchus smaragdinus working the flowers, but I haven't got a photo yet this year. See my old photos at:
   http://us.fotolog.com/treebeard/67646163/
   http://us.fotolog.com/treebeard/37747572/
CommonNames: true bug; Bush Monkey Flower; Bush Monkey Flowers; Metallic Small-Headed Fly
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Diptera; Eulonchus smaragdinus
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Diptera; Eulonchus smaragdinus

File.Name: c702d_c701-001.jpg
Notes:
7490902046 This is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) struggling to get into a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. The bee can't do it!

The flowers are a fulcrum that takes a heavier bee - mostly Carpenter Bees (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae at our house - to open. You can see that the stamens and blue-tipped pistil require a larger bee to bring them around. Once opened, the ordinary bees can get in - for not-so-sweet seconds. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2012)
CommonNames: Honey Bee; White Sage; Carpenter Bees
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae

File.Name: c702e_c630-035.jpg
Notes: 7490873244 This is another fantastic Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - one of my favorite summer flowers. Of course I took many photos of this critter in the air to get this keeper. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: c702f_c630-018.jpg
Notes: 7490796472 These are new dreadlock-like catkins of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family - with Little Pine Mountain and Madulce Peak in the background. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c702g_c630-010.jpg
Notes: 7490740388 This is much of a native summer plant of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now maybe renamed as C. fimbriata? - in the Liliaceae plant family. You can see it's natural habitat - bare, dry, rocky soil where nothing should be able to grow - but these tough lilies do just fine! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: c702h_c630-003.jpg
Notes:
7490689640 Here is another native summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now maybe renamed as C. fimbriata? - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. This species is listed as "rare, threatened or endangered in California and elsewhere" and "fairly endangered in California" (CNPS: 1B.2). It's doing fine in our mountains, even in this drought year. The flowers are just beginning. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

If you compare my [Older] photo, you can see that different flowers are very different. They have variable amounts of yellow and violet. It's part of what makes them so charming.
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: c703a_c703-026.jpg
Notes:
7498271622 This critter has two wings - it's a fly. And that's not a stinger, it's an extra-long proboscis coming from it's head, I'm pretty sure this is a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - I guess Eulonchus smaragdinus. Neither bee nor flower is in focus, but the proboscis is perfect! It's exiting from a flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. The flies are a perfect match for the flowers, one of the few insects I find working them. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

See the photos at BugGuide. They patrol a 50 foot patch of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family, and stop randomly at a flower here or there in a way that is impossible to predict. They are very fast. I guess they are fairly common, but photos of them are rather uncommon!

Ewan and Roz and my grandkids - with Julie, to help out while Ewan recovers - fly back to Portland tomorrow. I'm ready for some peace and quiet!
CommonNames: Metallic Small-Headed Fly; Bush Monkey Flower; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Diptera; Eulonchus smaragdinus; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; photos

File.Name: c703b_c703-034.jpg
Notes: 7498225968 I spotted this male Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into the new flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. I took me a minute to realize that it was committing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering under the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

File.Name: c703c_c703-029.jpg
Notes:
7498102238 This is a new American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden today. This is the first one I've managed to get a photo of this year. You can see the four eye spots on the upper hindwing, but you can't make out the two large eyespots below. The white spot in the orange rectangle of its upper wing is characteristic, as is the bright orange color below. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

These butterflies favor Gnaphalium "everlasting flowers" flowers in the Asteraceae plant family. I'll have a photo of a nest tomorrow.

File.Name: c703d_c703-008.jpg
Notes: 7498035338 This is a Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly eye-to-eye with a tiny Wasp (that has a long ovipositor) in the insect order Hymenoptera. They are meeting on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: c703e_c703-019.jpg
Notes:
7497990966 Here is a black and white Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

Hah, I know where this butterfly has been. You can see the pollen of a native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family on the veins of its lower wing. See my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7470045438/
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Humboldt Lily
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

File.Name: c703f_c703-012.jpg
Notes: 7497916948 Here I am face-to-face with a small brown Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera. It's perched on a dried flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

File.Name: c703g_c703-007.jpg
Notes: 7497823592 I believe these are the very glandular new flower heads of Coast Tarweed (Madia sativa) in the Asteraceae plant family. The pretty composite flowers open and close early in the morning, so I rarely see them, and these flowers are already closing. We also have the smaller Madia gracilis in our mountains (see this photo), and some plants that seem to be intermediate between the two. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

File.Name: c703h_c702-003.jpg
Notes: 7497782492 These are blushing new berries of (probably) native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. They look like little apples! There's some confusion about what species our plants really are. The real S. mollis is said to only grow a foot or two tall, and our plants (more like S. albus) are 2-4 feet tall. It definitely some kind of Snowberry. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2012)
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus

File.Name: c704a_7704-094.jpg
Notes:
7505122300 Who needs fireworks for Independence Day when there is a red, white, and blue butterfly like this in the woods!

This is an astounding Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly "puddling" on wet ground near the garden. It let me get close. Why should any critter in nature look so beautiful to our human eyes? (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2007)

My comment five years ago said: "I'm listening to Peter Tosh - 'Talking Revolution' - this is a good day to remember our American roots." Well, I'm listening to him again now - good stuff, good message.

I took the notion to do red, white, and blue flowers today for the Fourth of July, with a couple of bookends from the archive. This is an old photo from July 4, 2007 - 5 years ago! I've never seen these butterflies in the garden again, though I usually find them by the river later in summer, in Paradise.

I helped drive Julie and the kids and the grandkids to the airport today. The house is quiet, and I'm thankful.

File.Name: c704b_c703-023.jpg
Notes: 7505049998 This is native RED Fringed Indian Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. major) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family. The species name "laciniata" sounds like "lacerate" - irregularly slashed and jagged as if torn. Freddy Krueger comes to mind, but I'd rather think of Henri Matisse in his later years. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

File.Name: c704c_c704-018.jpg
Notes: 7505019284 This is a fantastic large WHITE magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) flower in the Solanaceae plant family by the river. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 July 2012)

File.Name: c704d_c703-021.jpg
Notes: 7504941392 These are fantastic BLUE summer flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The long stamens and pistils look like the trails of fireworks - fitting for the Fourth of July! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

File.Name: c704e_c630-048.jpg
Notes: 7504918022 These are fantastic RED summer flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They are not common in our mountains, but I find them here and there. They've been flowering all the month of June and are just about done for the year. (Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: c704f_c701-011.jpg
Notes:
7504805424 I believe this broken WHITE flower head (with some webbing) is the nest of the caterpillar of a American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly on Fragrant Everlasting (Gnaphalium canescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

By chance, I happened to get my first photo of the adult butterfly on the same day.

File.Name: c704g_c703-033.jpg
Notes: 7504741594 This is a male Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into the new BLUE flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. I took me a minute to realize that it was committing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering under the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2012)

File.Name: c704h_c131-005.jpg
Notes:
7504643728 Red, white, and blue - not to mention green, yellow, brown, and almost black!

I took the notion to do red, white, and blue flowers today for the Fourth of July, with a couple of bookends from the archive. This is an old photo from the end of January, just before I renewed this Flickr account.

This little "true bug" in the order Hemiptera has an amazing raindow of colors! It also has 5 antennae segments and and large scutellum which places it in superfamily Pentatomoidea of that order. It's perched on a leaf of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden. Browsing at BugGuide, I believe it might be the Red-Cross Shield Bug (Elasmostethus cruciatus), though this one is more colorful than other photos I've seen on the web. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2012)

File.Name: c705a_c622-017.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a very black Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera. They have short wing covers and a protruding abdomen. This one is about to take wing from a flower head of Western Vervain (Verbena lasiostachys) in the Verbenaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

The family of Blister Beetles (Meloidae) includes the infamous "Spanish Fly" (Lytta vesicaroria), but I don't believe any of our California species has those nasty properties.

Today's photos are all from the recent archive - good photos that I never posted for one reason or another, usually more good photos!

I had a camera malfunction today - my tripod mount was finally stripped out and would no longer hold. I took camera and tripod to Samy's Camera in Santa Barbara hoping to find a tripod bolt that would bite, but no luck. So I got a quick release and (*gulp*) epoxied the plate to the bottom of my camera. The epoxy is curing tonight. I'll report tomorrow.

(I'm ready for a new camera anyway. I've had my Nikon D300 for 4 years and 150,000+ photos! That's what the shutter is rated for, though I hope for many more. It's got some quirks and sticky buttons. Maybe it's time for a full-frame D800, or wait for a D400?)
Locality: I'm ready for a new camera anyway.  I've had my Nikon D300 for 4 years and 150

Notes:
I believe this is a "stink bug" in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera - possibly a Red-shouldered Stink Bug in the genus Thyanta. It's feeding on a new berry of a native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) shrub in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

Look at its remarkable beak. I'm not sure of the anatomy, but I think the thin front stylets are sheathed by the sturdy rear labium. It's interesting to see them in separate pieces like this.

File.Name: c705c_c622-013.jpg
Notes: This light-colored Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera has such a quizzical expression on it's face! It's perched on a plant of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you look close, you can see a red mite on it's shoulder. It's an adult. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

File.Name: c705d_c622-019.jpg
Notes: As a contrast to my [Older] photo, here is a very light-colored Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera. It's still a nymph, without wings. I'm better at finding them than identifying them! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2012)

File.Name: c705e_c704-001.jpg
Notes: This is a very dark Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera that's perched on a down fence railing by the mailbox. I've posted a couple of photos of dark grasshoppers before (here and here), but this hopper doesn't quite look like either. I didn't notice any particular color on it's hind legs. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2012)

File.Name: c705f_c626-011.jpg
Notes: Here is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the woods. As I recall, it's a leaf of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella or R. californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You can clearly see the fly's "spurious vein" if you know what to look for. And behind the fly's wings, you can see its "halteres" - modified hind wings that apparently function as little gyroscopes as it flies. All flies have them. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2012)

File.Name: c705h_c626-027.jpg
Notes: This is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera perched on our steel bridge railing over the creek. I don't have much to say about them, except that we have quite a few different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2012)

File.Name: c708f_c708-003.jpg
Notes:
7531665508 This looks like a common Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) - but they are trash flies, and I found this critter in the woods, perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. And what gives with those brilliant red eyes and that yellow beard? (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2012)

I suspect this fly is the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria). See the images at: BugGuide.

If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. This fly is native to North America. It's larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feeds on already dead tissue.

It actually has some medical uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. Visit Wikipedia for a start, and follow the links. Interesting stuff! There's more info at Wikipedia.
Locality:
Cochliomyia macellaria). See the images at: BugGuide.

If I'm right

File.Name: c709e_c709-014.jpg
Notes: 7539965904 Here is a pretty brown Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's still a nymph, but this must be the last instar before it gets true wings and becomes a mature adult.
Locality: Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's still a nymph

File.Name: c709f_c709-017.jpg
Notes: 7539862654 This is a cute little Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera hanging on a stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. It's still a nymph without true wings. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2012)

File.Name: c709g_c707-023.jpg
Notes: 7539694950 This is *almost* a good photo showing the upper wings of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly in flight and showing the dog face - d'oh, if only that darned branch weren't there! It's quickly flying between flower heads of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. NatureShutterbug has had better luck! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2012)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: c709h_c708-017.jpg
Notes: 7539653584 Here's another photo showing how Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies pollinate the amazing summer flowers of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family. The butterfly can't help but be dusted with pollen as it hangs down from the lily flowers to get nectar. The female stigma of the flower starts straight down, but curves to the side as the flower matures to receive pollen - hopefully from other flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2012)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

File.Name: c710a_c710-029.jpg
Notes:
7547133980 This is a beautiful Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly perched with its tongue out on new acorns of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see the tails. I have no idea what it's doing in an oak, but here it is. Don't they prefer gooseberries (Ribes)? (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2012)

It was *very hot* today. I measured 98∞ in the shade on the porch when I got home from my hike today. It seemed to be too hot for insects, and certainly too hot for insect photographers! Tomorrow and the next day are expected to be more of the same. I'm trying not to think too much about that F-Word that rhymes with "ire".
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Lycaena arota; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Ribes
associatedTaxa: Lycaena arota; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Ribes

File.Name: c710b_c710-012.jpg
Notes: 7547078880 I guess this is a Harford's Sulphur (Colias harfordii aka C. alexandra harfordii) butterfly working new flowers of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden this morning. You can see from the outline that it has no a dogface, and it doesn't have the "double cell-spot" of the similar Orange Sulfur (C. eurytheme). (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2012)
CommonNames: Harford's Sulphur; Butterfly Bush; Orange Sulfur
ScientificName: Colias harfordii; C. alexandra harfordii; Buddleia davidii; C. eurytheme
associatedTaxa: Colias harfordii; C. alexandra harfordii; Buddleia davidii; C. eurytheme

File.Name: c710c_c710-010.jpg
Notes:
7546826974 This is a honeybee-like and honeybee-size Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) in the order Diptera stuffing its very large proboscis into a deep flower of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden this morning. Maybe it's the Drone Fly (Eristalis tenax)? (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2012)

If I got my ID right, the fly is not native, though it was introduced sometime before 1874. The maggots are interesting. BugGuide says:

"The larva of the Drone-Fly feeds on decaying organic material in stagnant water in small ponds, ditches and drains. Such water usually contains little or no oxygen and the larva breathes through the long thin tube that extends from its rear end to the surface of the water and that gives it its common name of ërat-tailed maggotí."

We have a "water garden" in an old tub that fits the bill perfectly!
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Butterfly Bush; Drone Fly
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Buddleia davidii; Eristalis tenax
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Buddleia davidii; Eristalis tenax

File.Name: c710d_c710-037.jpg
Notes: 7546708712 This is a pretty yellow "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera working a flower of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It has four antenna segments. I guess it's in the family Miridae. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 July 2012)
CommonNames: true bug; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: c710e_c710-027.jpg
Notes:
7546509274 These are remarkable new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. In time they will fill the underside of the leaf with red fuzz, but there are individual capsules inside. See my old photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2012)

I've been looking for these interesting galls, but they are right on time. Last year, I found them on July 10, and in 2010 I found them on July 16. I'm glad there is some regularity in nature, even in this drought year with 1/2 normal rainfall!
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c710f_c710-017.jpg
Notes: 7546466576 This is another odd "foliose gall" on native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe this is the gall of a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera - maybe the species Ceanothus Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi or kin? (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2012)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Ceanothus Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi

File.Name: c710g_c710-022.jpg
Notes: 7546399088 The brilliant seed plumes of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae) family are just starting to get their feather-like plumes. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2012)

File.Name: c710h_c710-032.jpg
Notes:
7546256510 New ripe berries of native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) shrub in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's now been renamed as Sambucus nigra ssp. caerulea in the Adoxaceae - if you really care. It took me years to get used to the transition from Munz to Jepson, and it will take years again! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2012)

The unripe berries are mildly poisonous, but the ripe ones are delicious - at least when cooked. They make the best pancake syrup ever. See my old photo of Breakfast on Santa Cruz Island!

File.Name: c711a_c711-002.jpg
Notes:
7554070704 Here are a mating pair of Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) hanging from a flower cluster of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden this morning. I think it's the first time I've ever seen them stand still - though a moment later they flew off in tandem. I guess they didn't like me watching, and I don't blame them! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2012)

It was another very hot day today, and breezy as well. Wind and heat are a bad combination. We have one more very hot day in the forecast. I'm trying not to think too much about that F-Word that rhymes with "ire".

I had a board meeting tonight for the The Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. I'm tired. Four photos was more than enough tonight!

File.Name: c711b_c711-012.jpg
Notes:
7554022590 I'll keep trying to get the perfect photo of the upper wings of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly. This one isn't it, but it does show its proboscis unwinding and some of the iridescent colors. These butterflies always close their wings when they visit flowers, so the only chance to show them is to catch them in the air - and even so, the sun has to be just right to bring out the iridescent colors. It's a great challenge!

This one is flying between flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. That's where most of the butterfly action is in these not summer days. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2012)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: c711c_c711-021.jpg
Notes:
7553894016 This spider sure has a "cat face" on it's abdomen!

But it's not the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) - that one is different. (See my old photo!) I guess this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. It has a little spider web house, with an orb-web attached. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2012)
CommonNames: Cat-Faced Spider; Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers

File.Name: c711d_c711-017.jpg
Notes: 7553783020 This is a handsome little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family by the creek today. Even when these spiders are looking off to the side, they are looking right at you with their Posterior Lateral Eyes (PLE)! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2012)
ScientificName: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Posterior Lateral Eyes
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Posterior Lateral Eyes

File.Name: c712a_c712-008.jpg
Notes:
7559426318 This is an immature Katydid in the family Tettigoniidae of the suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera. It's perched on a new Aster (Asteraceae) flower in the garden this morning. It was overcast today, perfect for capturing these delicate colors. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2012)

It may well be an Angle-wing Katydid in the genus Microcentrum - possibly the California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). These are gentle leaf-like creatures which do no harm.

We had an interesting weather day! It was overcast all day, and about 10 degrees cooler than yesterday. We've had a few raindrops from tropical monsoon moisture, and there's a chance of rain - and lightning - tonight!
Locality:
Microcentrum californicum). These are gentle leaf-like creatures which do no harm.

We had an interesting weather day! It was overcast all day
CommonNames: Katydid; Aster; Angle-wing Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Asteraceae; Microcentrum; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Asteraceae; Microcentrum; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: c712b_c712-016.jpg
Notes:
7559364360 This is a still-fresh flower of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are summer flowers, still hanging on in our drought-year summer. Just beautiful. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 July 2012)

See my [Older photo].

File.Name: c712c_c712-015.jpg
Notes: 7559247294 This is the "thistledown" of a spent flower of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are summer flowers, still hanging on in our drought-year summer. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 July 2012)

File.Name: c712d_c712-021.jpg
Notes: 7559222408 These are new flowers of Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. it really does have bluish stems - in a "blue dun" trout fly sort of way. These are classic "hummingbird flowers" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting today. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 July 2012)

File.Name: c712e_c712-028.jpg
Notes:
7559079130 This is the Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. I think that's a terrible common name, but you can see that it really does have bluish stems - in a "blue dun" trout fly sort of way. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 July 2012)

It's a classic "hummingbird flower" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting today. (And see NatureShutterbug's recent photo!) This straggly shrub has long flowering stalks with leaves in whorls of three. This is our other woody Keckiella (aka "Penstemon") in the woods. It's less common, but only because it prefers the high mountain tops. We also have the Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia).
Locality: aka "Penstemon") in the woods.  It's less common
ScientificName: Keckiella ternata; septentrionalis; Scrophulariaceae; Keckiella; Keckiella cordifolia
associatedTaxa: Keckiella ternata; septentrionalis; Scrophulariaceae; Keckiella; Keckiella cordifolia

File.Name: c712f_c712-031.jpg
Notes: 7559039046 These are handsome new dreadlock-like catkins of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's related to oaks and has acorns, but the erect catkins distinguish it. I like the corrugated leaves. It was very windy with approaching weather when I got this photo. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 July 2012)

File.Name: c712g_c712-023.jpg
Notes: 7558899736 This is a handsome clump of (I think) the Narrowleaf Swordfern (Polystichum imbricans) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. It's a "relict plant" from wetter times that manages to survive on north-facing slopes of our higher mountains and other cool places. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 July 2012)

File.Name: c712h_c712-003.jpg
Notes:
7558814714 After the party...

This is a spent native flower of Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) in the Onagraceae plant family - possibly a hybrid? The yellow flowers seem to last two nights, and then this. It was overcast today, and the subdued light was perfect. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2012)

File.Name: c713a_c713-026.jpg
Notes:
7565410082 This is another Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - the first I've seen this year in our canyon. It's hanging upside-down from a cluster of flowers of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. The butterflies are distinctive because the black on its forewings runs along the wing rather than across it. See my [Older] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2012)

Finally the non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) plants in the Apiaceae plant family that we've been saving - and trimming flowers - might be visited by caterpillars! It's a native butterfly that favors a non-native brood plant. It must have found other Apiacea plants before Fennel was introduced, but which ones?

We didn't get any more rain last night, but today was still hot - too hot for insects? This hot weather in a drought year has been taking a toll on insects and flowers.
Locality: Foeniculum vulgare) plants in the Apiaceae plant family that we've been saving - and trimming flowers - might be visited by caterpillars! It's a native butterfly that favors a non-native brood plant. It must have found other Apiacea plants before Fennel was introduced

(San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2012)
