File.Name: c713c_c713-008.jpg
Notes: 7565280298 This is another Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek. Sure, it's a tight crop. It's watching me! (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2012)

File.Name: c713d_c713-016.jpg
Notes: 7565244268 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae clutching her unfinished egg sack on a plant of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. The blue colors of her chelicerae are remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2012)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae

File.Name: c713e_c712-032.jpg
Notes:
7565125960 Here is a interesting little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2012)

It's interesting for several reasons. It really looks like a crab! It's missing its two hind legs on the left side - a real disability for these spiders that grab with their front legs and hang on. But most remarkable is that unmistakable relief face on its abdomen! Now why would a spider have a cartoon human face on its butt?

File.Name: c713f_c711-014.jpg
Notes: 7565055730 This is another immature Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera hanging on a stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek. I guess it's another Oedaleonotus phryneicus in the family of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2012)
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae

File.Name: c713g_c713-023.jpg
Notes: 7565013056 These are spiky berries of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The fruit turn red when they are ripe, and they are getting close. The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. The trick is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2012)

File.Name: c713h_c713-012.jpg
Notes:
7564936568 I guess I should apologize for making a weed look so good, but they are rather pretty before they go to seed.

This is non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) in the Asteraceae plant family, which is slowly invading our mountains. It's distantly related to our native "Cobweb Thistles" - like the Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) I posted yesterday. But it doesn't belong here. Cut and bag it when you see it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2012)
ScientificName: Cirsium vulgare; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; venustum
associatedTaxa: Cirsium vulgare; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; venustum

File.Name: c714a_c714-007.jpg
Notes:
7571737590 This is a remarkable Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera. It's "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden today. It's quite large, nearly an inch long. They have amazing patterned eyes, but you can only see a bit of them out in this photo. I'll keep trying for a better photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2012)

There is a Black Soldier Fly Blog!

File.Name: c714b_c714-010.jpg
Notes: 7571695126 Our Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly came back to the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. Their wings look like panels of stained glass. I hope they are laying lots lots of eggs in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2012)

File.Name: c714c_c714-005.jpg
Notes: 7571612920 Here is a tiny "grain of rice" Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a new flower of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden this morning. It's collecting pollen on its abdomen, so I reckon it's in the family Megachilidae. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2012)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: c714d_c714-018.jpg
Notes:
7571504018 This is a new flower head of Prince's Plume (Stanleya pinnata) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's growing on Monterey Shale above the Santa Ynez River. It's quite uncommon in our mountains. It was windy today, but I managed. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 July 2012)

I love the unusual summer flowers that grow along the river. Maybe it's because I used to be a school teacher, and spring always got busy - so summer was the main time I had for photos!

File.Name: c714e_c714-013.jpg
Notes: 7571445496 I'm pretty sure these are new summer flowers and buds of Cliff Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. These summer composites get confusing! The flowers are rather pretty. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 July 2012)

File.Name: c714f_c714-021.jpg
Notes: 7571350700 This is a new flower cluster of Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family, shot against the blue sky. There are lots of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) on the flowers. The plants have leaves like willows, but they have these composite flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 July 2012)
ScientificName: Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c714g_c714-024.jpg
Notes: 7571233194 This is a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a new flower head of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. The plants look very different near the coast - succulent and glabrous. They are the first of many summer-flowering composite shrubs with yellow-orange along the river. These summer flowers are important - they keep the insects alive! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 July 2012)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae

File.Name: c714h_c714-002.jpg
Notes: 7571186080 The elegant spring flowers of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family become elegant seed pods. See my photo of probably the same flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2012)

File.Name: c715a_c715-031.jpg
Notes:
7579466748 These are beautiful new flowers of native Dunn's Lobelia (Lobelia dunnii var. serrata) in the Campanulaceae plant family. It's uncommon, but only because it's habitat is so uncommon - perennial creeks that flow in our dry summer. I found this flower today where Lewis Creek empties into the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

I didn't plan to have all water plants today, but that's what happened. It was windy at home this morning as the coastal fog blew over the mountain passes, so I went down to the river again. Insects are still scarce - I guess the heat wave last week clobbered them. I got most of my photos today near the magical place where Lewis Creek comes in to the Santa Ynez River. I'll stealth the location and only show the trailhead.

File.Name: c715b_c715-032.jpg
Notes: 7579374114 This is a beautiful red flower of native Scarlet Monkeyflower (Mimulus cardinalis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing on the travertine basins at the bottom of Lewis Creek where it comes in to the Santa Ynez River. It reminds me of a baby bird with its mouth open for food. It grows along flowing summer creeks in our mountains, and it's as common as it's habitat - which is scarce, especially in this drought year! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c715c_c715-040.jpg
Notes: 7579197578 This is a pretty flower of the native California Thread-torch (Castilleja minor ssp. spiralis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's an Indian Paintbrush that favors wet places along the river. It's not common. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c715d_c715-034.jpg
Notes: 7579142172 These are new flowers of native Coastal Brookfoam (Boykinia occidentalis) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. It grows along flowing summer creeks in our mountains, and it's as common as it's habitat - which is scarce, especially in the drought year! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c715e_c715-036.jpg
Notes: 7579032918 This is a pretty native Maidenhair Fern (Adiantum capillus-veneria) in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing along the still-flowing Lewis Creek near the river. The water at the bottom of my photo looks thick and scummy. There's probably some sulfur in the water. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c715f_c715-038.jpg
Notes: 7578964618 I found this native Giant Chain Fern (Woodwardia fimbriata) in the Polypodiaceae plant family at the bottom of Lewis Creek today. It's our largest native fern, that only grows along permanent creeks and springs. Yeah, it's contrasty light - but that's how it is with these ferns that favor the shade. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c715g_c715-027.jpg
Notes: 7578913880 These are Cattails (genus Typha) in the Typhaceae plant family growing along the Santa Ynez River today - I don't know (or care) what kind they are. The thin male spikes appear above the fatter female spikes. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c715h_c715-039.jpg
Notes:
7578760006 These are Cattails (genus Typha) in the Typhaceae plant family growing on a place where the Santa Ynez River still has some flow in this drought year because Lewis Creek comes into the river here. Much of the river bed is dry, but these wet places are special. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

I went back to the river today because the marine layer fog spilled over the mountain passes and brought "fog winds" at home. It was still breezy at the river, but not so bad.

File.Name: c716a_c716-001.jpg
Notes:
7587800150 I noticed this Band-Tailed Pigeon (Columba fasciata) perched on our old CB radio antenna on the roof. I like to think it's the same fledgling bandtail we "rescued" last month. See my photos here and here. It's a wild pigeon, not a city bird. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2012)

It was gloomy and cold and windy all day today. The temperature barely got to 60 degrees. What a difference from the 100∞ temperatures last week!

File.Name: c716b_c715-024.jpg
Notes:
7587730124 This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into a native flower of Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

This beautiful large shrub that has an odd distribution. I find it in the foothills of Santa Barbara; along the Santa Ynez River at Paradise; and in the remote back country by Pool Rock - but not in between. It's interesting that all these places are arranged nearly north-south across our east-west mountains.
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae

File.Name: c716c_c715-007+011.jpg
Notes: 7587622152 Here are a pair of little Damselflies (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) by the river yesterday. That's the pretty blue male on top, and the duller female on the bottom - think about ducks. I have no idea if they are the same species, though I found them a few feet apart on a dry sandy flat along the river, and they have similar markings on their thorax. They both have amazing eyes. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c716d_c715-003.jpg
Notes: 7587588980 There are still a few beautiful flower heads of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) plant in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in a sandy place along the Santa Ynez River. This plant has an intense peppermint smell that is shocking when you walk through a patch of it without knowing the source. It seems to be uncommon. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c716e_c715-013.jpg
Notes:
7587439048 This photo should give you pause in coastal southern California!

This is real Great Basin Sagebrush aka Big Sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata ssp. tridentata) growing in the woodland on a dry river flat near the Santa Ynez River behind Santa Barbara instead of in the interior desert. It`s not common here. The few plants I`ve found here look very different from their desert kin, softer and whiter. This plant is beginning to flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c716f_c715-017.jpg
Notes:
7587370648 The native flowers of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family are starting to open along the river. It's a central California endemic related to Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis), but different. It's endemic, but quite common locally. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

Sara Allen Plummer Lemmon was one of the early naturalists who settled in Santa Barbara in the 1890s. It was Sara Lemmon who became the leader of the movement to have the California Poppy adopted as the California state flower.
CommonNames: Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush

File.Name: c716g_c714-014.jpg
Notes:
7587131614 This is an odd one. It appears to be a perennial Cliff Aster (Malacothrix saxatilis), definitely in the Asteraceae plant family. But those woolly white leaves have me stumped. It sure looks like the Carmel Valley Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. arachnoidea), but I'm not sure what it's doing here in Santa Barbara County. A knowledgeable friend suggests var.commutata which sounds more likely. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 July 2012)

The key choice in the Jepson Manual (TJM2) goes:
   6. Herbage tomentose................................ var. arachnoidea
   6' Herbage glabrous to +/- hairy................ var. commutata

It sure looks tomentose to me. On the other hand, the variety commutata is described as growing on crumbling shale along road cuts in the SCoR, which fits this plant perfectly.

I found this stumper plant on a dry Monterey Shale slope along the river road. I didn't pay it much attention at the time because I was distracted by the uncommon Prince's Plume (Stanleya pinnata) in the Brassicaceae just above it. (See my photo.)

Any ideas Lynn?
Locality:
TJM2) goes:
   6. Herbage tomentose................................ var. arachnoidea
   6' Herbage glabrous to +/- hairy................ var. commutata

It sure looks tomentose to me. On the other hand
CommonNames: Cliff Aster; Carmel Valley Malacothrix; Prince's Plume
ScientificName: Malacothrix saxatilis; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea; commutata; Stanleya pinnata; Brassicaceae
associatedTaxa: Malacothrix saxatilis; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea; commutata; Stanleya pinnata; Brassicaceae

File.Name: c716h_c715-012.jpg
Notes: 7587064824 This is a whole flowering plant of native Dove Weed aka Turkey Mullein (Eremocarpus setigerus) in the locally uncommon Euphorbiaceae plant family. This is nobody's favorite flower, but it is a native, and here it is growing on on a sandy flat by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)

File.Name: c717a_c717-010.jpg
Notes:
7594761758 This is another Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae perched on a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 July 2012)

It was gloomy and cold and windy again today. I drove up to the high place between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro hoping to get above the fog, but no luck. On the other hand, the fog was perfect light for this photo!
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: c717b_c717-011.jpg
Notes:
7594691040 It was cold and gloomy today. I found this large Bumble Bee (genus Bombus, family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) hiding head down in a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 July 2012)

The bee wasn't moving, but the plant sure was! It was windy today with "fog winds" - caused by the instability between the marine layer and the dry upper layer of the atmosphere, but wind is wind. I did the best I could.
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Bombus; Apidae; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Apidae; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: c717c_c717-025.jpg
Notes:
7594605726 Even the spent flowers are beautiful!

This is a spent flower of native summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. This species is listed as "rare, threatened or endangered in California and elsewhere" and "fairly endangered in California" (CNPS: 1B.2). It's doing fine in our mountains, even in this drought year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 July 2012)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: c717d_c717-005.jpg
Notes: 7594561450 These are the very glandular new composite flowers of Coast Tarweed (Madia sativa) in the Asteraceae plant family. The gloomy weather we've been having is perfect for getting photos of these composite flowers that normally close when the sun hits them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2012)

File.Name: c717e_c717-001.jpg
Notes:
7594467762 Yipes, we're infested!

These are native Dusky-Footed Wood Rats (Neotoma fuscipes) eating the spilled bird seed under our feeder today. I reckon that's mama on the left and one of her young on the right. They are cute - but this is too close to home! Sorry birds, no more food for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2012)

Wood rats belong in the woods. They build large houses out of sticks, like beaver "lodges" in the woods. These rats are also called "pack rats" because they like to steal shiny things to add to their nests, at least that's the myth. In one of the classic Donald Duck comics (#52, 1957), Carl Barks tells the story of "The Lost Peg Leg Mine" - where pack rats are the key to the story!

File.Name: c717f_c717-009.jpg
Notes: 7594397064 I believe this is the gall of the Willow Rosette Gall Midge (Rabdophaga salicisbrassicoides) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. It's growing on an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2012)
CommonNames: Willow Rosette Gall Midge; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: Rabdophaga salicisbrassicoides; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Rabdophaga salicisbrassicoides; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: c717g_c715-033.jpg
Notes: 7594287362 I believe these are the galls of the Willow Apple Gall Sawfly (Potania californica) in the family Tenthredinidae of the order Hymenoptera. I guess it's on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family. You can see exit holes where the critters got out. (Or did some other critter get in?) (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)
CommonNames: Willow Apple Gall Sawfly; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: Potania californica; Tenthredinidae; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Potania californica; Tenthredinidae; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: c717h_c715-030.jpg
Notes: 7594197148 These are leaves of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the river. There is a lumpy gall on the petiole of the central leaf that I believe is caused by the Poplar Petiole Gall Aphid (Pemphigus populitransversus, Aphididae, Homoptera). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2012)
CommonNames: Fremont Cottonwood; Poplar Petiole Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Populus fremontii; Salicaceae; Pemphigus populitransversus; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Populus fremontii; Salicaceae; Pemphigus populitransversus; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: c718a_c718-004.jpg
Notes:
7600966756 This is a beautiful little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess it's family Megachilidae - I can see it cleaning the pollen brushes on its abdomen in another photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2012)

I really confused by the mouthparts of these little bees. I think I see two mandibles in front of its eyes - and a round thing between them, a tongue-like proboscis, and a curled thing down below its head!

It was cloudy again today, but different from the gloomy marine layer clouds we've had for the last couple of days. These are real clouds, the remnants of once-hurricane Fabio. We had a few drops of real summer rain this afternoon. It smells great and feels so sensuous!
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: c718b_c718-020.jpg
Notes: 7600937794 Here is another fantastic nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera perched on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Isn't it time for these critters to grow up! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2012)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: c718c_c718-015.jpg
Notes:
7600800446 This is another photo of the tiny grasshopper in my previous photo.

I guess from the small size and look that this is another Monkey Grasshopper in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera - probably a male Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica). It's perched on a dead oak leaf in the woods. It has remarkable gold trim on its shoulders. I found this in dim light and only had time for a few quick photos before it hopped away, none of them great. Compare this photo of a nymph. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2012)

File.Name: c718d_c718-017.jpg
Notes: 7600689318 I guess from the small size and look that this is another Monkey Grasshopper in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera - probably a male Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica). It's perched on a dead oak leaf in the woods. It has remarkable gold trim on its shoulders. I found this in dim light and only had time for a few quick photos before it hopped away, none of them great. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2012)

File.Name: c718e_c718-013.jpg
Notes: 7600614028 This is looking close at a brilliant new flower of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium - aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The white pollen grains are remarkable, like little barley corns in a soup. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2012)
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: c718f_c717-018.jpg
Notes: 7600488692 Here are brilliant new flowers of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. canum - aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. You can see another branch of the same plant in the background, with whitish linear leaves, which marks the subspecies. Other plants have wider greenish leaves and are ssp. angustifolium. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 July 2012)
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; angustifolium
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; angustifolium

File.Name: c718g_c717-013.jpg
Notes: 7600395440 These are new summer flowers of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. The small white flowers are hard to find in the green leafy bracts, and they never open much more than this. But it's a tough plant that flowers during the dry times, and you have to respect it. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 July 2012)

File.Name: c718h_c717-021.jpg
Notes: 7600329600 These are the pale white berries of native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Despite the lack of rain this year, they seem to be doing well. Galium is an odd group of plants, and it's hard to keep the many local species straight. Some have berries and others have nutlets. It's one of my favorite genera of local plants. I have a photo of the flowers here. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 July 2012)

File.Name: c719a_c719-041.jpg
Notes:
7610508478 This is a beautiful female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly rolling up its proboscis after working some of the last flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in the canyon. You can see how sparkly the eye-spots on its hindwing are. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2012)

It's interesting - this year the usually common Acmon Blues (Plebejus acmon) have been scarce, and the Marine Blues (Leptotes marina) have been more common. Every year is different.

Our power went out yesterday from 1 pm to after 11 pm. No computer, no internet, no TV - just a quiet night eating popcorn and reading Nero Wolfe by Coleman lamp. Now that I think about it, that's not too bad!
Locality:
Leptotes marina) have been more common. Every year is different.

Our power went out yesterday from 1 pm to after 11 pm. No computer
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Deerweed; Acmon Blues; Marine Blues
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Plebejus acmon; Leptotes marina
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Plebejus acmon; Leptotes marina

File.Name: c719b_c719-020.jpg
Notes:
7610473510 Here is another pretty little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working some of the last flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in the canyon. You can see some sparckles in the eye-spot on its hindwing. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2012)

It's interesting - this year the Marine Blues have been common, and the usually common Acmon Blues (Plebejus acmon) have been scarce - but see my [Newer] photo.
CommonNames: Marine Blue; Deerweed; Marine Blues; Acmon Blues
ScientificName: Leptotes marina; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Plebejus acmon

File.Name: c720a_c720-023.jpg
Notes:
7612769340 Whenever you find a butterfly resting too-still on a plant, look closer!

This is a non-native Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly resting too-still on a plant of non-native Catnip aka Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria) in the (Lamiaceae) plant family.

Look in the upper-right corner, and you can see why. A little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera has grabbed it and is sucking it dry. It's probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). If you can't spot it, see my [Older] photo and then come back. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2012)

I probably won't be able to post photos tomorrow. We are heading out to Hollister Ranch to meet with a few good old friends for a BBQ and beach walk. This is much better that a boring ol' power outage!
CommonNames: Cabbage White; Catnip; Cat Mint; Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Pieris rapae; Nepeta cataria; Lamiaceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica
associatedTaxa: Pieris rapae; Nepeta cataria; Lamiaceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: c720b_c720-017.jpg
Notes: 7612705248 This is a Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera on the right. It's probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's perched on new flowers of non-native Catnip aka Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria) in the (Lamiaceae) plant family. Look - its captured a much larger Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly! See my [Newer] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2012)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Catnip; Cat Mint; Cabbage White
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Nepeta cataria; Lamiaceae; Pieris rapae
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Nepeta cataria; Lamiaceae; Pieris rapae

File.Name: c720c_c719-046.jpg
Notes:
7612632098 I wouldn't want to meet this predator in a movie theater - at least not tonight.

This is a perfectly camouflaged Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) on a new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). They are only about 1/4 inch long, but they can bring down much larger prey. See my [Newer] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2012)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; California Buckwheat; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Phymata pacifica
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: c720d_c720-004.jpg
Notes:
7612586866 Hummingbirds don't just visit classic hummingbird flowers!

This female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) is working a morning flower of Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) in the Onagraceae plant family - or possibly a hybrid? Well, why not? The flower does have a very deep inferior ovary. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2012)
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Oenothera elata; hookeri; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Calypte anna; Oenothera elata; hookeri; Onagraceae

File.Name: c720e_c719-008.jpg
Notes: 7612505702 Here is yet another Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) in the garden, getting a perfect bull's-eye on a flower of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. It's not a native shrub, but the native butterflies (and moths) sure like it. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2012)

File.Name: c720f_c720-007.jpg
Notes:
7612387374 This is another Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) in the garden, working an unusual (for them) flower of (I believe) Capsicum pubescens in the Solanaceae plant family - aka Chile Manzano or Rocoto. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2012)

The plant is large and growing lots of flowers, but I haven't seen any chilies yet. Darn, our garden is getting shaded out by the oak trees.

File.Name: c720g_c719-009.jpg
Notes: 7612358686 This is a handsome little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. They have fantastic eyes. This one seems to have a safety line for it's next jump. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2012)

File.Name: c720h_c719-050.jpg
Notes:
7612304782 There's a "golden moment" during sunset that brings out the colors like nothing else!

These are native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family in the woods last night, about 8:08 pm. It only lasts for a minute. The dark has already got to the California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family in front. I got this photo balancing my camera on a pile of books from our porch. There was some wind. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2012)
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: c721a_c721-036.jpg
Notes:
7624807448 These are the complicated new flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. They are native, but kind of weedy at the coast and the valley. There are interesting insects associated with these flowers, but I didn't find any of them. (Little Drake's, Hollister Ranch, 21 July 2012)

The humble milkweeds are really our most advanced flowers, except maybe the orchids. They have sepals and petals like most flowers, but they also have another ring of unusual structures above them called the corona. See my photo with explanation.

We went out to the fabulous beaches on Hollister Ranch - aka "The Ranch" to surfers - today for a reunion of past teachers at Dunn Middle School. Great place, great company, great time!

File.Name: c721b_c721-046.jpg
Notes:
7624625176 These are new flowers of native Sticky Baccharis (Baccharis douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. Note the tiny black ants. (Little Drake's, Hollister Ranch, 21 July 2012)

We have at least three other species of Baccharis in our mountains - Ms. Plummer's Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae), Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia), and the important Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis), which isn't flowering yet.
CommonNames: Sticky Baccharis; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Mule Fat; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Baccharis douglasii; Asteraceae; Baccharis; Baccharis plummerae; Baccharis salicifolia; Baccharis pilularis
associatedTaxa: Baccharis douglasii; Asteraceae; Baccharis; Baccharis plummerae; Baccharis salicifolia; Baccharis pilularis

File.Name: c721c_c721-031.jpg
Notes: 7624574818 These are attractive new flower heads of native Sneezeweed aka Rosilla (Helenium puberulum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing above the high-tide line on the beach. (Little Drake's, Hollister Ranch, 21 July 2012)

File.Name: c721d_c721-016.jpg
Notes: 7624466542 This is new growth from a buried runner of Salt Grass (Distichlis spicata) in the Poacea plant family. It's a wide-ranging native grass that can take salt - at the beach or the desert. (Little Drake's, Hollister Ranch, 21 July 2012)

File.Name: c722a_c721-014.jpg
Notes:
7626395282 New butterflies first...

This is a new Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly working the almost-faded flowers of English Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family in our garden. Despite their common name, these butterflies are never common in our mountains, but I find them every year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2012)

File.Name: c722b_c722-010.jpg
Notes:
7626311566 This a muscular Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) with its spider prey. I got this photo this morning on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2012)

The wasps flick their wings constantly. They are often iridescent. I see them often, but they rarely stop at flowers, so they are hard to photograph. This is a lucky shot - the kind of luck that comes from always having camera ready and knowing what you have!

File.Name: c722c_c722-005.jpg
Notes:
7626125114 I'm pretty sure this is a Closterocoris amoenus "true bug" in the family Miridae of the order Hemiptera. It's perched on an open flower of Squash (Cucurbita) in the garden this morning. The nymphs are remarkable ant-mimics. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2012)

Look at its remarkable beak. I'm not sure of the anatomy, but I think the thin front stylets are sheathed by the sturdy rear labium. It's interesting to see them in separate pieces like this.
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Hemiptera; Cucurbita; beak; stylets; labium
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Hemiptera; Cucurbita; beak; stylets; labium

File.Name: c722d_c722-002.jpg
Notes:
7626042112 I believe this is a Drone Fly (Eristalis tenax) in the garden this morning. It's a honeybee-like and honeybee-size Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) in the order Diptera. They blow up their face to make the large proboscis that can fit into the deep flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii). If you look closer, you can see a normal proboscis inside a fleshy sack. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2012)

My, what furry eyeballs you have!
CommonNames: Drone Fly; Syrphid Flower Fly; Butterfly Bush
ScientificName: Eristalis tenax; Syrphidae; Diptera; Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Eristalis tenax; Syrphidae; Diptera; Buddleia davidii

File.Name: c722e_c720-026.jpg
Notes:
7625914896 "Don't chew your nails!"

This is a small female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) cleaning its toes in the garden. Sure it's a lucky capture - the kind of luck that comes from always having camera ready! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2012)

This is another 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo, taken with my 105 mm macro lens. It's about the only lens I ever mount these days.

File.Name: c722f_c722-009.jpg
Notes:
7625839694 This is a beautiful little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) trying to warm up on a new Aster (as in genus Aster) flower in the Asteraceae plant family this morning. The first light is just hitting it - at about 10 am. Our garden is slowly getting shaded out by the oak trees. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2012)

I guess it's in the family Megachilidae - I can see pollen brushes on its abdomen. Did you notice the tiny spider in the lower-right corner? Did you notice the tiny spider in the lower-right corner?
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Aster; Asteraceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Aster; Asteraceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: c722g_c719-004.jpg
Notes:
7625757774 This is a large Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of (I think) native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. They keep flying until they wear their wings down to nubs. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2012)

These native plants like summer watering!
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: c722h_c721-009.jpg
Notes: 7625671542 These are bristly new seeds of native Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Look at the plant stem on the right that gets to the bottom of the photo, and you can see the characteristic "four-whorl" of leaves. This plant has dry nutlets, unlike other species of the genus that have berries. (See this photo). It's a little shrub. Galium sure is an interesting genus! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2012)

File.Name: c723a_c723-015.jpg
Notes: 7633924828 Here is another fantastic summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. Everything comes in three's with these flowers - three sepals, three petals, three+three stamens, and three arms on the pistil. They are still going in the mountains, despite the drought year - and they haven't started to flower yet in our canyon. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 July 2012)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: c723b_c723-012.jpg
Notes:
7633852608 This is a "yellow-form" Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's perched on a sepal of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 July 2012)

The yellow color is remarkable, but maybe it was working the yellow flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family nearby. These critters are color-shifters.
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Weed's Mariposa Lily; California Goldenrod
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Solidago californica; Asteraceae

File.Name: c723c_c723-023.jpg
Notes: 7633758712 Here is a clutch of (I think) small "true bugs" in the insect order Hemiptera on seed heads of non-native Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can make out a proboscis on the top-most critter. I hope they get a taste for it - it's a nasty weed in our mountains! (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2012)

File.Name: c723d_c719-026.jpg
Notes: 7633660018 This is a perfect little Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera hanging on a stem of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family that is getting ready to flower. I first thought Oedaleonotus phryneicus in the family of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae), but Alice says the wings are wrong. She suggests Melanoplus cinereus cyanipes in the same family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2012)
CommonNames: Short-horned Grasshopper; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Spur-throated Grasshoppers
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae; Melanoplus cinereus cyanipes
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae; Melanoplus cinereus cyanipes

File.Name: c723e_c723-001.jpg
Notes: 7633614968 I guess this is a worn Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly - you can barely make out the white-fringe on its hindwings. It's resting on a blackberry leaf on the wild edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2012)

File.Name: c723f_c720-010.jpg
Notes:
7633574332 How quickly they fade...

This is another California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden. Compare this photo that shows the blue on its forewings. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2012)

File.Name: c723g_c719-003.jpg
Notes: 7633479732 This is a perfect American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly perched on a fuzzy leaf of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in our garden. Despite the four eye-spots on top of the hindwings, it only shows two spots below. The small white spot in the first orange cell of the forewing is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2012)

File.Name: c723h_c720-021.jpg
Notes:
7633432656 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2012)

The composite flowers seem to be our best butterfly flowers.

File.Name: c724a_c724-008.jpg
Notes:
7641142198 This is another photo of the remarkable tiny Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera in my [Older photo].

Now it looks less like its blowing hot jet fuel from its abdomen, and more like it has a bunch of fiber optic material stuck up its *uh* abdomen! I found it this morning on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess it's an Issid Planthopper (Issidae) - maybe genus Thionia? (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2012)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The bug is about 1536 pixels from head to the tip of its bristles, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x1526/4288 = 8.45 mm, about 1/3 inch - and the actual insect is only 620 pixels = 3.4 mm, about 1/8 inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!

I keep thinking that insects are getting scarce in the summer woods, but I keep finding new ones! I'm taking it one day at a time. We're getting ready to head north to Oregon for a few weeks, hopefully within the next week. I'll keep looking for new insects, but I'll be a bit distracted.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Issidae; Thionia
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Issidae; Thionia

File.Name: c724b_c724-005.jpg
Notes: 7641029082 This tiny Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera looks like its blowing hot jet fuel from its *uh* abdomen! I found it this morning on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess it's an Issid Planthopper (Issidae) - maybe genus Thionia? See my [Newer] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2012)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Issidae) - maybe genus Thionia
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Issidae) - maybe genus Thionia

File.Name: c724c_c724-004.jpg
Notes: 7640943100 This is a closer photo of the dead Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae) in the insect order Neuroptera that I found this morning. Its lifeless eye is amazing. I guess that the 6-sided star in its eye is a diffraction pattern from the many 6-sided "ommatidia" simple-eyes that make up the compound eyes of this insect. The sun must have been just right. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2012)

File.Name: c724d_c724-001.jpg
Notes: 7640852608 I found this unfortunate Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae) in the insect order Neuroptera dead on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. These critters have been common on our window screens at night. It's pretty enough, but look at that star on its eye! See my [Newer] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2012)

File.Name: c724e_c724-012.jpg
Notes:
7640724774 Yoga for flies?
"I could do that but I don't want to..."

This is a large bumblebee-like fly (Diptera) grooming its wings with its hind legs, on a leaf of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. I guess its a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae), maybe genus Merodon? (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2012)
CommonNames: fly; Butterfly Bush; Syrphid Flower Fly

File.Name: c724f_c723-024.jpg
Notes:
7640604092 This is a handsome little black and white Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of (I guess) a flowering Aloe plant in the Aloaceae or Asphodeloideae or Xanthorrhoeaceae plant family, depending on your authority. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2012)

This little bee has a regular circuit in the garden between the Aloe plants near the porch, the Aloe plants in the garden, and the Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria, Lamiaceae) flowers. It has a curious way of glomming onto the flower. It moves fast and is hard to photograph. I'll keep trying for a better photo.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Aloaceae; Asphodeloideae; Xanthorrhoeaceae; Nepeta cataria
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Aloaceae; Asphodeloideae; Xanthorrhoeaceae; Nepeta cataria

File.Name: c724g_c724-013.jpg
Notes:
7640481340 This little Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) has captured a tiny fly (Diptera) and brought it back to its perch in a spent flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2012)

This may well be the same kind of spider that the Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) dragged off in my [Previous photo.
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; fly; Canyon Sunflower; Spider Wasp
ScientificName: Araneidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c724h_c724-016.jpg
Notes:
7640440672 This is the tent of little web-spinner Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly caterpillars on a flowering branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. You can see a few tiny caterpillars on the web. They may kill a branch or two of the plants, but they do not do too much damage. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2012)

The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year!

File.Name: c725a_c725-027.jpg
Notes:
7647814640 Here is a mama Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) with her fawn by the river today. She was munching the non-native White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) in the Fabaceae plant family, which has been growing luxuriously this year - 8 to 10 feet tall! You have to beat a path through these weeds to get to the river. It's odd that this plant is doing so well in a drought year, but here it is. It wasn't nearly this thick last year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)

There are reports that wilted Sweet Clover contains coumarin which is used as a blood thinner. The rat poison Warfarin is based on this toxin, as are some blood thinner medications.

It was cool and breezy on the mountain today, so I went down to the river. *Sigh* it was breezy there as well, but I did get some photos.
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae; coumarin; Warfarin
associatedTaxa: Odocoileus hemionus; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae; coumarin; Warfarin

File.Name: c725b_c725-014.jpg
Notes: 7647757262 Here is a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of native California Loosestrife (Lythrum californicum) in the Lythraceae plant family near the river today. The flowers are small, but pretty. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lythrum californicum; Lythraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lythrum californicum; Lythraceae

File.Name: c725c_c725-019.jpg
Notes: 7647647130 I believe these are the pretty little flowers of Water Speedwell (Veronica anagallis-aquatica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's a non-native weed that thrives in the water and mud as the river dries up. It's common. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)

File.Name: c725d_c725-011.jpg
Notes:
7647590838 These are new flowers of the native composite Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. These plants look very different from the California Goldenrod (S. californica) that grows in our mountains. See this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)

There are lots of these yellow-flowered composite that grow along the river and the dry flood plain. The season is just starting!
ScientificName: Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae; S. californica
associatedTaxa: Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae; S. californica

File.Name: c725e_c725-015.jpg
Notes: 7647496574 I believe these are the leaf galls of the Willow Gall Mite (Aculops tetanothrix) in the family Eriophyidae in the order Acarina. They are growing on the leaves of some kind of Willow (genus Salix) in the Salicaceae plant family by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)
ScientificName: Aculops tetanothrix; Eriophyidae; Acarina; Salix; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Aculops tetanothrix; Eriophyidae; Acarina; Salix; Salicaceae

File.Name: c725f_c725-032.jpg
Notes: 7647426894 These are new flowers of Creek Clematis aka Yerba de Chivato (Clematis ligusticifolia) in the Ranunculaceae) plant family. The flowers are smaller than the earlier-flowering Chaparral Clematis (C. lasiantha), and they flower later in the year. They usually have 5 or 7 leaflets on the stems. I often find it hanging down from trees, but this one was growing low over shrubs by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)
CommonNames: Creek Clematis; Yerba de Chivato; Chaparral Clematis

File.Name: c725g_c725-001.jpg
Notes: 7647258720 This is a hard plant to photograph - the leaves are withered, and the flowers are widely spaced. I believe it is the native Slender Buckwheat (Eriogonum gracile) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's growing on a dry, sandy flat along the river, about a foot tall. Yes, this plant is pretty obscure, but here it is where it ought to be! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)

File.Name: c725h_c725-004.jpg
Notes:
7647200552 This is a thoroughly dried out black-spored mushroom growing on the dry sandy flood plain of the Santa Ynez River. I think it might be the Gastroid Coprinus (Montagnea arenarius), but I'm not sure. If so, there is a small volva at the base of the the stem - and maybe it's visible in this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)

It seems paradoxical, but the dry flood plane of the river - which is sometimes under water - is actually the driest, most desert-like habitat we have. I really enjoy snooping around there seeing what I can find. Julie jokes that I can find mushrooms anywhere - she might be right!

File.Name: c726a_c726-027.jpg
Notes:
7653847284 This is one of our most interesting insect galls, though it doesn't have any visible structure like many galls. I believe this is the gall (or at least a sign of the gall) of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) - or kin - in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The gall wasp larvae is making changes in the oak's acorn to produce real honey under pressure that will ultimately force the immature acorn (and the wasp larvae?) from its cup. The oak is a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family.

"Scrub Oak honey" - sounds like an oxymoron if you've ever nibbled an acorn, but here it is! It really does taste sweet. The little bee/wasp (Hymenoptera) is interested. I've been noticing lots of honey bees in the oak trees lately, and I'll bet this is why. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)
CommonNames: Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c726b_c726-011.jpg
Notes: 7653749234 I call this a "Lumpy Gall" on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It always causes a bend in the stem. It's not listed in my "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, 2006) and I don't know what causes it. Now look closer at the old catkins that are attached. You can see little exit holes. The catkins have been galled too! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Field Guide to Plant Galls

File.Name: c726c_c726-021.jpg
Notes: 7653711368 Here is the beautiful gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls - all galls - have been scarce this year, but the "summer gall season" is just beginning. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: c726d_c726-025.jpg
Notes: 7653618662 At the top are insect galls on a seed head of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. They are open at the top, like little volcanoes erupting from the plant. They are not listed in my book on plant galls, and I don't know whose galls they are. I see similar galls on the seed heads of Eriodictyon crassifolium down by the river. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)

File.Name: c726e_c726-036.jpg
Notes: 7653596310 This is looking face-to-face at a little Treehopper (family Membracidae of the insect order Homoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen. They have remarkable eyes, like all jumpers that have to see where they're jumping. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)

File.Name: c726f_c726-038.jpg
Notes: 7653550412 This is a little Treehopper (family Membracidae of the insect order Homoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen. They have remarkable eyes, like all jumpers that have to see where they're jumping. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)

File.Name: c726g_c726-022.jpg
Notes: 7653513358 This is another little Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera perched on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's a late-instar hopper, but not quite an adult yet. It has wonderful eyes! I won't try to guess what it is - I'm usually wrong. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)

File.Name: c726h_c726-039.jpg
Notes: 7653428302 This is a another beautiful red flower of native Scarlet Monkeyflower (Mimulus cardinalis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's growing in our "water garden" - just an old fiberglass tub filled with water and plants, not to mention frogs and worms. I can't remember where we collected it, probably rescued from a slide along some creek. It's been flowering every year for many years. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)

File.Name: c727a_c727-016.jpg
Notes:
7659651192 This is a large bumblebee-size Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae of the order Diptera on non-native flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. When I first saw them years ago, I thought they were Horse Flies and wondered why they didn't try to bite me! But they are really Syrphid Flower Flies - you can see the "spurious vein" in their wings between R4+5 and M1+2. See my [Older] photo for a better view of its remarkable proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2012)

It was breezy today and we had errands in town. Just a few photos.
CommonNames: Mexican Cactus Fly; Butterfly Bush; Horse Flies; Syrphid Flower Flies
ScientificName: Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae; Diptera; Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae; Diptera; Buddleia davidii

File.Name: c727b_c727-006.jpg
Notes: 7659596940 This is a large bumblebee-size fly (Diptera) on flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. I'm sure it's a Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae of the order Diptera. It's quite large - horse fly-size, but it's definitely not a horse fly. This photo isn't quite sharp - it was windy this morning - but I like it because you can see it's remarkable proboscis - with "forked-tongue" tips! (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2012)
CommonNames: fly; Butterfly Bush; Mexican Cactus Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Buddleia davidii; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Buddleia davidii; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: c727c_b718-013.jpg
Notes:
7659357064 "Have you seen this missing beetle?"

I call this a "Mid-summer Mugwort Beetle", since I always find about this time of year on the Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plants in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our creek. It's a good-sized beetle in the order Coleoptera, maybe an inch (2.5 cm) long. It looks like a puppy dog! But this year they are missing - so far. I'm posting this photo to break the jinx - I expect to find one tomorrow! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2011)

Alice found it, as see so often does - it's a Willow Borer Xylotrechus insignis in the family of Longhorned Beetles (Cerambycidae). And maybe that explains why they are missing this year - the neighbors did some work on the creek last year to clear out the bridge - including removing some willow trees.

I've found them before (with photo links) - always in midsummer, for example on:
   9 July 2011
   15 July 2010
   26 June 2009
   18 July 2008

It sure looks like a pattern, but this year they are absent, so far. It's like missing a friend...
Locality: with photo links) - always in midsummer
CommonNames: Mid-summer Mugwort Beetle; Mugwort; beetle; Willow Borer; Longhorned Beetles
ScientificName: Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Coleoptera; Xylotrechus insignis; Cerambycidae
associatedTaxa: Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Coleoptera; Xylotrechus insignis; Cerambycidae

File.Name: c727d_c726-018.jpg
Notes: 7659278350 These are wonderful new seed plumes of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae) family. They look quite gray with the sun in front, but they turn brilliant white with the sun behind. This photo is in between, with the sun over my shoulder. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)

File.Name: c727e_c726-033.jpg
Notes: 7659201586 Here is a healthy flowering clump of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family - our largest grass. That's a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the background. These grasses are quite tall, 5-7 feet - not to be confused with the non-native Giant Reed (Arundo donax) that favors the beaches and gets even larger. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)
CommonNames: Giant Rye Grass; Coulter Pine; Giant Reed
ScientificName: Leymus condensatus; Poaceae; Pinus coulteri; Arundo donax
associatedTaxa: Leymus condensatus; Poaceae; Pinus coulteri; Arundo donax

File.Name: c727f_c726-006.jpg
Notes:
7659144610 These are new galls on leaves of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know what insect causes them, and they are not listed in my book on "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, 2006). There's a different "lumpy gall" in the background - another gall that's not in the book. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)

Quercus chrysolepis is considered an "Intermediate Oak" - separate from the "Black Oaks" like Coast Live Oak and the "White Oaks" like Scrub Oak. For some reason, they are having a good year for galls, but the other oaks not.
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Field Guide to Plant Galls; Coast Live Oak; Scrub Oak

File.Name: c727g_c726-012.jpg
Notes: 7659068286 This is a new acorn of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. We have a number of different oaks in our mountains, and all their acorns are distinct. For example, these have smooth scales like Coast Live Oak, but they are much squatter. See my [Older] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2012)
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: c727h_c727-002.jpg
Notes:
7659004510 These are new acorns on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family in morning light today. They are small for this time of year, but well-formed. Not a bad crop for a drought year! (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2012)

We have a number of different oaks in our mountains, and all their acorns are distinct. For example, these have smooth scales, while the Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in my [Older] photo has lumpy acorns.

Many - maybe most - of the new acorns will fail to develop. The honey-forming galls in my [Older] photo will cause many of them to turn black and drop prematurely.

File.Name: c728a_c728-028.jpg
Notes:
7665695268 This is another goofy-looking Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera. I found a pair of them today on foliage of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)

The last one of these critters I found was quite gray, and this one is quite orange. I'll admit - I'm confused. I'm usually confused by one thing or another!
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c728b_c728-003.jpg
Notes: 7665632210 This is another beautiful tiny Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera. It looks like its blowing hot jet fuel from its *ah* abdomen! I found it this morning on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess it's the nymph of an Issid Planthopper (Issidae) - maybe genus Thionia? (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Issidae; Thionia
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Issidae; Thionia

File.Name: c728c_c728-009.jpg
Notes: 7665568168 The male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies get all the glory, but the females are quite beautiful too. Look at the pink colors on its head and feet! This one was "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)

File.Name: c728d_c728-014.jpg
Notes: 7665526286 The native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family is just about done for the year, but this little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly has moved over to the non-native White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) in the Fabaceae plant family. Here it is, making loops with its proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)
CommonNames: Deerweed; Marine Blue; White Sweet Clover
ScientificName: Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Leptotes marina; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Leptotes marina; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae

File.Name: c728e_c728-006.jpg
Notes:
7665443478 This is a perfect little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly making loops with its proboscis on flowers of non-native flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)

Skippers have been scarce this year. They mostly look like this Umber Skipper (Poanes melane), and not much else. Of course the little differences are important in a drought year.
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Buddleia davidii; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Buddleia davidii; Poanes melane

File.Name: c728f_c728-026.jpg
Notes:
7665384040 Be careful little wing, some of these summer flowers have fangs!

This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae has taken over one of the last flower heads of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)

These are the plants growing in full sun where our neighbor did some clearing last year. We have another colony in the shade by the creek that is just starting to flower. I love these summer flowers that just keep coming, despite the drought.
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: c728g_c728-020.jpg
Notes: 7665333586 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae perched next to a flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Look close - the spider is munching a little wasp as prey. These spiders have the image of a demon face on their backs! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Woolly Blue Curls; wasp
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: c728h_c728-024.jpg
Notes:
7665245114 This is another fantastic summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)

This is the first flower I've seen in our canyon on San Marcos Pass. In the spring the flowers happen here first and move up the mountains, but in the summer they flower first in the high mountains, and move down to the pass. Maybe it's because of our "fog days" - the marine layer influence?
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: c729a_c729-004.jpg
Notes:
7673735852 This is a beautiful Closterocoris amoenus "true bug" in the family Miridae of the order Hemiptera. I found it perched this morning on an open flower of Capsicum pubescens in the Solanaceae plant family - aka Chile Manzano or Rocoto. You can see its long proboscis folded under its head. The nymphs are remarkable ant-mimics. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2012)

I'm getting distracted with our upcoming trip to Oregon. We hope to leave on Wednesday or Thursday. I'll get more distracted as the day draws closer!
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Hemiptera; Capsicum pubescens; Solanaceae
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Hemiptera; Capsicum pubescens; Solanaceae

File.Name: c729b_c729-011.jpg
Notes: 7673656130 I noticed this Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) perched too-still on a flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family today. Then I saw the Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). Look to the left of the throat of the bee, and look for the orange antenna. These are wonderful - and frightening - predators. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2012)
CommonNames: Honey Bee; California Buckwheat; Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: c729c_c729-001.jpg
Notes: 7673541088 I can't resist another photo of (I think) a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working flowers of non-native flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. Or is it a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly? Sometimes the pales get more yellow. I don't really care. I wonder if they realize how beautiful they are! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2012)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Butterfly Bush; Western Tiger Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Buddleia davidii; Papilio rutulus
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Buddleia davidii; Papilio rutulus

File.Name: c729d_c728-002.jpg
Notes:
7673313332 Here is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a flower in the garden. I don't know what this garden flower is, but it has flowers so blue/violet/indigo that they are nearly black. My camera can't capture them. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)

I believe the flowers are Blue Throatwort (Trachelium caerulum) in the Campanulaceae. "Purple Haze" would be a good name for them. Thanks for again the ID Jeff - it took me a day to remember!
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Trachelium caerulum; Campanulaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Trachelium caerulum; Campanulaceae

File.Name: c729e_c728-019.jpg
Notes: 7673171722 I found this handsome Spider on the fluffy nest of an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on a white Cudweed - Gnaphalium stramineum or Gnaphalium luteo-album in the in the Asteraceae plant family. It wasn't attached to any obvious web. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium stramineum; Gnaphalium luteo-album; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium stramineum; Gnaphalium luteo-album; Asteraceae

File.Name: c729f_c728-011.jpg
Notes: 7673102600 This is a small Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera hiding under a leaf of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It appears to be eating something that looks "insect like" - maybe the remains of a recent molt? (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: c729g_c728-013.jpg
Notes: 7672920656 These little beetles in the order Coleoptera are "doing it" - making more little beetles - on the last flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon. I guess they are Leaf Beetles in the family Chrysomelidae, in particular, Case-bearing Leaf Beetles in the subfamily Cryptocephalinae - maybe genus Babia? (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)

File.Name: c729h_c728-029.jpg
Notes:
7672755866 This is a different goofy-looking Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera on foliage of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I found a pair of them in the woods yesterday. They are quite small, maybe 1/2 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2012)

If something doesn't look quite right, it's because I flipped the photo almost 180∞ to make it look "more natural". This critter was really hanging upside-down.
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
