File.Name: c730a_c730-050.jpg
Notes:
7681300656 This is another two-fer of male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies working the old-fashioned flowers of Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden this afternoon. One has its wings open! They are beautiful, with some iridescence on the upper wings. They are the reason why this is the official California State Butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

I dig how you can see the figure 8 on their hind wings - from above and below!

File.Name: c730b_c730-051.jpg
Notes: 7681221248 This is a two-fer of male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies working the old-fashioned flowers of Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden this afternoon. They are beautiful, with some iridescence on the upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

File.Name: c730c_c730-022.jpg
Notes: 7681063110 This is a humble non-native Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly working the bluer-than-blue non-native flowers in the garden. It believe the flowers are Blue Throatwort (Trachelium caerulum) in the Campanulaceae. "Purple Haze" would be a good name for them. Thanks for again the ID Jeff - it took me a day to remember! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

File.Name: c730d_c730-041.jpg
Notes:
7680931658 It's a small miracle that there is a new violet flower on the native Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon in our dry summer - and a wasp found it!

This is a large Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia) in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera working the last flower of this native thistle. The wasp is recognizable because of its goggle-eyes and long proboscis. What will it do when the flower is done? (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c730e_c730-031.jpg
Notes:
7680854128 "Pleased to meet you,
Hope you guess my name..."

This is looking eye-to-eye at the large tan-colored Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) that I found this morning munching the seeds of a spent flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have no idea what its name is, but I'll bet Alice knows! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

I have another shot of the same grasshopper in my [Older] photo.

File.Name: c730f_c730-036.jpg
Notes: 7680746196 This is a rather large tan-colored Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) that I found this morning munching the seeds of a spent flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have no idea what it is, but I'll bet Alice knows! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

File.Name: c730g_c730-005.jpg
Notes: 7680646464 Red eyes and red butt... I think this fly might be a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae in the order Diptera. It's fairly large for a fly. It was perched for a moment on a succulent leaf in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

File.Name: c730h_c730-010.jpg
Notes:
7680559656 I happened to notice this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on the tip of a leaf of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden this morning. It really is tiny - only about 400 pixels long, and I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. That comes to less than 1/10 inch! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

It should be obvious that it wasn't windy today!

File.Name: c731a_c731-008.jpg
Notes:
7688260052 This is a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) taking moisture from a leaf in the garden after we watered today. It looks black at first, but you can see a iridescent blue sheen to it. They don't visit flowers, and are hard to capture. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2012)

We're starting to get ready for our trip to Oregon. We're leaving on Thursday morning. I hate getting ready for a trip, but it's cool once we're on the road! We'll be back by the end of the month.

Probably no new photos tomorrow - unless I get lucky and capture that Spilomyia interrupta in the garden that's been taunting me! But I do have something to post.

File.Name: c731b_c731-006.jpg
Notes:
7688223342 Me and my shadow...

I got this photo of a gorgeous iridescent green Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) in the garden this morning. These little flies are predators. Note the scooped-out head - it's a trait shared by other insect predators like robber flies and dragonflies. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2012)

File.Name: c731c_c731-003.jpg
Notes:
7688113190 Here is another photo of the handsome little black and white Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of (I guess) a flowering Aloe plant in the Aloaceae or Asphodeloideae or Xanthorrhoeaceae plant family, depending on your authority. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2012)

I find it on these flowers every summer - but how does it know to come to them? I reckon it can't be the same bee year after year, and it can't be instinct to visit a non-native flower!
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Aloaceae; Asphodeloideae; Xanthorrhoeaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Aloaceae; Asphodeloideae; Xanthorrhoeaceae

File.Name: c731d_c730-001.jpg
Notes: 7688071694 Here is a large Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) committing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering under the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. It's robbing a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly" in the Lamiaceae plant family) in the garden. You can see the proboscis of the bee sliding under the sepal. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

File.Name: c731e_c730-045.jpg
Notes: 7687986382 This is a large gray granite-colored Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) that I found perched on a rock encrusted with dead moss and gray lichens. I never would have noticed it, but it jumped - and I saw where it landed. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

File.Name: c731f_c729-015.jpg
Notes: 7687906270 These tiny beetles in the order Coleoptera are "doing it" - making more tiny beetles - on a single flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Everything about this photo is tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2012)

File.Name: c731g_c731-002.jpg
Notes: 7687770362 I found this tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on a non-native Milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden today. The flowers look like orange Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa), and they may well be. The spider sure looks like a small copy of the large Cat-Faced Spiders (Araneus gemmoides) that spin their webs under our eaves in the fall - I can even see a little "cat face" on its abdomen! I guess large spiders start small? (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2012)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Milkweed; Butterfly Weed; Cat-Faced Spiders
ScientificName: Araneidae; Asclepiadaceae; Asclepias tuberosa; Araneus gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Asclepiadaceae; Asclepias tuberosa; Araneus gemmoides

File.Name: c731h_c730-052.jpg
Notes: 7687735084 I can't resist one more photo of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly showing the dog face on its upper wing. It's working the old-fashioned flowers of Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden. They are beautiful, with some iridescence on the upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

File.Name: c801a_c801-004.jpg
Notes:
7695343320 This is a beautiful Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly in the garden - the first I've see in our canyon this year. It's sticking its proboscis into a flower of (I guess) a non-native Winter savory (Satureja montana, Lamiaceae) plant in our garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2012)

We've been packing and getting ready to leave for Oregon in the morning. I'll be back before the end of the month, hopefully with lots of new photos.

File.Name: c801b_c801-001.jpg
Notes:
7695305606 I prefer living butterflies to specimens!

This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a new flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. There are also a couple of little ants interested in the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2012)

File.Name: c801c_c729-021.jpg
Notes: 7695238192 This is a closer shot of the male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly that I collected 30+ years ago on 14 August 1981. See my [Older photo] for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2012)

File.Name: c801d_c729-029.jpg
Notes:
7695042376 Mea culpa - 30+ years ago, I made a real insect collection with poison jars, pins, labels, boxes - the works. Most of my specimens are eaten by beetles now. It really takes constant vigilance to maintain an insect collection.

I collected this male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly years ago. It's dated 14 August 1981, and it still looks like it was collected yesterday. I didn't collect many butterflies - I was mostly interested in flies and wasps - but I made an exception for this one. I made a neat air-tight wooden box for the specimen - with hand tools, we lived in a cabin with no electricity back then - and gave it to my Mom and Dad as a Christmas present. Mom and Dad are gone now, and I retrieved the specimen from their home. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2012)

I'd much rather collect specimens with my camera. On the other hand, we probably take out more insects just driving to a collecting spot - *splat* - than we would ever collect!

File.Name: c801d_c730-003.jpg
Notes: 7694939228 This is a rather large Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) perched on flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. It's sticking its tongue out - and it looks like an anteater! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2012)

File.Name: c801e_c729-014.jpg
Notes: 7694876856 This is a handsome Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I've been hoping to get a sharper photo - get the eyes sharp! - but I'm out of time. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2012)

File.Name: c801f_c725-006.jpg
Notes: 7694855946 I found this handsome little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) on a stem of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 July 2012)

File.Name: c801g_c723-019.jpg
Notes: 7694806772 Here are native summer flowers of Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Flowering now is quite a trick after a drought year and a drought summer, but these are tough natives that can handle it. They are hard to photograph - you can get close and just capture the flowers, which look like any other buckwheat flowers - or back up to get the plant. The leaves at the bottom are totally shriveled. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 July 2012)

File.Name: c824a_c824-034.jpg
Notes:
7854799408 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly ovipositing on an unlikely Fuchsia flower bud in the garden today. Sure, it's a bit overexposed - but it's worth it to make out that beautiful blue fringe on its wings. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2012)

We got back from our Oregon trip last night. I took 865 photos (with pruning) and actually kept 314 of them. I'll be featuring them for a few days. Just as well, as I have a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society newsletter to prepare by the end of the month!
Locality: with pruning) and actually kept 314 of them.  I'll be featuring them for a few days.  Just as well

File.Name: c824b_c817-025.jpg
Notes: 7854761832 Here are a pair of Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterflies thinking about mating on damp soil by a seep in the Oregon woods. They have a beautiful "blue dun" color - much prized for trout dry flies. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

File.Name: c824c_c817-033.jpg
Notes: 7854687288 This is a Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) butterfly perched on a leaf in the Oregon woods. These humble moth-like butterfly's are hard to photograph as they rarely visit flowers. This one landed right by me while I was getting a photo of the orchids nearby. (Photos soon!) (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

File.Name: c824d_c817-016.jpg
Notes:
7854598118 I won't try to guess what kind of Fritillary Butterfly ( genus Speyeria) this is, but here is "puddling" in a puddle in the Oregon woods. It doesn't seem to have "silvered" wings. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

This was the only photo of a Fritillary that I got during our time in Oregon. Fritillaries and Parnassians were both scarce, even in the high meadows near Twin Lakes. I was disappointed. I believe that southern Oregon shared our drought year, though Portland did better.

File.Name: c824e_c805-017.jpg
Notes:
7854528526 Did you notice the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on this flower of Tansy Ragwort (Senecio jacobaea) in the Asteraceae plant family? This butterfly didn't, and paid the price.

It's a pretty Pine White (Neophasia menapia) butterfly - now deceased. I got this photo in the Oregon woods on our summer vacation trip. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 August 2012)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Tansy Ragwort; Pine White
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Senecio jacobaea; Asteraceae; Neophasia menapia
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Senecio jacobaea; Asteraceae; Neophasia menapia

File.Name: c824f_c806-029.jpg
Notes: 7854447930 This is a pretty little Copper Butterfly - maybe the same Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) we have at home? If so, it's at the very northern end of its range. I believe this one is perched on a flower head of Gray's Lovage (Ligusticum grayi) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's a native plant that looks a lot like the non-native Queen Anne`s Lace (Daucus carota). (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)
CommonNames: Copper Butterfly; Gorgon Copper; Gray's Lovage; Queen Anne`s Lace
ScientificName: Lycaena gorgon; Ligusticum grayi; Apiaceae; Daucus carota
associatedTaxa: Lycaena gorgon; Ligusticum grayi; Apiaceae; Daucus carota

File.Name: c824g_c817-029.jpg
Notes: 7854354886 This is a handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a non-native flower head of Fuller's Teasel (Dipsacus fullonum) in the Dipsacacea plant family growing in the Oregon woods. We don't have these weeds at home, though I've found them near Lompoc. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

File.Name: c824h_c804-021.jpg
Notes: 7854269160 I believe these are the caterpillars of the Cinnabar Moth (Tyria jacobaeae) in the order Lepidoptera. They are happily munching flowers of Tansy Ragwort (Senecio jacobaea) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods. It's a non-native caterpillar eating a non-native weed. Good! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2012)
ScientificName: Tyria jacobaeae; Lepidoptera; Senecio jacobaea; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Tyria jacobaeae; Lepidoptera; Senecio jacobaea; Asteraceae

File.Name: c825a_c825-027.jpg
Notes:
7861382600 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a beautiful large Dragonfly (suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata) perched on a Cattail (family Typhaceae) leaf by the river. I guess it's a Flame Skimmer (Libellula saturata). I saw it on its favorite perch and didn't think I could sneak up on it, but it kept coming back to pose for my photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 August 2012)

Look at the way its wings are buttressed to give them strength. That is great engineering.

I went to the river today hoping to find "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia virgulti) butterflies on the starting-to-flower composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrubs by the river. The shrubs had new flowers, but there were no butterflies. In fact, the butterflies at home have mostly disappeared too. Hot weather and drought may be taking a toll.
Locality: Lepidospartum squamatum) shrubs by the river. The shrubs had new flowers
CommonNames: Dragonfly; Cattail; Flame Skimmer; Behr's Metalmark; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Anisoptera; Odonata; Typhaceae; Libellula saturata; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Anisoptera; Odonata; Typhaceae; Libellula saturata; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: c825b_c825-022.jpg
Notes:
7861286372 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

I don't really know dragonflies, but I guess this is a male Western Pondhawk (Erythemis collocata) in the skimmer family (Libellulidae) in the order Odonata. I found this one perched on a low branch above the green scum that makes up much of the Santa Ynez River now. Fortunately the dragonfly kept coming back to the same perch! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 August 2012)

Notes: 7860972018 I noticed this beautiful Dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) perched on a leaf trying to get warm in the morning sun. I don't really know my dragonflies yet, but maybe this is a Striped Meadowhawk (Sympetrum pallipes)? I think I see thoracic stripes. (Cape Blanco State Park, Curry County, Oregon, 20 August 2012)

File.Name: c825f_c817-038.jpg
Notes: 7860898732 This is an interesting Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata that I found in the Oregon woods. Real dragonflies hold their wings out in parallel when they rest. Damselflies fold their wings in along their body. This one is in between. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

Notes:
7860819770 This is another impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) - maybe subfamily Asilinae. It's perched on a leaf of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the Ericaceae plant family. It looks so muscular!

I found this fly at Cape Blanco State Park in southern Oregon, not too far from Port Orford. Quite a few of these flies were out in the morning trying to warm up in the sun on a chilly morning. (Cape Blanco State Park, Curry County, Oregon, 20 August 2012)

This is absolutely our favorite Oregon coast campsite.

File.Name: c826a_c806-061.jpg
Notes:
7869363360 These are beautiful new flowers of a White Bog Orchid (Platanthera dilatata var. leucostachys) in the Orchidaceae plant family. The white flower stalks grow several feet tall. This one is growing in a soggy meadow in the Oregon woods. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)

Local flowers back home are not looking so lush. Flowers and insects are scarce in this drought year, especially at the end of our dry summer.

File.Name: c826b_c820-019.jpg
Notes: 7869310122 I believe this is the Coast Piperia (Piperia elegans) orchid in the Orchidaceae plant family. This one is just starting to flower at Cape Blanco State Park in the wind-blown scrub along the lighthouse trail. It has a thick, leafy stem. (Cape Blanco State Park, Curry County, Oregon, 20 August 2012)

File.Name: c826c_c817-030.jpg
Notes: 7869104870 I guess this is a native Royal Rein Orchid (Piperia transvera) in the Orchidaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods. The way the flower spurs are oriented horizontally is distinctive. If you compare the Piperia orchid in my [Previous] photo, they look clearly different - though I found them just 20 feet apart. I might be wrong about this identification. There are a few aphids on the flowers. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

File.Name: c826d_c817-035.jpg
Notes: 7868995758 I believe this is a flower spike of the Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family - aka Habenaria unalascensis ssp. elata. It's an orchid, but it really looks more like an asparagus stalk when it flowers. I checked the usual places and couldn't find it on our trip north to Portland, but I did find it on our return trip. Note that the spurs of the flowers are mostly aligned with the stalk - and compare my [Newer] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

File.Name: c826e_c804-001.jpg
Notes: 7868963136 Here are new flowers of Rattlesnake Orchid (Goodyera oblongifolia) in the Orchidaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods. They are the most common of the mid-summer orchids near our cabin by far, appearing after most other wildflowers. They have beautiful green leaves at the base - they are not saprophytes. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2012)

File.Name: c826f_c806-008.jpg
Notes:
7868843746 I believe this is a Western Coralroot (Corallorhiza mertensiana) orchid in the Orchidaceae plant family in the Oregon woods. I was too late for flowers, but the seed capsules are pretty too. This orchid is saprophytic, without chlorophyll. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)

I believe all orchids are mycotrophic because the orchid seeds are dust-like and carry no food supply for the emerging plant. They need a connection with a fungus to develop. But this orchid needs an additional myco-heterotrophic fungal connection to a tree because it has no chlorophyll of its own.

File.Name: c826g_c806-063.jpg
Notes:
7868717184 I believe these are unusual flowers of Clustered Broomrape (Orobanche fasciculata) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. This is a plant without green leaves that is a root-parasite on other plants. The Jepson manual (TJM3) says the usual host plants are Artemisia, Eriodictyon, Eriogonum, Galium - and I think that's a woodland Gallium on the left. The flowers look a lot like Mimulus. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)

I usually favor tradition, but "Broomrape" is a horrible common name.

File.Name: c826h_c804-005.jpg
Notes:
7868631188 These are the strange white flowers of Indian Pipes (Monotropa uniflora). This oddball has been placed in the Ericaceae plant family, but now it's back in its own Monotropaceae plant family. They are pure white and do not contain chlorophyll like most plants. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2012)

These flowers seem to be completely parasitic on forest trees. Or more likely they are myco-heterotrophs which are parasitic on the mycorrhizal fungi that are parasitic on trees. They have a fungal connection with tree roots in a three-way relationship mediated by an mycorrhizal connection between flower-fungus-tree. We only see them at all because they have to flower - like all other plants.

Maybe the whole forest is connected by fungi? See the interesting speculations by Paul Stamets .

File.Name: c827a_c827-032.jpg
Notes:
7877059330 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly in the air after taking wing from some kind of Lavender (Lavandula) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the garden. I had my camera set to take a burst of photos at 6 fps. In one photo, it's perched on the flower. This is the next photo! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2012)

File.Name: c827b_c804-014.jpg
Notes:
7876991016 Here is a large Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Homoptera) on a plant stem in the Oregon woods. I believe this is the actual cicada, caught in the act of molting. It has remarkable claws on its front legs - maybe just for this occasion, to hang on while molting. What an opportunity - to leave your old life behind in a husk and start over as an new adult! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2012)

We heard cicadas singing at night. At least Julie did. Their chorus is the same pitch as my tinnitus - that constant ringing in my ears that comes from too many years of too-loud music. Be warned! (On the other hand, a constant chorus of cicadas isn't bad!)

It has a frosted head and thorax, and a rather "depleted" abdomen. I don't know what it is. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

7891119576 I can't go to Oregon without at least one photo of a fern!

This is an unusual fern that I've only noticed in this one place in the Oregon woods. I guess that it's a Leathery Grape Fern (Botrychium multifidum) in the Opioglossaceae plant family. 7891003996 I can't go to Oregon without at least one photo of a giant slug!

I found this large Slug (Gastropoda) in the Oregon woods on our recent trip. It's quite large, maybe 3 inches. Maybe it's a Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus), though it sure looks different from our Southern California species- see this photo. File.Name: c830a_c830-004.jpg
Notes:
7898025166 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a rather pale Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working the flowers of a well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. This butterfly is missing its tails - the birds take a toll. This is our largest butterfly, and it's beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2012)

It was good to see this critter today. I haven't seen any swallowtails since we got back from Oregon a week ago. Drought and heat have taken a toll, and there's a general lack of insects in the woods. What will the autumn spiders do?

File.Name: c830b_c807-002.jpg
Notes:
7897928158 This isn't a mushroom, but a slime mold. This is the "Dog Vomit Slime Mold" - really! If you don't believe me, you can find it at Wikipedia. It lives up to it's name.

This is NOT a fungus - for one reason, it can move. This is the interesting slime mold Fuligo septica in the fascinating Myxomycetes class. This photo shows the mobile plasmodium stage becoming the solid lime-encrusted spore-bearing aethalium stage. It looks and feels like a toasted marshmallow. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 August 2012)

It's more of a "them" than an "it". Slime molds live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in a substrate like this patch of moss and fallen wood. But sometimes they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form this visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" that can crawl along like a slug. It finally dries and forms a mushroom-like "aethalium" structure to release tiny spores that will disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy for these critters' prime mission - to carry mother nature's seed to new decaying wood in the woods.

I find the same slime mold at home. See my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7178979043/

File.Name: c830c_c820-041.jpg
Notes:
7897865622 I believe this is a mature Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom coming up in the woods at Cape Blanco State Park in Oregon. It's growing under Sitka Spruce (Picea sitchensis) trees. Note the brown cap with "stars" that are the remnants of the universal veil that also leaves a cup or volva at the base of the stem. You can also see the ring or partial veil on the stem. See my [Older] photo. (Cape Blanco State Park, Curry County, Oregon, 20 August 2012)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. They should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Picea sitchensis; A. muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Picea sitchensis; A. muscaria

These mushrooms are quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. It should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.

File.Name: c830e_c822-003.jpg
Notes: 7897693880 I believe this is a fresh-looking Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom coming up in the moss under Sitka Spruce (Picea sitchensis) trees in Northern California. It's beautiful, but it sure looks different from the mushrooms in Oregon. Notice that perfect little hole nibbled into the top by some critter! (Big Lagoon County Park, Humboldt County, California, 22 August 2012)

File.Name: c830f_c805-010.jpg
Notes:
7897580148 I guess this is a Western Grisette (Amanita pachycolea) mushroom in the Oregon woods a few weeks ago. They have free white gills and a volva, but no ring on the stem. These distinctive mushrooms used to be classed as genus Amanitopsis, but now they are included as section Vaginatae of the Amanitaceae. The dark brown striate (lined) cap is distinctive. It's said to be edible - if you dare eat any Amanita. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 August 2012)

This was the only fleshy fungus that I found near the cabin by Susan Creek. It's the dry season. Oregon basically shares our winter-wet/summer-dry climate. There's just lots more of the winter-wet! I was surprised to find any mushrooms.

File.Name: c830g_c820-044.jpg
Notes:
7897484052 It seems strange, but this Lobster Mushroom (Hypomyces lacifluorum) is really delicious - at least fresh off the grill, when the fungus is fresh. Strange, because it's a parasitic fungus that engulfs and changes the acrid wild Lactarius and Russula mushrooms in the woods - probably Russula in this case. We passed on this specimen. (Cape Blanco State Park, Curry County, Oregon, 20 August 2012)

I have photos of fresher specimens at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65777992/
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65778335/

File.Name: c830h_c822-004.jpg
Notes: 7897397640 I found several different Russula mushrooms in Oregon and Northern California last week, even in summer, when it's dry even there. They mushrooms are easy to recognize to genus. Just give it the "Russula test" - the stems are brittle, and you can snap them like a piece of chalk. But there are many different kinds, and it's harder to figure the species. Fortunately, I don't have to know Oregon and Northern California mushrooms! (Big Lagoon County Park, Humboldt County, California, 22 August 2012)

File.Name: c831a_c831-005.jpg
Notes:
7903572166 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is the remarkable nymph of an Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae), in the insect order Hemiptera. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. It's perched on a stem of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing on the wild side of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2012)

It looks a lot like the nymphs of Bush Katydids (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera, for example this photo.

I'm glad to see this predator, but I wonder what future it will face. Insects are already scarce at the end of our dry summer in a drought year with just 1/2 normal rainfall.

Notes: 7903473550 These are barely-recognizable galled leaves of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I guess that this is an infection, like "Peach Leaf Curl". (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2012)

File.Name: c831d_c820-038.jpg
Notes: 7903441588 This is a new cone on a Sitka Spruce (Picea sitchensis) tree on the Oregon coast. If you look close, you can spot a tiny ant on the inside curve of the cone. The cones feel soft, quite different from pine cones. (Cape Blanco State Park, Curry County, Oregon, 20 August 2012)

File.Name: c831e_c806-067.jpg
Notes:
7903376790 This photo shows the remarkable color of Orange Agoseris (Agoseris aurantiaca) in the Asteraceae plant family. I found this beautiful flower in a dry mountain meadow. I'm sorry there's not a butterfly working the flower, but butterflies were scarce this trip. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)

Going to this Oregon meadow is like stepping back a few months to springtime!

File.Name: c831f_c806-025.jpg
Notes: 7903335696 This is some kind of short-awned Brome Grass in the genus Bromus of the Poaceae plant family. It's quite beautiful in full flower. I don't know what species it is. I got this photo in the high meadows near Twin Lakes in Oregon, about 5,000 feet elevation. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)

File.Name: c831g_c806-051.jpg
Notes: 7903243958 These are odd green flowers of Green False Hellebore aka False Hellebore (Veratrum viride) in the Liliaceae plant family - maybe moved to the Melanthiaceae. They are said to be quite toxic, but who would want to eat green flowers? I got this photo in the high meadows near Twin Lakes in Oregon, about 5,000 feet elevation. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)

File.Name: c831h_c806-028.jpg
Notes: 7903178970 These are pretty yellow flowers of a native buckwheat in the Polygonaceae plant family, probably the Sulfur Buckwheat (Eriogonum umbellatum). I believe the violet blotches in the background are the flowers of Mountain Owl-Clover (Orthocarpus imbricatus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. I got this photo in the high meadows near Twin Lakes in Oregon, about 5,000 feet elevation. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)
ScientificName: Polygonaceae; Eriogonum umbellatum; Orthocarpus imbricatus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Polygonaceae; Eriogonum umbellatum; Orthocarpus imbricatus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: c901a_c901-042.jpg
Notes:
7910449146 Here is a beautiful Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) working new flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. This is the first of these distinctive butterflies I've seen this year, and I only saw this one. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2012)

Maybe not by chance, aliceinwl1 posted a photo of the butterfly pupa today. They seem late this year.

I reckon I need one more day to be done with my Oregon photos. Yeah, I'm stalling because I need one more day to be done with the newsletter I've been working on, though I have been taking photos every day this week.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c901b_c818-023.jpg
Notes:
7910385370 These are new flowers of giant Elk Clover aka California Spikenard (Aralia californica) in the Araliaceae plant family growing in a ditch along Boundary Road in Oregon. We have this same plant back home, but I have to hunt for it in deep north-slope canyons! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2012)

Note the tiny flies on the flowers.

File.Name: c901c_c806-015.jpg
Notes: 7910329612 Here are new flower heads of Nettleleaf Giant Hyssop (Agastache urticifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in a mountain meadow in Oregon. We have other non-native plants in this genus growing in our garden at home. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)

File.Name: c901d_c804-028.jpg
Notes: 7910257806 These are new flowers of Douglas' Spiraea (Spiraea douglasii) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's growing in a marshy area near our cabin in the Oregon woods. It's a pretty shrub that gets to about 6-7 feet tall, that can take standing in water. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

File.Name: c901e_c806-005.jpg
Notes: 7910157742 These are the odd flowers of native Pipsissewa aka "Prince's-Pine" (Chimaphila umbellata" in the Pyrolaceae plant family in the Oregon woods. The odd flowers have an odd name. The plant has some medicinal uses. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2012)

File.Name: c901f_c817-011.jpg
Notes:
7910046048 Life and death in the Oregon woods.

I found these jaw bones 1/4 mile from our cabin in the Oregon woods. This is just as I found them. I believe these are the remains of a Rocky Mountain Elk (Cervus canadensis nelsoni), though I'm not sure of the species. They are much prized by hunters in the fall, though this looks like a natural kill. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2012)

File.Name: c901g_c804-027.jpg
Notes:
7909910574 I wish all weeds were this beautiful! "Beautiful weed" sounds like an oxymoron, but I think this one qualifies - at least growing one-by-one and not en masse.

I believe this is a flower head of Spotted Knapweed (Centaurea maculosa) in the Asteraceae plant family, though it's not spotted. It's become a very common weed in Oregon. Sometimes it covers whole fields and miles of roadsides. On the other hand, it's not thorny like our invasive Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) at home, and doesn't seem to invade the woods away from the roads (?). (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2012)

File.Name: c901h_c807-011.jpg
Notes: 7909850040 This is a flower head of Tansy Ragwort (Senecio jacobaea) in the Asteraceae plant family growing pretty deep in the Oregon woods. It's a introduced weed, and rather pretty in full flower like this. But it's a bad weed that gets into the woods. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 August 2012)

File.Name: c902a_c901-018.jpg
Notes:
7918187178 I believe this is a mighty female Tarantula Hawk (Pepsis mildei, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera). It's peacefully working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river, but she's hell with tarantula spiders! I guess this is a female - larger with coiled antennae. (See Wayne's Word.) Compare my [Older] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2012)

This is one of the world's largest wasps - as big as a dragonfly and almost as large as a small hummingbird! I believe this is a female because of the size and coiled antennae. In this photo you can see her brilliant blue iridescent colors. They seem clumsy on the flowers - too big!

I reckon I'm done with my Oregon photos. But I've been laboring all day on my newsletter, so just two photos today. I've been putting off my labor to get new photos, but I'll be done tomorrow, on Labor Day, with a deadline looming. As it should be.
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c902b_c901-020.jpg
Notes: 7918132426 Here is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (Pepsis mildei, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera). It's peacefully working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. I guess this is a male - smaller, with straight antennae. Compare my [Newer] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2012)
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c903a_c827-007.jpg
Notes:
7926348036 This is a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe Spilomyia interrupta or kin. It's working an orange (oh so orange) flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family in the garden. It has amazing patterned eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2012)

Just two photos again today. I finished my newsletter labor today on Labor Day, just sneaking in under the deadline. Almost done, but I have to visit the printers tomorrow. I post a link when it's final.
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia interrupta; Tithonia rotundifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia interrupta; Tithonia rotundifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: c903b_c827-011.jpg
Notes: 7926270926 On the left is a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe Spilomyia interrupta or kin. (See my [Newer] photo.) On the right is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly. They are sharing a composite flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the garden. It's a great butterfly plant. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2012)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; California Dogface; Mexican Sunflower
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia interrupta; Colias eurydice; Tithonia rotundifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: c904a_c904-008.jpg
Notes:
7934243916 I found this beautiful Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working the new flowers of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family in the garden this morning. The butterflies have been scarce this year, and I haven't seen a single caterpillar on the non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) plants in the Apiaceae plant family near home. It's curious that this native butterfly favors non-native plants. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2012)

This photo is all sweetness and light. The rest of my photos today are all predators, most of them with prey. The summer pollinators are falling prey to the autumn predators. It is their time.

My newsletter went to the printer today, and my work is almost done for a while. Whew!
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Tithonia rotundifolia; Asteraceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Tithonia rotundifolia; Asteraceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: c904b_c831-029.jpg
Notes: 7934066252 This is a another tiny but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's waiting for prey on a flower of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. Be careful little wing! (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2012)

File.Name: c904c_c831-019.jpg
Notes: 7933969588 I spotted this Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) hanging at a weird angle from a flower head of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family by our mailbox. I looked close and then I saw the Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) that brought it down. Look close just to the right of the bee's eye. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2012)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; California Buckwheat; Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: c904d_c901-030.jpg
Notes: 7933841118 I believe this is a Silver Argiope (Argiope argenatata) spider with those impressive spines on the back of its abdomen. It's got a wrapped bee-size prey, and a tiny Fly> (order Diptera) is interested in it. Considering the source, I guess it's a Freeloader Fly (family Milichiidae), though I've never seen one so engorged. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2012)

File.Name: c904e_c831-015.jpg
Notes: 7933764358 I guess this is a Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata) garden spider in the native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family by our mailbox. You can see just a corner of its "stabilimentum" - a zigzag structure of webbing that looks like cigarette smoke in an old film noir movie. This spider has a remarkably thin abdomen. If its hungry, that "packaged prey" should help. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2012)
ScientificName: Argiope trifasciata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; stabilimentum
associatedTaxa: Argiope trifasciata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; stabilimentum

File.Name: c904f_c825-002.jpg
Notes: 7933674326 This is a different Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae with a white and red abdomen. It's perched on a flower head of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It seems to match the faded flowers, though I don't know that these spiders are color-shifters. Beautiful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 August 2012)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: c904g_c824-023.jpg
Notes: 7933627720 This mighty Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae has captured a Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a newly flowering plant of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess it's a Megachilidae bee which collects pollen on its abdomen - but I also see pollen on it's rear leg. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2012)

File.Name: c904h_c827-029.jpg
Notes: 7933499672 This is a rather large yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae). It's made a home for itself on the flower of non-native composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden - or is it a trap? Looking at BugGuide, it might be the Whitebanded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), a highly variable species. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2012)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy; Whitebanded Crab Spider

File.Name: c905a_c828-010.jpg
Notes:
7940924722 This is a Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia) in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera on a flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family in the garden. They have remarkable goggle-eyes and a long proboscis. These wasps are hard to photograph as they move so fast between the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2012)

Well blow me down - we had some rain today! It came from tropical depression John, about 0.06 inches so far on the porch. Not much, but not bad for September, and it's enough to settle the dust. And it smells so good! No thunder or lighting so far.
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Tithonia rotundifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: c905b_c825-006.jpg
Notes: 7940768998 This is an interesting Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) that I found working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. It must be a female with that long ovipositor. The antennae go on a long way. It sure looks like a Katydid in the family Tettigoniidae, though ours are usually leaf-green. Alice? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 August 2012)
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Tettigoniidae

File.Name: c905c_c825-040.jpg
Notes:
7940716360 Here is a Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) perched on a sandstone rock on the dry flat by the river. It has pretty blue tibia on its hind leg - the last large segment. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 August 2012)

Even grasshoppers have become scarce in this very dry year.

File.Name: c905d_c831-002.jpg
Notes:
7940568300 This is an adult male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) huddled in a dead leaf of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Crickets sleep all day and work the night shift. They are the songsters of our woods in the fall. I guess that this is the Western Tree Cricket (Oecanthus californicus or kin) that make a steady trill, unlike the Snowy Tree Crickets (Oecanthus niveus group) which pulse. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2012)

They drive me nuts when sing in the house at night. They are incredibly loud, but stop singing whenever you get close enough to see them. On the other hand, they must be good luck.
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Wild Sweet Pea; Western Tree Cricket; Snowy Tree Crickets
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Oecanthus californicus; Oecanthus niveus
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Oecanthus californicus; Oecanthus niveus

File.Name: c905e_c905-011.jpg
Notes:
7940471490 This is an odd-looking Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. I guess it's in the family Issidae, genus Neaethus. This one is perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2012)

Today was not a good day for photos - it was cloudy and breezy, but I did my best. I'm sure I'll get better photos of this critter as the month goes on. They are pretty common in our woods.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Neaethus; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Neaethus; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: c905f_c824-032.jpg
Notes: 7940436318 This is a different Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a single flower of non-native Milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden today. The flowers look like an orange cultivar Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa), and they may well be. The spider has pretty violet front legs and remarkable toenails! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2012)

File.Name: c905g_c827-021.jpg
Notes: 7940377800 Here is a beautiful Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a non-native Zinnia flower in the garden. Zinnias are great butterfly flowers as long as you avoid the double flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2012)

File.Name: c905h_c904-017.jpg
Notes:
7940339586 Here is another photo of the beautiful Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working the new flowers of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family in the garden yesterday morning. See my [Older] photo. The blue specks on its wings look like showers of fireworks. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2012)

This butterfly looks newly emerged, but I'd like to know where it emerged from. I haven't seen a single caterpillar on the non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) plants in the Apiaceae plant family near home - it's usual host plant.
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Tithonia rotundifolia; Asteraceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Tithonia rotundifolia; Asteraceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: c906a_c906-023.jpg
Notes:
7947259058 These are frisky Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies on flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. I'm sure they are thinking about mating - the smaller male fluttering towards the larger female, pretending to feed. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2012)

D'oh - I set my ISO low for a flower photo, and forgot to put it back. But this photo will do.

I went to fold, collate, label, and stamp newsletters this morning. And then went to my favorite place by the river. I'm done! We've had a bit more rain today. Our total is now about 0.12 inches in the rain gauge on the porch.

D'oh - I set my ISO low for a flower photo, and forgot to put it back. But this photo will do.

Notes:
7947002554 This is a very large pollen-dusted Wasp in the insect order Hymenoptera working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. My first thought was the Cicada Killer (Sphecius convallis), but it sure doesn't look like any of the photos at BugGuide. The fringes on the segments of its abdomen seem distinctive. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2012)

Please help me with an ID.

Notes: 7946944794 This is a different Sand Wasp in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera. It's smaller and lighter in color than my previous photos - but it has the same goggle-eyes! This one is working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2012)

File.Name: c906f_c906-002.jpg
Notes:
7946873536 This is an enormous Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. Well, it SEEMS enormous because after all it's just a fly. It's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long, bumblebee size. I found this one working the late flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. These flies are camera shy. This is a 1:1 crop from a distant shot. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2012)

It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the bristly local "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae.

File.Name: c906g_c906-032.jpg
Notes:
7946775288 Here is an interesting Fly> (order Diptera) working the new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. I believe it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae in the genus Cylindromyia. The larvae of these flies are predators on other insect larvae, but the adults visit flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2012)

D'oh - I set my ISO low for a flower photo, and forgot to put it back. But this photo will do.

File.Name: c906h_c906-039.jpg
Notes: 7946731822 This is another photo of the same Silver Argiope (Argiope argenatata) spider with those impressive horns on the back of its abdomen down by the river. You can see its "stabilimentum" - the zigzag structures of webbing that looks like cigarette smoke in an old film noir movie. It has captured and packaged an unfortunate Wasp in the upper-left. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2012)

File.Name: c907a_c907-036.jpg
Notes:
7952931148 I was already focused on the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) when this Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera flew into my photo. The spider raised its legs to strike... and the fly got away. I love these life and death struggles in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)

I finally took my usual walk through the canyon today, but all my photos are from the garden this morning. Gotta have a garden! It's like a water hole in the woods for much native life.

File.Name: c907b_c907-029.jpg
Notes:
7952898390 This is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera on a pretty Zinnia flower in the garden. It seems to be gathering pollen - you can see some stuck on its proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)

This might be the same lucky bee fly in my [Newer] photo!

File.Name: c907c_c907-037.jpg
Notes:
7952828858 These are Yellow Jacket wasps - family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera - on the body of a dead Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) on the ground by the front porch today. The bees just wear themselves out and finally die. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)

I came back after my three-hour walk, and the bee was still there. I guess it's too big for the wasps to handle. Leave it for the ants.

File.Name: c907d_c907-020.jpg
Notes:
7952748416 I'm pretty sure this is a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (genus Eumeninae, family Vespidea, order Hymenoptera). It's working flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden. That "double abdomen" is distinctive. Maybe it's genus Eumenes. There are Zinnia flowers in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)

These wasps make neat little clay pots for their nests. See my photo at: http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7238373724/
ScientificName: Eumeninae; Vespidea; Hymenoptera; Menthe spicata; Eumenes; Zinnia
associatedTaxa: Eumeninae; Vespidea; Hymenoptera; Menthe spicata; Eumenes; Zinnia

File.Name: c907e_c907-014.jpg
Notes: 7952723024 Here are tiny Wild Bees (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) "doing it" - making more little bees - on a leaf in the garden. I guess that's the larger female below. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)

File.Name: c907f_c907-073.jpg
Notes: 7952648620 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on the dark inner cone of a flower of non-native composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. The flowers are native to the U.S., but not here in California. (Thanks for the reminder Urban Woodswalker!) I've noticed that these spiders often carry bits of pollen on their pedipalps as bait. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: c907g_c907-004.jpg
Notes:
7952573070 These are new flowers of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium - aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. They are growing on the dry side of our garden. They are beautiful and surprising in this dry time of year at the end of summer. I reckon their job is to keep the hummingbirds alive. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)

We rescued the original plant from a slide along the road years ago. It's done well here.
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: c907h_c907-007.jpg
Notes:
7952501830 I found this LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - on the dry side of our garden today, just a day or two after we had a bit of summer rain. It's growing with sweet-smelling native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)

I'm most interested in the base of the mushroom. The spawn is growing on a group of "glued-together" fallen acorns, with root-like rhizomorphs. It sure looks like the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushroom, but it would be surprising to find one this early in the season.
CommonNames: Yerba Buena; Coast Live Oak; Oak-Loving Collybia
ScientificName: Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Collybia dryophila
associatedTaxa: Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Collybia dryophila

File.Name: c908a_c908-056.jpg
Notes:
7959504936 This is an uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly perched on a new flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are uncommon, but I seem to find them every year. I wasn't sure if I would see these in this dry year, but here it is. The common name comes from the colors, not the taste! I also found my first Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterflies on the pass today. Photos tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

Today was a just about a perfect photo day - warm (90s), but not too hot, gentle wind. The recent rain has all evaporated by now, raising the humidity. I hope for more of the same!
CommonNames: Fatal Metalmark; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Mormon Metalmark
ScientificName: Calephelis nemesis californica; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Apodemia mormo
associatedTaxa: Calephelis nemesis californica; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Apodemia mormo

File.Name: c908b_c908-052.jpg
Notes:
7959408176 This is a remarkable "parasitic wasp" - Hymenoptera, but I have no idea what kind. It's perched on new galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. This is on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls make real honey on their surface, and the wasp is interested. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

The wasp has a remarkably long ovipositor. I watched it for a while, and I think it was after the honey, not a site to explore with its ovipositor - but I'm not sure of anything about this photo. If you know about the wasp, please let me know!
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c908c_c908-044.jpg
Notes:
7959230254 Here is a meeting of interesting wasps (order Hymenoptera) on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. What they have a common is the gall of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera in between them. They come for the sweet honey that the galls make on the top. There seems to be a new gall erupting from the twig bark in the top-center of my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

I'm not sure what either wasp is. I guess the wasp on the right is family Vespidae. It's quite small for a vespid, maybe 3/8 inch. The wasp on the left is a "parasitic wasp" - but I have no idea what kind. See my [Newer] photo.
CommonNames: wasps; Scrub Oak; Clasping Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Vespidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Vespidae

File.Name: c908d_c908-029.jpg
Notes:
7959102316 Here are interesting ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending a new gall of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. This is on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls make real honey on the surface to feed the ants, and the the ants protect them from parasitic wasps. Sounds like a good trade that benefits both. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

I'm pretty sure the ants are genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. Look close at the ant on bottom. They have two spines on their thorax and an articulated abdomen. I've seen them before.
CommonNames: ants; Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: c908e_c908-031.jpg
Notes:
7959018438 These are last year's galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera from last year. (The galls, not the photo.) The galls used to contain wasp larvae, which control the abnormal plant growth. The young galls make honey, but these old galls are just dead husks. I found these a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

I added this photo to show how colonial the galls usually are. This year is different as I've only found one or a few of the galls on a branch. (See my [Newer] photos.) It hasn't been a very good year for galls in general. Maybe the late-season rains clobbered them?
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c908f_c908-054.jpg
Notes:
7958938528 This is a mass of galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I wonder if a single female gall wasp is responsible for them all? There are individual capsules here, but it's hard to make them out in the mass. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including maybe this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.
CommonNames: Crystalline Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae

File.Name: c908g_c908-037.jpg
Notes:
7958882020 These are remarkable new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can still see the individual capsules. In time they fill the leaf with fuzz. See my [Newer] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including maybe this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. File.Name: c908h_c908-033.jpg
Notes: 7958771218 These new galls of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like "candy corn" and are about the same size. They are monothalamus (just one wasp larvae inside) and easily detachable (you can break them off). They have an interesting reticulate pattern. They look like mushrooms growing from the oak leaves, but they are the nests of wasps. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: c909a_c909-030.jpg
Notes:
7967580522 This critter has the best camouflage I've ever seen. Any better, and I never would have spotted it!

Look across the top of my photo. The critter is feeding on the red-tipped stamen of a flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family by our mailbox. Look at the left-most stamen and look for the legs, then follow the body back. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2012)

I guess this is a little "looper" type caterpillar (Lepidoptera) for some kind of moth. I only noticed it because it was standing up and waving back and forth when I came by. It must "glue" flower petals to its body - all the more remarkable because it picks fresh white and old brown petals to complete the illusion on these fading flowers.

File.Name: c909b_c909-027.jpg
Notes:
7967468668 This critter has the best camouflage I've ever seen. Any better, and I never would have spotted it!

I'll give a hint - look across the top of my photo. I got this photo on fading shrubs of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family by our mailbox. If you still can't see it, look at my [Newer] photo and come back! (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2012)

File.Name: c909c_c828-003.jpg
Notes:
7967333898 Here's something you don't see every day - an acorn blowing a bubble!

I believe this the gall (or at least a sign of the gall) of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis or kin) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. I found this on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family.

The gall wasp larvae make changes in the oak's acorn to produce real honey under pressure to ultimately force the immature acorn from its cup. I guess a film of Live Oak Honey got across the cap after it expelled it's acorn, and it just kept building pressure to make this bubble. It's not a one-shot - I've seen a few of these. It's a bubble of spun-honey! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2012)

If I'm wrong about this, please let me know!
CommonNames: Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Honey
ScientificName: Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c909d_c909-021.jpg
Notes:
7967166262 "Live Oak honey" - sounds like an oxymoron if you've ever nibbled an acorn, but here it is! It really does taste sweet, but I've only nibbled.

This is one of our most interesting insect galls, though it doesn't have any visible structure like the galls I showed yesterday. I believe this is the gall (or at least a sign of the gall) of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis or kin) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2012)

The gall wasp larvae is making changes in the oak's acorn to produce real honey under pressure that will ultimately force the immature acorn from its cup. The lower acorn is about to go. Most of our acorns go this way, prematurely. There's an ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on acorn enjoying a sweet treat. The oaks are full of buzzing bees and wasps.

If I'm wrong about this, please let me know!
Locality:
family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on acorn enjoying a sweet treat. The oaks are full of buzzing bees and wasps.

If I'm wrong about this
CommonNames: Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; ant

File.Name: c909e_c909-016.jpg
Notes: 7967050654 This is a rather large (but not giant) Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera. It's unusually attractive. The legs go on a long ways from the edge of my photo. It's perched in the fold of a dusty leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2012)

File.Name: c909f_c909-014.jpg
Notes: 7966978956 This is another odd-looking net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. I believe it's in the family Issidae, genus Neaethus. This one is perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Soon after the sun got to it, it rolled under the stem. They have amazing eyes - like most hoppers that have to navigate on the fly. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2012)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Neaethus; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Neaethus; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: c909g_c909-002.jpg
Notes:
7966882804 Strange bedfellows...

I found this Orb-Weaver spider in the family Araneidae curled up with a piece of string and a cactus spine today. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2012)

File.Name: c909h_c908-009.jpg
Notes:
7966773936 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - on an interesting new flower of late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's getting nudged out by a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

The beautiful Stephanomeria flowers finish by mid-morning and get droopy. I'll have a better photo tomorrow. This is the first Mormon Metalmark butterfly I've seen in the mountains, though I found them first down by the river.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Honey Bee
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c910a_c908-006.jpg
Notes:
7974860579 This is a handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a new late-season flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plant is an interesting perennial that grows on rocky banks - often road cuts - in our mountains. It flowers at a time when the insects need it, at the end of our long dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)

The beautiful flowers open early and finish by mid-morning, and get droopy. I got this photo just in time. One of these days, I'll get out of the canyon early enough to catch them fully open.
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

File.Name: c910b_c826-016.jpg
Notes:
7974833973 Just because a plant is rare doesn't mean it's beautiful.

These are late-season flowers of the uncommon (in our mountains) Rough-leaved Aster - Aster radulinus in the Asteraceae plant family. This native plant is growing - and flowering! - on bone dry soil in a place that was cleared for a firebreak a few years ago. The leaves are crunchy-dry, but it has still managed a few flowers in our summer-dry climate. I only know it from near this place on the Pass. It's a fire-follower plant, but in this case clearing the chaparral down to bare ground was good enough to give them a place. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2012)

File.Name: c910c_c910-006.jpg
Notes:
7974769030 This is the small larvae of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) in the insect order Neuroptera. If you look close, you can see its impressive mandibles! I found it this morning on a hairy leaf and stem of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Labiatae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2012)

This was my only keeper photo today. It was breezy, I had obligations in town, and I'm feeling a bit under the weather.

File.Name: c910d_c908-026.jpg
Notes: 7974732335 Here is the surprisingly beautiful gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls - all galls - have been scarce this year. Maybe the late-season rains interfered with the wasps mating? (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2012)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: c910e_c908-019.jpg
Notes:
7974711832 This is a real "oak apple" gall - the gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera) growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It's about the size of a golf ball. There are critters living in there!

Many (including this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. I find them fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.
