File.Name: c910f_c907-040.jpg
Notes:
7974654022 Here is a beautiful ripening fruit of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The trees are related to avocado, and it show in the fruit, which look like little avocado "cukes". (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2011)

Have you ever accidentally eaten the "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so - and the fruit are even more so than the leaves. But hidden inside the fruit is a real nut which is delicious when dark roasted - rich, almost like bitter chocolate or coffee. I'll post a recipe here when the season comes.

If you can't wait, see this YouTube video!

File.Name: c910g_c909-004.jpg
Notes: 7974605840 Here are pretty new flowers and fruit of non-native Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa in the Solanaceae) family) growing in the garden. The fruit of this plant are the essential ingredient in chile verde and much of my favorite food. The green fruit hang from the plant in those neat little paper lanterns. I suppose we originally planted this deliberately, but it's been coming up as a volunteer for as long as I can remember. Does that mean it's naturalized? (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2012)

File.Name: c910h_c901-048.jpg
Notes: 7974591858 This is a different little "looper" type caterpillar (Lepidoptera) for some kind of moth. It's standing at attention on a flower head of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family by our mailbox. It's doesn't have the fancy decoration of the caterpillar in my [Previous] photo, but it's still well-camouflage with light and rusty colors to match the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2012)

File.Name: c911a_c911-019.jpg
Notes:
7978317093 It's a dirty job, but someone's got to do it!

This is a beautiful pollen-dusted Wasp (order Hymenoptera) - I guess in the family Vespidae. It's working flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash of the river today. I really like its eyes. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 September 2012)

File.Name: c911b_c911-027.jpg
Notes:
7978253456 The colors of summer...

Here are a mating pair of "True Bugs" in the order Hemiptera - maybe in the family Lygaeidae? They are having their nuptials on flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash of the river today. I had to over-expose the flowers a bit to bring out the colors in the shade. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 September 2012)

File.Name: c911c_c911-010.jpg
Notes: 7978213055 Here is another photo of the same Silver Argiope (Argiope argenatata) spider I've been showing in older photos. You can see the impressive horns on the back of its abdomen. You can also see a bit of its "stabilimentum" - the zigzag structures of webbing that looks like cigarette smoke in an old film noir movie. Many of these Argiope spiders have a face on their backs. This one seems particularly glaring. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 September 2012)

File.Name: c911d_c911-007.jpg
Notes: 7978193501 This is a perfect net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. I believe it's in the family Issidae, genus Neaethus. This one is perched on the rim of a green flower pot in the garden. These critters are small, only about 1/4 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2012)

File.Name: c911e_c911-055.jpg
Notes:
7978153544 Here are small Lepiota mushrooms growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family in our garden. There are many species and I won't try to guess, but they all have a cap with a dark center that breaks into scales as it expands, making it an easy genus to recognize. The one on the left (that I pulled up) seems to be growing from an acorn cup. They have free white gills and spores, no volva, and the remnants of a ring on the stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2012)

I hope this is an omen for a good rain year!

File.Name: c911f_c911-036.jpg
Notes: 7978133393 These are new flowers of the native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's another of the late-season composite flowers that grows along the river and on rocky banks (like road cuts) in the mountains. These late-season composite flowers keep the insects alive. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 September 2012)

File.Name: c911g_c911-012.jpg
Notes: 7978093426 These are flowers of native Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash along the river. There's a little bee or fly in the air above the flowers. They don't draw the pollinators like Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum), but they do get a few. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 September 2012)
ScientificName: Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Asteraceae; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Asteraceae; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: c911h_c911-014.jpg
Notes:
7978061470 These are flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash of the river today. It's my favorite late-summer bloomer that is usually full of wasps and flies. The shrub has no leaves to speak of by the time it blooms at the end of summer, hence its common name "broom". It's a wonderful native plant that keeps the insects alive when they need help. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 September 2012)

It seems like a paradox, but the river wash is the driest and most desert-like environment we have in the coastal mountains.

File.Name: c912a_c912-044.jpg
Notes:
7981352261 This is a beautiful Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on flowers of Dodder - probably California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae plant family growing on non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. I'm glad *something* gets the fennel! The butterfly sat so still, I thought it was captured by a spider, but no, it flew way. Maybe the dodder flowers are narcotic for these butterflies? (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 September 2012)

It was gloomy and drizzly at home this morning, so I went up the road to the higher mountains to get above it. It turned out to be a beautiful photo day with little wind!
CommonNames: Gray Hairstreak; Dodder; California Dodder; Fennel
ScientificName: Strymon melinus; Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: c912b_c912-042.jpg
Notes: 7981320005 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - working an interesting new flower of composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you look at the flower on the left, you can see that it has one main series of phyllaries subtended by a few outer phyllaries - distinctive for this genus. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 September 2012)

If you look at the flower bud in the foreground, you can see that it has one main series of phyllaries subtended by a few outer phyllaries - distinctive for this genus. Notes: 7981216747 These are pretty new flowers of native composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a wonderful native shrub that flowers in the summer-dry mountains when most flowers are done. There's a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the front flower. See my [Newer] photo. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 September 2012)

File.Name: c912e_c912-003.jpg
Notes:
7981196900 These are new late-season flowers of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. There's a little beetle in the order Coleoptera on the flower in front. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2012)

I got this photo early in the morning on a cloudy day. The plant is an interesting perennial that grows on rocky banks - often road cuts - in our mountains. It flowers at a time when the insects need it, at the end of our long dry summer.
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; beetle

File.Name: c912f_c912-063.jpg
Notes:
7981156153 This is the whole plant of a native Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a bit past it's prime, but still quite attractive. See my [Older] photo for the flowers. It's growing by the radio towers near La Cumbre Peak. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 September 2012)

I know, I have a problem over-exposing yellow flowers. It happens when I trust the viewfinder and don't check the individual color histograms.

File.Name: c912g_c912-065.jpg
Notes:
7981142844 These are not-so-new flowers of native Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a beautiful composite shrub that only grows higher in the mountains, near the summits of the Santa Ynez Range.

I know, these late-summer composite flowers all look the same up close! See my [Newer] photo.
Locality: Ericameria arborescens) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a beautiful composite shrub that only grows higher in the mountains

File.Name: c912h_c912-024.jpg
Notes: 7981122761 Here are the characteristic flower heads of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family - still going strong higher in the mountains than home. They are growing in a little sandstone cave. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 September 2012)

File.Name: c913a_c913-007.jpg
Notes:
7984486702 This is a brilliant female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a new flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden, with the sun behind. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2012)

I had a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting tonight, and got home late. Two photos will do just fine tonight.

It's a good night to remember Victor Jara, a wonderful singer-songwriter-activist who was tortured and killed 39 years ago in the Estadio Chile (Chile Stadium - now renamed the Estadio VÌctor Jara) during the September 11 military coup which brought down the elected government of Salvador Allende in Chile. It's "the other 9/11". His death meant a lot to me way back in 1973 when I was a young grad student.

A good place to begin with Victor Jara's music is Te Recuerdo Amanda.

I remember you Amanda
when the streets were wet,
running to the factory
where Manuel worked.
With your wide smile,
the rain in your hair
nothing else mattered,
you were going to meet him.

With him, with him, with him...
five minutes only,
all of your life
in five minutes
The siren is sounding
time to go back to work.
And as you walk
you light up everything
those five minutes
have made you flower.

where Manuel worked.
With your wide smile,
the rain in your hair
nothing else mattered,
you were going to meet him.

With him, with him, with him...
and he took to the mountains to fight
He had never hurt a fly
And he took to the mountains to fight
And in five minutes
it was all wiped out
The siren is sounding
time to go back to work
Many will not go back
one of them Manuel

I remember you Amanda
when the streets were wet,
running to the factory
where Manuel worked.

File.Name: c913b_c913-002.jpg
Notes:
7984437993 These are ripening berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family hanging down the retaining wall in our garden. They look so good... but they have a horrible bitter taste. The plants have opposite leaves, with the uppermost pair fused distinctively at the base to surround the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2012)

Most web sites just repeat the claim that the berries are "edible but bitter" - isn't that an oxymoron? Even the birds seem to ignore them until there's nothing else.

File.Name: c914a_c914-012.jpg
Notes:
7987152106 This is a tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly resting on a flower head of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. It's the first I've seen this year, though I saw several of them today. It's quite distinctive. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2012)

Oh lordy, it was hot today. We got to 101∞ today on the porch at home, and 105∞ at the river where I was today. The forecast is for more of the same for the next two days. I'm ready for a change!

File.Name: c914b_c914-011.jpg
Notes: 7987115725 I believe this is a pretty Lygaeid Bug in the family Lygaeidae of the order Hemiptera. It's working new flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2012)

File.Name: c914c_c914-010.jpg
Notes: 7987099674 The bug sure looks like a Bagrada Bug (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera, though it only seems to have 4 antenna segments instead of the expected 5 in the Pentatomidae. Maybe I'm not counting right. There's a mating pair in the background. These bugs are all the over flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2012)
ScientificName: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c914d_c914-044.jpg
Notes: 7987038943 These are little mating Datura Beetles (my name) in the insect order Coleoptera. They are pretty common on Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure they are genus Lema in the family Chrysomelidae. I got this photo on the shady side of a flower today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2012)

File.Name: c914e_c914-037.jpg
Notes:
7987011197 This is a magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family on a dry flat by the river. The new flowers make perfect mandalas as they unwind. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2012)

Actually, Toloache is probably non-native too - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

File.Name: c914f_c914-030.jpg
Notes: 7986996761 This is a new flower of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family. You can see the goodies inside, like one of those fancy easter eggs that you look into. I'm always surprised to find these flowers at the end of our summer-dry season, but here they are. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2012)

File.Name: c914g_c914-016.jpg
Notes: 7986969777 This is a new flower cluster of Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family, shot against the blue sky. It has willow-like leaves, but composite flowers. They have late-summer colors. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2012)

File.Name: c914h_c914-001.jpg
Notes:
7986946312 This is a brilliant male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a new flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden, with the sun behind. It's almost the perfect match for my [Previous] photo of the female. You can see the outline of the dog face on it's wings. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2012)

These Colias eurydice butterflies are probably the most common butterflies in our garden right now.
ScientificName: Colias eurydice; Tithonia rotundifolia; Colias eurydice
associatedTaxa: Colias eurydice; Tithonia rotundifolia; Colias eurydice

File.Name: c915a_c915-001.jpg
Notes:
7990284105 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae guarding her nest (out of frame, down below) on a still-flowering plant of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see her mighty chelicerae with fangs at the bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2012)

"It's Too Darn Hot!"
(And listen to Ella sing it!)

It really is too damn hot. This is the third time I've written this - I keep pressing some darn key combination that makes it all disappear. I blame the weather. It was "only" 99∞ at home today, but a whopping 108∞ at the river. I'm ready for a change, but probably not tomorrow which promises more of the same.
Locality:
And listen to Ella sing it!)

File.Name: c915b_c915-025.jpg
Notes:
7990192081 This is a remarkable tiny Moth (Ledpidoptera) sitting on a small flower of a native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's definitely a "microlepidoptera" - only a few millimeters long! It looks metallic silver, though my photo shows it as gray. It has remarkable spiny hind legs which it raises in alarm - I guess to frighten off potential predators like me. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2012)

*Maybe* this is a Twirler Moth in the family Gelechiidae?

File.Name: c915c_c915-010.jpg
Notes: 7990151689 These are pretty new flowers of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains. It's related to wetland plants, but this one picks the bone-dry late-summer soil to flower. Look at the flower on the right - there's a tiny diurnal Moth (Ledpidoptera). See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2012)

File.Name: c915d_c915-018.jpg
Notes:
7990127119 We do have "fall colors" in our part of California! These are brilliant leaves of deciduous native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family turning red before they fall to the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2012)

"Leaves of three, let it be.
Leaves of four, eat some more!"
- Homer Simpson

File.Name: c915e_c915-015.jpg
Notes: 7990105872 These are new feathery seed plumes of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family, shot against the clear blue sky today. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2012)

File.Name: c915f_c914-032.jpg
Notes:
7990059216 These are new feathery seed plumes of native Creek Clematis aka Yerba de Chivato (Clematis ligusticifolia) in the Ranunculaceae) plant family. They are beautiful when they catch the sun just right. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2012)

The flowers are smaller than the earlier-flowering Chaparral Clematis (C. lasiantha), and they flower later in the year. They usually have 5 or 7 leaflets on the stems. I often find it hanging down from trees, but this one was growing low over shrubs by the river.
CommonNames: Creek Clematis; Yerba de Chivato; Chaparral Clematis

File.Name: c915g_c907-044.jpg
Notes:
7990033390 This are new flower buds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - but I don't think they will flower in this very dry year. The flower buds look dry and scarious up close. See my [Previous] photo.

This humble shrub is usually a very productive nectar source that many late-season insects depend on. What a (small) environmental disaster for local critters that it's not flowering this year because of low rainfall last winter, just 1/2 normal. On the other hand, most of the insects that depend on it are already gone. It's an interesting rain year, though tough for photographers. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2012)

File.Name: c915h_c915-006.jpg
Notes:
7989973876 This native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family looks all poised to flower - but I don't think it's going to happen in the very dry year. The flower buds look dry and scarious up close. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2012)

This humble shrub is usually a very productive nectar source that many late-season insects depend on. What a (small) environmental disaster for local critters that it's not flowering this year because of low rainfall last winter, just 1/2 normal. On the other hand, most of the insects that depend on it are already gone. It's an interesting rain year!
Locality: small) environmental disaster for local critters that it's not flowering this year because of low rainfall last winter

File.Name: c916a_c916-001.jpg
Notes:
7994496894 Be careful little wing, some of these summer flowers have fangs!

This small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's just waiting on flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden until prey comes along. See my [Previous photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2012)

It was cooler today, only in the low 90s, but it was still very hot at the river, in the 100s. Tomorrow is forecast to be 10 degrees cooler. I'm so glad this heat wave is winding down.
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Spearmint
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Menthe spicata
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Menthe spicata

File.Name: c916b_c916-004.jpg
Notes:
7994433660 This small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica), has captured a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2012)

Nature is tough like that, especially as fall approaches and all the summer pollinator insects will disappear in one way or another.

File.Name: c916c_c916-014.jpg
Notes:
7994361537 "The exception proves the rule..."

Yesterday I said that native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family wasn't flowering this year, but I stand corrected. These are female pistillate flowers on a shrub in the canyon.

But look above the left-most flower to spot the little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). I was actually focused on a flower off-photo to the right, and didn't notice the predator until I got the photo on my computer screen. Sure, this is a pretty deep crop. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2012)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug

associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: c916d_c916-018.jpg
Notes:
7994315180 "The exception proves the rule..."

Yesterday I said that native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family wasn't flowering this year, but I stand corrected. Here are male, staminate flowers on a stem of Coyote Bush that have real flowers. I had to look close for these, and note the dry and scarious flower heads that won't flower above. They are not flowering very much, but there are a few. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2012)

These native shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants.

File.Name: c916e_c916-010.jpg
Notes: 7994247749 I'm pretty sure this is a Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly working an orange (oh so orange) flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. It's smaller and darker than the Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly, which we also have at home. Lovely names for these dark moth-like butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2012)
CommonNames: Funereal Duskywing; Mexican Sunflower; Mournful Duskywing
ScientificName: Erynnis funeralis; Tithonia rotundifolia; Erynnis tristis
associatedTaxa: Erynnis funeralis; Tithonia rotundifolia; Erynnis tristis

File.Name: c916f_c916-038.jpg
Notes: 7994216611 Here are lots of Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii?) in the order Homoptera lined up like cattle at a feeding trough on the orange cultivar of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Maybe they are native to the Mediterranean? I don't know how they find the garden milkweeds every year, but they do. They're pretty, so we leave them be. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2012)

File.Name: c916g_c916-009.jpg
Notes: 7994163882 I guess this is the pupal nest of a Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin. It's stuck on the concrete black retaining wall above our garden. That little hole in the nest is intriguing - you want to look inside, like one of those fancy easter eggs that you look into. It's too small for an exit hole. I guess some parasitic critter got inside. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2012)

File.Name: c916h_c916-030.jpg
Notes:
7994096829 These are pretty new flowers of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. (I think it's got its real name back in the new Jepson Manual, and I'm glad. I love saying the name - "cor-e-thro-gyn-e-fil-ag-in-i-folia" - it kind of rolls off the tongue!)

This is another one of the late-summer native plants that always surprise me by flowering at the harshest time of our summer-dry climate. Note the small Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the left-most flower. Last year they flowered into January. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2012)
ScientificName: Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c917a_c917-003.jpg
Notes: 7998278574 This is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly - the first I've recognized this year. It's perched on a Zinnia flower in the garden. If you look close, you can see that it has five eyes under its hind wings, different from the American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterflies I've been finding with just two eyes. Some years, we have huge spring migrations of these butterflies from Mexico. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2012)

File.Name: c917b_c917-005.jpg
Notes: 7998236215 This is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly - the first I've recognized this year. It's perched on a Zinnia flower in the garden. If you look close, you can see that it has five eyes under its hind wings, different from the American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterflies I've been finding with just two eyes. Some years, we have huge spring migrations of these butterflies from Mexico. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2012)

File.Name: c917c_c917-016.jpg
Notes:
7998136493 Thanks again Alice. I see from the BugGuide photos that this is indeed the California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) and not genus (Scudderia). Well, It's been a year since I've seen them, and I forget. This katydid has wider, more leaf-like wings. The Scudderia wings are narrower and straighter. I'll have to rethink the nymphs. Original comment:
I believe this is the Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) or kin in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It has beautiful leaf-like green wings. This one is resting on a blade of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family. I'm not sure what that brown spot on the top of its thorax is. I suspect it's a gland that has something to do with mating. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2012)

This is the adult that comes from the beautiful nymphs I've been photographing all summer - like here and here.
CommonNames: California Angle-winged Katydid ; Fork-tailed Bush Katydid; Giant Rye Grass
ScientificName: Microcentrum californicum; Scudderia; Scudderia furcata; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Leymus condensatus; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Microcentrum californicum; Scudderia; Scudderia furcata; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Leymus condensatus; Poaceae

File.Name: c917d_c917-029.jpg
Notes:
7998101068 I believe this is an adult female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) huddled in a leaf in the garden. Crickets sleep all day and work the night shift. They are the songsters of our woods in the fall, though the females don't join in the jam. I guess that this is the Western Tree Cricket (Oecanthus californicus or kin) that make a steady trill, unlike the Snowy Tree Crickets (Oecanthus niveus group) which pulse. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2012)

I don't much like the composition of this photo, but here it is.
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Western Tree Cricket; Snowy Tree Crickets
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Oecanthus californicus; Oecanthus niveus
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Oecanthus californicus; Oecanthus niveus

File.Name: c917e_c917-032.jpg
Notes:
7998044205 This is a remarkable Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the dark cone of a non-native composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. It's body is tiny, but the front legs have a 1/2 inch span! Candidates at BugGuide are genus Mecaphesa and Misumenoides. Maybe it's an immature form? (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2012)

The flowers are interesting too. The cones are almost black. You can see separate male staminate flowers and female pistillate flowers dusted with pollen. These Black-Eyed Susans are not native in California, but they are native back east.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy; Black-Eyed Susans

File.Name: c917f_c917-023.jpg
Notes:
7997977691 This appears to be a very different Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the dark cone of a non-native composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. It's body is tiny, but the front legs have a 1/2 inch span! Note that this one is missing its second long leg on the right. That's an occupational hazard for these spiders that grab prey and try to hold on. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2012)

Candidates at BugGuide are genus Mecaphesa and Misumenoides. Maybe it's an immature form?
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: c917g_c917-020.jpg
Notes:
7997940370 This large white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is guarding the throat of a flower of Mexican Giant Hyssop (Agastache mexicana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the garden. Note the little ant on the left. I mostly see hummingbirds working the flowers, so I'm not sure what this spider is waiting for. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2012)

This has been a good garden flower for us, with hummingbird flowers all summer long. It's a relative of the Nettleleaf Giant Hyssop (Agastache urticifolia) that grows in the mountain meadows of Oregon. See my photo.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Mexican Giant Hyssop; Nettleleaf Giant Hyssop
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Agastache mexicana; Lamiaceae; Agastache urticifolia
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Agastache mexicana; Lamiaceae; Agastache urticifolia

File.Name: c917h_c917-035.jpg
Notes:
7997893108 This rather large yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has captured a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera for lunch. This action is taking place on an annual Marigold (Tagetes) flower in the garden. If you look close, you can see that the spider is disabled - missing an important front leg. That's an occupational hazard for these spiders that grab and try to hold on. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2012)

Thus fall the summer pollinators as we slide into autumn.

File.Name: c918a_c918-043.jpg
Notes:
8001757869 This is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. It came down and drove away the Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly just before I got the photo. I don't know who's more surprised! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2012)

As a True Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae subfamily, It is perched on just four legs, with her two front legs folded up in front in T-rex fashion. Many of our most distinctive butterflies share this trait of perching on four legs with two in the air.

I went to the river today. It was breezy and still hot, with a high of 96∞ when I was there tromping around on the hot, dry river bed. We're just a few days away from the Autumnal Equinox. I hope it will start to cool down soon.
CommonNames: Common Buckeye; Scale Broom; Mormon Metalmark; True Brushfoot
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Apodemia mormo; Nymphalidae
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Apodemia mormo; Nymphalidae

File.Name: c918b_c918-037.jpg
Notes: 8001721471 I guess this is a Cardinal Meadowhawk (Sympetrum illotum) dragonfly - family Libellulidae, suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata. I got this photo by the river today, but this critter was attached to a small pond cut off from the main pool. It is rather small, and perches with its wings forward.

File.Name: c918c_c918-034.jpg
Notes: 8001669836 I believe this is a beautiful Red Rock Skimmer (Paltothemis lineatipes) dragonfly - family Libellulidae, suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata. It's perched on alkali-encrusted rocks near the river that is going dry. It really wanted to perch flat on the rocks near the edge of the water. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2012)

File.Name: c918d_c918-047.jpg
Notes:
8001638861 This is a large Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. It's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long, bumblebee size. I found this one working the late flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2012)

These flies are camera shy, and they seem to prefer the top-most flowers of the shrub, so they can see you coming. It was windy today, which didn't help! I'll keep trying for a better photo.
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c918e_c918-003.jpg
Notes:
8001611501 I found this not-quite-honey-bee-size Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden this morning. It's definitely not a honey bee. It has beautiful golden silky hairs on its legs. I really want to learn more about bees. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2012)

This is my only photo from the garden today. The rest of my photos are from the river.

File.Name: c918f_c918-008.jpg
Notes: 8001539174 I guess these are black Blister Beetles in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera. They are mating on late-season flowers of native Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash along the river. Note that the upper one is feeding while "doing it". I've seen this before. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2012)

Notes: 8001510819 Here is a Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) perched on a stem of native composite Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. It appears to have blue legs, but I don't think it looks like the Blue-winged Grasshopper (Leprus intermedius). I might be wrong. Alice? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2012)

File.Name: c918h_c918-024.jpg
Notes: 8001449390 Hot and dry as its been, there are still new shrubs starting to flower. Here are new native composite flowers of Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) just starting to appear on the dry flood plain by the river. It's got to be one tough shrub to start flowering now at the end of our summer-dry Mediterranean climate! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2012)

File.Name: c919a_c919-034.jpg
Notes:
8005005925 This is a remarkable Feather-legged Fly (genus Trichopoda) in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera - maybe species Trichopoda subdivisa? You can see the feathery wings on its rear legs. It's working flowers of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family on the mostly-dry river bed of the Santa Ynez River. It has really large "calypters". Compare the photos at BugGuide. See my [previous] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2012)

Fennel is a bad weed in our mountains, but the native insects seem to like it.

Notes: 8004938638 This is a remarkable Feather-legged Fly (genus Trichopoda) in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera - maybe species Trichopoda subdivisa? It's working flowers of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family on the mostly-dry river bed of the Santa Ynez River. In this photo, you can see that the wings on its legs really are feather-like. You can also see its proboscis if you look close. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2012)

File.Name: c919c_c919-022.jpg
Notes:
8004884213 This is a bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera on a new flower head of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a fly-sized fly, but it looks very different up close. The bristles make the difference. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2012)

Baccharis plummerae has similar flowers to the more familiar native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis), but it's different. It's endemic to central California, but it's quite common - both along the river and in the mountains.
CommonNames: Tachinid Fly; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush

File.Name: c919d_c919-002.jpg
Notes: 8004865335 This large white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is watching a patch of flower head of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family that still has real flowers to attract pollinators. The spider seems dusted with scales - maybe it's had a recent encounter with a butterfly? (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2012)

File.Name: c919e_c919-024.jpg
Notes:
8004811549 This is looking straight down on a Silver Argiope (Argiope argenatata) spider in the family Araneidae wrapping its prey. You can see the remarkable horns on its abdomen, and also a bit of its "stabilimentum" - the zigzag structures of thick webbing that looks like cigarette smoke in an old film noir movie. I found this spider in a flowering plant of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2012)

I got this photo in deep shade with wind, and I know the white balance is off. Shade and wind is not a good combination for photos, but this is still pretty cool.
CommonNames: Silver Argiope; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis

File.Name: c919f_c919-023.jpg
Notes: 8004794517 I believe these are pretty Lygaeid Bugs in the family Lygaeidae of the insect order Hemiptera. They are mating on a stem of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2012)
CommonNames: Lygaeid Bugs; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis

File.Name: c919g_c919-038.jpg
Notes:
8004751031 Maybe this an immature Western Side-blotched Lizard (Uta stansburiana elegans) on a hot rock by the Santa Ynez River today. I can see a dark patch behind its right foreleg. These lizards are incredibly fast and hard to capture with a camera.

On the other hand, it does seem to have a "third eye" on the back of its head, so maybe it's just a different-looking Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis)? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2012)
CommonNames: Western Side-blotched Lizard; Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard

File.Name: c919h_c919-039.jpg
Notes: 8004721605 Someone with way too much time on their hands has been stacking rocks on the dry river bed of the Santa Ynez River in one of the places I go. Is it a Buddhist thing? Or a yoga thing? Or just a balance thing? Very cool! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2012)

File.Name: c920a_c920-003.jpg
Notes:
8008291950 This is looking into the new flower on the San Pedro Cactus (Trichocereus Pachanoi or kin) in the garden, growing against the sunny side of our house. The flower is the size of my open hand, native to the Andes. It looks like something that belongs under the ocean. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2012)

We've had these cacti for 20+ years, but it has only flowered once before, in late August 2005 - which was a very wet year, and this is a very dry year, go figure. We had three buds, and this flower was the first to open. They are all open tonight, but I haven't seen any bats or moths visiting. This cactus grows as a tall column, 10 or 12 feet tall. I had to get my tripod up on a ladder for this photo!

"San Pedro" is Saint Peter who holds the keys. Try a web search for more info, it's an interesting story. For me it's just an amazing seldom-seen flower.

We had a well-attended Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society lecture tonight on "Bats on the Central Coast" by Paul Collins who is Curator of Vertebrate Zoology at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. Fascinating stuff, including listening to real bats with an Anabat detector that frequency-shifts the ultrasonic echolocation calls of bats. I got home late, and these two photos will do just fine tonight.

File.Name: c920b_c920-010.jpg
Notes:
8008259464 This is a flowering San Pedro Cactus (Trichocereus Pachanoi or kin) in the garden, growing against the sunny side of our house. The flower is the size of my open hand, native to the Andes. It has only flowered once before at our house, in late August 2005 - which was a very wet year, and this is a very dry year, go figure. We've had three buds, and they are all open flowers tonight, but I haven't seen any bats or moths visiting. This cactus grows as a tall column, 10 or 12 feet tall. I had to get my tripod up on a ladder for these photos! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2012)

"San Pedro" is Saint Peter who holds the keys. Try a web search for more info, it's an interesting story. For me it's just an amazing seldom-seen flower.

File.Name: c921a_c921-012.jpg
Notes:
8010824517 This is a beautiful Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. You can see just a bit of its dark gray upper wings as this butterfly rubs its wings back and forth in a characteristic way. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 September 2012)

The Autumnal Equinox happens tomorrow morning at 7:49 am. Happy equinox! The moon is getting higher, but the sun is getting lower - until the solstice, when they reverse direction. I hate to see the sun leave. Our house on the north-facing slopes gets dark as winter approaches. But it will bring mushrooms!
CommonNames: Gray Hairstreak; Scale Broom; Autumnal Equinox

File.Name: c921b_c921-010.jpg
Notes:
8010803040 This Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) has blue legs. A moment ago, it was munching on the flower of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. And a moment later, it was gone! Note the little yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) just above the grasshopper who knows when a prey is too big to handle.

Maybe it's a another "Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper" (Melanoplus cinereus)?
Locality: Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. And a moment later

File.Name: c921c_c921-021.jpg
Notes: 8010762746 This is a rather small but striking Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) perched on a sandstone rock on the dry wash by the river. It really is small, 1/2 inch or less long. It's rather pretty, but it didn't like me looking and it hopped away before I could get a better photo. Alice? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 September 2012)

File.Name: c921d_c921-013.jpg
Notes:
8010743170 This is another Silver Argiope (Argiope argenatata) spider in the family Araneidae wrapping its prey - no doubt an unfortunate Yellow Jacket type wasp in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera. You can see the remarkable horns on the spider's abdomen, and also a bit of its "stabilimentum" - the zigzag structures of thick webbing that looks like cigarette smoke in an old film noir movie.

Counting its pairs of legs from the head at the bottom, it's wrapping its prey with the 1st and 3rd legs. What a delicate touch! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 September 2012)

File.Name: c921e_c920-016.jpg
Notes: 8010709564 This is a small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It sure seems to be sticking its proboscis into a female flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a mighty predator, but I wonder if they turn to flowers when insect prey are scarce? I've seen this before. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2012)

File.Name: c921f_c921-035.jpg
Notes:
8010685598 This are remarkable summer flowers of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family still flowering growing on a dry flat near the river. The flowers look like little mousetraps ready to spring. The plants have an intense vinegar smell, like vinegar chips. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 September 2012)

This little Trichostema lanceolatum is a cousin of the Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) shrubs in our mountains.
ScientificName: Trichostema lanceolatum; Lamiaceae; Trichostema lanceolatum; Trichostema lanatum
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanceolatum; Lamiaceae; Trichostema lanceolatum; Trichostema lanatum

File.Name: c921g_c920-027.jpg
Notes:
8010631310 We had a well-attended Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society lecture last night (I'm on the board) on "Bats on the Central Coast" by Paul Collins who is Curator of Vertebrate Zoology at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. This is Paul's poster that shows the bats of central California. These are real specimens pinned onto a poster board. (Sedgwick Reserve, Santa Ynez Valley, 20 September 2012)

After the lecture, we walked to the pond at the UC Sedgwick Preserve and used theAnabat detector to listen to real bats. It's a device that frequency-shifts the ultrasonic echolocation calls of bats down to audible frequencies. I didn't see a thing, but we "heard" three kinds of bat - The "Big Brown Bat", a little Myotis, and the "Mexican Free-tailed Bat". I admit, it seemed like magic to me!

Anabat detectors work great, but they are pricey. There are plans for a "Simple Bat Detector" on the web. I want one!

File.Name: c921h_c921-004.jpg
Notes:
8010590709 I can't resist one more photo of a new flower on the San Pedro Cactus (Trichocereus Pachanoi or kin) in the garden, growing against the sunny side of our house. The flower is the size of my open hand, native to the Andes. It looks like something that belongs under the ocean. This is the flower at the top of the columnar stalk, 12+ feet tall. I had to get my tripod up on a ladder for this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2012)

Yesterday's flower is closed today, but might open again tonight. They don't last long. I still haven't seen any bats or moths visiting the flowers. It will be interesting to see if they set seeds. See my [Previous] photo for more info.

File.Name: c922a_c922-037.jpg
Notes:
8014009216 I'm pretty sure this is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly, in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata. It's a dragonfly that can handle dry conditions, as I don't think there is a drop of standing water in our creek. It perched on a dead stem in the woods, and posed for me! (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)

I've found this dragonfly several time in midwinter, for example this photo from 12 December 2011. I guess it overwinters in our canyon.

Today was an interesting weather day - mostly cloudy and cooler than its been, but more humid. The clouds seem to be coming all the way from south of Hawaii. The clouds brought gentle light and a bit of breeze.

File.Name: c922b_c922-032.jpg
Notes:
8013937618 This is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae guarding her nest in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)

A week ago, rjadams55 posted a comment to another photo of this spider to the effect that these spider can "spray venom as a defensive response (particularly females guarding their egg sac)." At first I thought he was putting me on - it's an old naturalist tradition! But a quick web search proved him right! I suppose I should worry more about my camera lens than my eyes.
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c922c_c922-030.jpg
Notes:
8013881852 I found these tiny insect eggs on a stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have no idea what critters will emerge from these beautiful eggs. They look like perfect little stash boxes. Really tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012) See my [Previous] photo.

These new insect eggs are maybe the reason I find adult Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae, Diptera) in the fall!
CommonNames: insect eggs; Coyote Bush; Tachinid Flies

File.Name: c922d_c922-009.jpg
Notes:
8013842963 This is an impressive bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera on new staminate flower heads of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It seems to be missing pieces of its front and rear legs - maybe the work of an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera? (See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)

Adult Tachinid Flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!
CommonNames: Tachinid Fly; Coyote Bush; Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: c922e_c922-019.jpg
Notes: 8013824542 Here is another photo of the nymph of an Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera. It has captured a Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a just-starting-to-flower native Coyote Bush Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It seems to be carrying the bee by its leg with its mighty proboscis. Nature is sometimes tough like that. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Bee; Coyote Bush; Coyote Bush

File.Name: c922f_c922-018.jpg
Notes:
8013716399 This is the nymph (barely) of an Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera. It has captured a Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a just-starting-to-flower native Coyote Bush Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see its mighty proboscis. I say it's "barely a nymph" because it only has "trainer wings" but hasn't yet got its real wings. It has one more molt to go. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)

The nymphs look a lot like the Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) nymphs in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. They have the same speckly legs. But these critters are definitely predators, not plant eaters like katydids.

Notes: 8013690685 I think this is a different net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera - it seems smaller and darker than the critters I posted recently, for example here. I believe it's in the family Issidae, genus Neaethus. It's tiny. This one is perched on foliage of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)

File.Name: c922h_c922-025.jpg
Notes:
8013665006 This seems appropriate for the first day of autumn - ripe nuts on native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family growing in our mountains. The nuts are small and taste rather bland, but I'll bet they would be good roasted. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)

The flowers of this native shrub appear in spring and have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They seem almost too beautiful to be native chaparral plants. See my photo of the flowers.

File.Name: c923a_c923-019.jpg
Notes: 8017937174 I believe this is a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) working new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. The wasp has a muscular look (in a Batman-movie sort of way). It looks black at first, but you can see the iridescent blue sheen to it. They twitch their wings constantly. They hunt spiders to lay their eggs on, but I guess they visit flowers to feed. This one is quite small. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)

File.Name: c923b_c923-012.jpg
Notes: 8017868447 This is a Yellow Jacket wasp (family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera) taking apart a fallen California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. The katydid had leaf-like green wings. The empty abdomen suggest she was a female, and maybe she already laid her eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)

I believe these spiders are also color-shifters. This one is perched on late-summer flowers of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)
