File.Name: c923d_c923-001.jpg
Notes: 8017777222 I seem to have captured this fat Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the act of shifting color from white to yellow or vice-versa. It's perched on a Zinnia flower in the garden this morning. I'm confused. Are the big yellow crab spiders the same species as the big white crab spiders? Either way, it's impressive, and here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)

File.Name: c923e_c923-028.jpg
Notes: 8017739962 This alert-looking Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) has blue legs. It's hanging on a dead-looking stem of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Maybe it's a another "Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper" (Melanoplus cinereus)? (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae; Melanoplus cinereus
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae; Melanoplus cinereus

File.Name: c923f_c923-043.jpg
Notes: 8017699249 These are characteristic flat flower sprays of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family - still going strong in the mountains, even in this very dry summer. There are lots of little bees and even a grasshopper if you look very close! (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)

File.Name: c923g_c923-038.jpg
Notes:
8017606299 Here are ripe berries of the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass. They look like little gooseberries, but they have a horrible bitter taste.

Botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen in 1831 from the coast, but the inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) is much more common. But his named collection stands as official. The Santa Barbara variety has narrow rather than wide leaves, and orange rather than red berries. Maybe it's more than a varietal difference? (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; L. subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; L. subspicata; denudata

File.Name: c923h_c923-005.jpg
Notes:
8017581144 After the party...

This is a spent flower of San Pedro Cactus (Trichocereus Pachanoi or kin) in the garden. The enormous flowers seem to flower for just two nights and the day in between, and then they're done. I didn't see any bats or moths visit them, so it will be interesting to see if they develop fruit. See my photos of the open flowers here and [previous] to that one. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)

File.Name: c924a_c924-021.jpg
Notes:
8021868202 This is a beautiful Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's unusually dark. It's working a native composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family, less than a foot from the spider in my [Previous] photo. Be careful little wing, it's a wild world! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

The weather is expected to be cooler tomorrow - in the high 70s instead of the 90s. It's about time!
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Sawtooth Goldenbush
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: c924b_c924-017.jpg
Notes:
8021799391 This mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae on her nest has captured a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). This action is taking place on composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

This answers one question I've had - do these spider feed while the guard their nests? YES!
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Sawtooth Goldenbush
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: c924c_c924-054.jpg
Notes: 8021777282 This large yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has captured a Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly on a late-season flower of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. The predators are at work picking off the pollinators as we slide into autumn. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: c924d_c924-026.jpg
Notes:
8021743707 Here's a little drama taking place on a new flower of composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. There's a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) confronting a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae). Look on the right-most petal of the flower for the spider. The bee got away. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

This is the lowest elevation plant of of these flowers that I've found in the woods. Usually they grow higher in the mountains.
CommonNames: Bush Groundsel; Douglas' Groundsel; Wild Bee; Crab Spider
ScientificName: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Thomisidae

File.Name: c924e_c924-033.jpg
Notes: 8021691109 I guess this is a Thalassica Grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica) - it has light green wings when it takes to the air. If I'm right, it is a Band-winged Grasshopper (Oedipodinae) in the Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae) in the order Orthoptera. Alice? (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)
CommonNames: Thalassica Grasshopper; Band-winged Grasshopper; Short-Horned Grasshopper

File.Name: c924f_c924-052.jpg
Notes: 8021664064 I guess these mating bugs are the Bagrada Bug (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera. You can count five antennae segments. These are perched on a flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family that may or may not flower in this very dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)
ScientificName: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c924g_c924-012.jpg
Notes: 8021576242 I found this pretty Wasp in the order Hymenoptera in the garden this morning, perched awkwardly on a ceramic pot. It seems to have some trouble with its legs, but it flew away just fine. Maybe it's some sort of Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae)? I see something like this on the light shades and window screens at night. I dig the shadow. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

File.Name: c924h_c924-051.jpg
Notes:
8021531533 This is a tiny but pretty flower of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains - with seeds and seedpods. The seeds develop under the flowers in an inferior ovary that splits open to release seeds. It's related to wetland plants, but this one picks the bone-dry autumn soil in our mountains to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

I have a backlog of late-summer native flowers to show, but I keep finding new insects! I'll get to the flowers...

File.Name: c925a_c925-008.jpg
Notes:
8025321182 This is a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - I guess Spilomyia interrupta or kin. It has amazing patterned eyes. This one is perched on male staminate (failed) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - but there's a real flower head behind. They pump their wings constantly. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2012)

Today was a wonderful cool day in the mountains - the first we've had for weeks. The temperature stayed in the 70s. We're going down to LA tomorrow morning, and we might get back late too late to post.

File.Name: c925b_c925-007.jpg
Notes: 8025291172 This is looking eye-to-eyes with a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - I guess Spilomyia interrupta or kin. It has amazing patterned eyes. This one is perched on male staminate (failed) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2012)

File.Name: c925c_c925-010.jpg
Notes: 8025270227 This is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey of a new flower head of a staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. They wait, and wait, and wait... (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2012)

File.Name: c925d_c925-001.jpg
Notes: 8025244326 This is another beautiful California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly perched on a Raspberry (Rubus idaeus and/or R. strigosus) leaf in the garden today. It's been "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden. These butterflies are puzzling because they never visit flowers. I guess they're born with full stomachs and fly until they're empty. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2012)
ScientificName: Adelpha bredowii; Rubus idaeus; R. strigosus
associatedTaxa: Adelpha bredowii; Rubus idaeus; R. strigosus

File.Name: c925e_c924-024.jpg
Notes:
8025142232 Here is another photo of mating Bagrada Bugs (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera, this time on non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) in the Brassicaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

Thanks for the heads-up about this bug NatureShutterbug! Apparently they are native to northern Africa, and are now munching kale, Brussels sprouts, and broccoli in Lompoc. See this link and this.

But I am puzzled. I've seen them for at least a year - see my old photo (29 Aug 2011). It's odd that they would appear in the woods before the cultivated fields.
ScientificName: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Brassica nigra; Brassicaceae
associatedTaxa: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Brassica nigra; Brassicaceae

File.Name: c925f_c925-014.jpg
Notes:
8025102795 The best thing about having aphids in the garden, is finding the critters that aren't aphids!

I guess that shiny orange slug in the upper-middle of my photo is the maggot of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). It's consuming the Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii?) in the order Homoptera on a seedpod of an orange cultivar of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2012)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Aphis nerii; Homoptera; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Aphis nerii; Homoptera; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: c925g_c922-008.jpg
Notes:
8025066885 These are new female pistillate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. A week or so ago, I said it wouldn't flower this year, but I was wrong. It's not much of a flowering, but there are a few shrubs with real flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)

The female pistillate shrubs look white from a distance, and the male staminate shrubs look yellow.

File.Name: c925h_c923-014.jpg
Notes: 8024988493 These are new male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. A week or so ago, I said they would skip flowering in this dry year, but I was wrong. It's not much of a flowering, but there are a few. On the left, you can see a few dry failed flowers. Most of these shrubs have nothing but failed flowers, but this one has real flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)

File.Name: c926a_c922-041.jpg
Notes:
8028602959 I believe this is the integral stem gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae (Lepidoptera). The moth causes an abrupt swelling on the stems of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see the exit hole where the critter got out. Or did some other critter get in? (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2012)

I went down to L.A. today, and there was no chance for photos. But it is a chance to dip into my backlog of photos of insect galls, berries, and seeds - the signs of autumn.
CommonNames: Baccharis Stem Gall Moth; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c926b_c924-034.jpg
Notes:
8028548262 That goblet-like structure on the left is a galled flower of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's interesting because the real flowers were done last spring, and the berries were gone by mid-summer, but this galled flower is still on the shrub. See my photo of the flowers last April here. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

I don't know anything about these galls. They are not included in my book on "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, 2006). That's one good thing about noticing and photographing plant galls - you can always find something new!
CommonNames: Holly-Leaved Redberry; Field Guide to Plant Galls

File.Name: c926c_c924-030.jpg
Notes: 8028525154 I guess this is a gall - maybe an old gall - of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but cp. the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis). It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. There is an exit hole on the gall where the wasp got out - or is it an entrance hole where some parasite got in? (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)
CommonNames: Round Gall Wasp; Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c926d_c924-045.jpg
Notes: 8028502876 These are fantastic galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The mass of galls is made up of individual capsules containing just one gall wasp larvae each. They are barely visible here. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c926e_c924-039.jpg
Notes: 8028489650 These are the feathery seed plumes of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family. They look brilliant white with the sun behind them, though they look grayish when the sun is in front. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

File.Name: c926f_c924-050.jpg
Notes: 8028461559 These are pretty feathery plumes on seeds of (I think) native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. (It might also be S. virgata, but this plant is only about one foot tall.) Maybe only that seed in the back will ripen? They are still flowering, but the flowers close pretty early in the morning. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

File.Name: c926g_c923-041.jpg
Notes:
8028423357 Here are black ripe berries and red not-quite-ripe berries of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus tomentella?) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (I don't think I can reliably distinguish R. tomentella and R. californica, but the leaves of this one are white and fuzzy underneath.) The birds - especially the little Bushtits (Psaltriparus minimus) - eat the ripe berries as fast as they ripen at home. I guess they haven't found these plants on West Camino Cielo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2012)

I haven't tasted the berries. The plants are related to Cascara Sagrada (Rhamnus purshiana) which has a strong laxative effect. That's a good way to spread seeds though!
ScientificName: Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae; R. tomentella; R. californica; Psaltriparus minimus; Rhamnus purshiana
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae; R. tomentella; R. californica; Psaltriparus minimus; Rhamnus purshiana

File.Name: c926g_c924-043.jpg
Notes: 8028410139 Fall colors and ripe seeds are a sure sign of autumn. This is native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It's pretty enough to look at, but don't touch if you are sensitive! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2012)

File.Name: c927a_c927-053.jpg
Notes:
8031577494 I believe this a Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I couldn't get the upper side of the wings, but you can make out chevrons through its wings. There were quite a few of them working the flowers near the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 September 2012)

Bill and Barbara drove by while I was busy with this butterfly today and asked "Are you Treebeard?" Yep, I sure am - it was a pleasure to meet you. Fame!

File.Name: c927b_c927-018.jpg
Notes: 8031554231 Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterflies get no respect, but maybe they should. This skipper has beautiful blue-green colors on its back. I guess it's an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) on spent flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2012)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: c927c_c927-015.jpg
Notes:
8031521615 I believe this is a Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae of the order Diptera - or kin. It's a large honey bee size fly. It has a remarkable proboscis - it literally blows up its face to produce it. This one is working new male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2012)

The closest cactus is in our garden, some distance away.
ScientificName: Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c927d_c927-029.jpg
Notes:
8031465702 I believe this fly is the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria). It has the iridescent colors of a common Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) - a trash fly - but the bright orange cheeks distinguish it. This one is working new male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2012)

If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. This fly is native to North America. It's larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feeds on already dead tissue.

It actually has some medical uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. There's more info at Wikipedia.
CommonNames: Secondary Screwworm; Blow Fly; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Calliphoridae

File.Name: c927e_c927-028.jpg
Notes: 8031434338 This little pollen-dusted Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) is working composite flowers of male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2012)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c927f_c927-058.jpg
Notes: 8031397198 This is a large Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I reckon it's a Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa), but it's different from those bees at home. For one thing, it has iridescent blue colors. Maybe it's a Bumble Bee (genus Bombus)? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 September 2012)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Coastal Goldenbush; Carpenter Bee; Bumble Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae; Xylocopa; Bombus
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae; Xylocopa; Bombus

File.Name: c927g_c927-042.jpg
Notes: 8031375224 I found this handsome little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) on a stem of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river today. It's munching on a little Wild Bee, though you can't see it in this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 September 2012)

File.Name: c927h_c927-037.jpg
Notes:
8031336682 These are mating Bagrada Bugs (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera, this time on native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 September 2012)

These bugs have been in the local news lately. Apparently they are native to northern Africa, and are now munching kale, Brussels sprouts, and broccoli in Lompoc. See this link and this.

If these bugs are really destructive pests as reported, I don't think there's a chance of containing them. They seem well established in the backcountry on native shrubs. I've seen them for at least a year - see my old photo (29 Aug 2011). It's odd (and spooky) that they would appear in the woods before the cultivated fields.
ScientificName: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c928a_c928-017.jpg
Notes:
8034478762 This is a large Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. It's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long, bumblebee size. I found this one working the late flowers of native composite staminate Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. I know, it's not quite sharp - it was breezy today. But you can see this critter just fine! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2012)

I find these amazing flies on composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) down by the river, and Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) at home - my two favorite summer shrubs! I guess they prefer composites.

*Sigh* More hot weather is expected this weekend - triple-digits and above. Soon it will be over.
CommonNames: Spiny Tachina Fly; Coyote Bush; Scale Broom; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Baccharis pilularis
associatedTaxa: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Baccharis pilularis

File.Name: c928b_c928-024.jpg
Notes:
8034402646 I guess this is a small Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) - maybe Lasioglossum ovaliceps or kin. At first, I thought it might be a interesting Western Orussus (Orussis occedentalis) wasp in the family Orussidae - but no, it's definitely a bee, note the hairy legs. It's perched on a surprising new flower of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium - aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family - surprising because it's here, at the end of our very dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2012)

But I'm the first to admit that I might be wrong about this ID!
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Western Orussus; California Fuschia
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lasioglossum ovaliceps; Orussis occedentalis; Orussidae; Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lasioglossum ovaliceps; Orussis occedentalis; Orussidae; Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: c928c_c928-002.jpg
Notes: 8034374610 This is another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly resting on a plant of non-native English Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family in our garden this morning. Look how golden its abdomen is! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2012)

File.Name: c928d_c928-011.jpg
Notes: 8034349102 Do you see the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae)? This tiny Moth (Ledpidoptera) didn't, and paid the ultimate price. Look for the spider's legs about half-way up the moth on the screen-left, and follow them back. This is happening on a male staminate flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2012)

File.Name: c928e_c927-020.jpg
Notes: 8034318601 This is a pollen-dusted ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on a male staminate flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have no idea what kind of ant it is, but it seems to have one nodal bump on its abdomen. Sure, it's a deep crop. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2012)

File.Name: c928f_c927-024.jpg
Notes: 8034301947 This is the same little pollen-dusted Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) I showed yesterday. It's is working new male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Notice that little white patch on its forehead. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2012)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c928g_c927-007.jpg
Notes:
8034264634 This tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) is working a non-native Aster (genus Aster, Asteraceae) flower in the garden. We probably got this plant from my Mom many years ago. It's a leggy plant that always flops over with stems 6+ feet long. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2012)

I like the light in this photo. Any more, and the pale flowers would look white.

File.Name: c928h_c927-039.jpg
Notes:
8034242902 I believe this is a nymph of the Bagrada Bug (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera on native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river. It has a remarkable leg-like jointed proboscis, which it is sticking into a flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 September 2012)

These bugs have been in the local news lately. Apparently they are native to northern Africa, and are now munching kale, Brussels sprouts, and broccoli in Lompoc. See this link and this. It's a new invasion, but they've been here for a while.

If these bugs are really destructive pests as reported, I don't think there's a chance of containing them. They seem well established in the backcountry on native shrubs. I've seen them for at least a year - see my old photo (29 Aug 2011). It's odd (and spooky) that they would appear in the woods before the cultivated fields.
ScientificName: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c929a_c929-005.jpg
Notes:
8037794301 This must be some kind of Horse Fly (genus Tabanus) in the family Tabanidae of the order Diptera that I found in the garden this morning. It has a neat tan-colored mane over its thorax. Looking at Bugguide, it may well be the Western Horse Fly (Tabanus punctifer). It's quite large - bigger than a bee. It didn't try to bite me. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2012)

It got into the 90s today, and it's expected to be 10 degrees warmer tomorrow. *Sigh* I hope the heat ends soon. This has been a hot summer.

File.Name: c929b_c929-043.jpg
Notes:
8037716453 This is an interesting Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata that I found by a land-locked pool at the river today. It looks like a damselfly, but perches with wings open like a dragonfly. I guess this one is the California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica), but I don't really know. This one must be a male with those "claspers" at the end of its abdomen. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 September 2012)

I had to get my shoes wet to get this photo!

File.Name: c929c_c929-034.jpg
Notes:
8037664608 This is a rather large Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae) in the order Orthoptera hugging the stem of a plant that shouldn't be here on the river bed - Tamarisk, maybe Tamarix ramosissima in the Tamaricaceae plant family, though there are several species. This grasshopper is quite large, 2-1/2 - 3 inches long. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 September 2012)

Tamarisk is a horrible weed in the western states. It literally takes over the banks of rivers like the Green River, making it impossible to find a place to land on a float trip. These small plants on the Santa Ynez River are hopefully ephemeral - they will disappear the next time the river floods. But there are larger plants upstream that will keep them going. Munch away, little grasshopper!

File.Name: c929d_c929-006.jpg
Notes: 8037615754 This Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly is sharing a flower with a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera). The flower is a non-native Aster (genus Aster, Asteraceae plant family) flower in the garden. We probably got this plant from my Mom many years ago. It's a leggy plant that always flops over with stems 6+ feet long. I guess the butterfly is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2012)
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Wild Bee; Aster; Umber Skipper
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Aster; Asteraceae; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Aster; Asteraceae; Poanes melane

File.Name: c929e_c929-012.jpg
Notes:
8037557646 If I could just reach out and remove that darn branch...
... but of course I can't, it would change everything!

This is a new Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. There's also a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) in the air heading to the flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 September 2012)

You can see that this butterfly has a line of four or five "eyes" on her hindwing, unlike the American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly that has just two. (See this photo.
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Scale Broom; Honey Bee; American Lady
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Vanessa virginensis
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Vanessa virginensis

File.Name: c929f_c929-037.jpg
Notes:
8037541278 This is a spiky seed pod of magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family on a dry flat by the river. It seems to be wearing a little Mexican hat! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 September 2012)

Actually, Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

File.Name: c929g_c929-021.jpg
Notes: 8037523656 These are new native composite flowers of Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae just starting to appear on the dry flood plain by the river. It's another of our fall-flowering yellow-flowered composites, very pretty up close. These flower heads have ray-flowers, others lack them. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 September 2012)

File.Name: c929h_c929-038.jpg
Notes:
8037456329 These are new "flowers" of native Cocklebur (Xanthium strumarium) in the Asteraceae plant family. The male staminate flowers form on top and the bristly female flowers form below. They are hardly recognizable as flowers, but they are. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 September 2012)

The plants are said to be native, but they grow like a weed in disturbed places like the river wash. We've all had to pick the seedheads from our socks and shoe laces. It`s been called the original inspiration for velcro - or is that Burdock Thistle (genusArctium, family Asteraceae)?
ScientificName: Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae; velcro; Arctium; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae; velcro; Arctium; Asteraceae

File.Name: c930a_c930-037.jpg
Notes:
8041776749 Here is an amazing Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera perched for the moment on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plants in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our summer-dry creek. You can see its mighty proboscis neatly folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)

I keep thinking that *today* will be the day that I can't find any new photos, and I keep finding them!

Today got to 95∞ on San Marcos Pass and it was 107∞ down by the river where I was yesterday! And tomorrow is expected to be warmer. I hope it ends soon. I'm dreaming of mushrooms and mosses...

File.Name: c930b_c930-057.jpg
Notes:
8041672291 This is a Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae) in the order Orthoptera perfectly posed on a stem gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae (Lepidoptera). The moth causes an abrupt swelling on the stems of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's Oedaleonotus phryneicus, one of the the Spur-throated Grasshoppers ( Melanoplinae). (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)

I first thought this grasshopper was still a late-stage instar, with training wings - but Alice informs me it's a "short winged species". It has blue legs.
CommonNames: Short-Horned Grasshopper; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth; Coyote Bush; Spur-throated Grasshoppers
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus;  Melanoplinae
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus;  Melanoplinae

File.Name: c930c_c930-017.jpg
Notes:
8041615494 This is an athletic ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) working late-summer flowers of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. The ant has one large pyramid-shaped nodal bump on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)

We always keep a few of these plants around for the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies, and try to keep them trimmed so they don't make seeds. I've seen the adult butterflies, but in this very dry sumer, we have no caterpillars. I guess it's almost time to trim them again.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon

File.Name: c930d_c930-022.jpg
Notes: 8041572579 This is a small Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata) in the Chrysomelidae family of the order Coleoptera. It's a common garden pest, but it also shows up in the woods. This one is working staminate native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. You can see one of the failed flowers in the foreground. Most of these native shrubs have nothing but failed flowers. It's been too dry for even these shrubs to flower this year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)
ScientificName: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c930e_c930-045.jpg
Notes: 8041524694 This is a large yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a pretty new flower of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. This is another one of the autumn native plants that always surprise me by flowering at the harshest time of our summer-dry climate. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: c930f_c930-048.jpg
Notes:
8041466876 This is one of those WTF photos!

I found this nest - I guess it's an insect nest - on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks like a terrestrial Caddisfly made it, and it appears to be put together with black asphalt. How did any insect larva lug all those rocks (really sand grains) up to the leaf? WTF? (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)

I've found this critter before. I've found this critter before. See my earlier photo.

File.Name: c930g_c930-051.jpg
Notes: 8041422611 These are new seeds of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. The seeds are staked up in the three chambers of the pod like tiny poker chips. I really should collect a few. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: c930h_c930-049.jpg
Notes: 8041406286 These are elegant seed pods of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. The pods are full of seeds. I guess I should collect a few. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: ca01a_ca01-002.jpg
Notes:
8045447991 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working a new flower of composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2012)

It was too hot for insects today, and too hot for photographers! I found these butterflies in the morning when it was still a bit cooler. It got to 98∞ on the pass, and a whopping 110∞ down by the river. It's been warm lately.

Julie and I are heading to Oso Flaco Lake and the ocean tomorrow morning. We may stay the night in Guadalupe, in which case I'll miss a day of posting.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Bush Groundsel; Douglas' Groundsel

File.Name: ca01b_ca01-005.jpg
Notes: 8045402270 This is a two-fer of Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterflies - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). They are busy on a new flower of composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family today. I wasn't quite ready for this to happen - it's a quick photo with focus where it is! (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2012)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Bush Groundsel; Douglas' Groundsel
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: ca01c_c930-010.jpg
Notes: 8045384826 This is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working a new flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden, with the sun behind. You can see the line of 4 or 5 "eyes" on it's underwing. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)8045328220 Here is another brilliant male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a new flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden, with the sun behind. You can see some of its "dog face" through the wings. They are probably our most common butterfly over the summer, but it's a great challenge to capture the dog face on its upper wings since they always fold their wings up when at rest. It's our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Mexican Sunflower; California Dogface; Mexican Sunflower
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Tithonia rotundifolia; Colias eurydice; Tithonia rotundifolia

File.Name: ca01e_c930-007.jpg
Notes: 8045290462 Here is a brilliant male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly in the air above the new flower of a Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden, with the sun behind. It was working the flower just a split-second ago. They launch themselves incredibly fast! (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)

File.Name: ca01f_ca01-023.jpg
Notes: 8045234096 This is another Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae) in the order Orthoptera - probably Oedaleonotus phryneicus, one of the the Spur-throated Grasshoppers ( Melanoplinae). It has very short net-like wings. This grasshopper is trying to hide from me on a branch of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. There's also a dead-looking branch of Galium nuttallii in the Rubiacae plant family in the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2012)
CommonNames: Short-Horned Grasshopper; Spur-throated Grasshoppers; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Oedaleonotus phryneicus;  Melanoplinae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae

File.Name: ca01g_c930-016.jpg
Notes:
8045210410 This is another photo of the ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) that I showed yesterday. It's tasting a goblet of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)

Fennel is a bad weed in our mountains, but the insects like it. We always keep a few of these plants around for the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies, and try to keep them trimmed so they don't make seeds. I've seen the adult butterflies, but in this very dry summer, we have no caterpillars. I guess it's almost time to trim them again.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon

File.Name: ca01h_c930-039.jpg
Notes: 8045184428 This is another photo of the Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera in my [Previous photo]. This is the neighbor leaf of a different native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plant in the Asteraceae plant family that it jumped to. You can see its mighty proboscis neatly folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2012)

File.Name: ca02a_ca02-005.jpg
Notes:
8049087294 Here is another brilliant male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly in the air above the new flower of a Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden, with the sun behind. It's also known as the "flying pansy" - you can see why. It looks like it's flying towards the flower, but it's actually backing away after a visit. They launch themselves incredibly fast - just to challenge us photographers! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2012)

There are not many butterflies in the woods right now, and most of them are in our garden! There are California Dogface, California Sisters, Painted Ladies, Mormon Metalmarks, a few different skippers. I really hope we see a few Monarchs this fall, and maybe a Queen!

"And then inertia set in..."
We didn't get away to Oso Flaco today. It was a miserable hot night, and I just didn't feel like moving in the morning. It was another hot day, with a high of 102.9∞ according to a neighbors weather station - but it felt cooler to me. It got to 110∞ at the river according to the NWS weather station at Los Prietos. That's crazy.

File.Name: ca02b_ca02-002.jpg
Notes: 8049037379 This Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly is casting a perfect loop with its proboscis on a non-native flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2012)

File.Name: ca02c_ca02-025.jpg
Notes: 8048997051 Here is a cute little Short-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Acrididae) in the order Orthoptera sitting like a puppy dog on the tip of a leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei in the Liliaceae) plant family. Maybe it's a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus)? My photo is a bit over-exposed, but I love the pose! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2012)
CommonNames: Short-Horned Grasshopper; Chaparral Yucca; Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Melanoplus cinereus
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Melanoplus cinereus

File.Name: ca02d_ca02-018.jpg
Notes: 8048969165 This is a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It's sprawled on a thin stem of non-native Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family, blowing back and forth in the wind. This might well be the worst of our recent invasive weeds. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2012)
ScientificName: Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Piptatherum miliaceum; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Piptatherum miliaceum; Poaceae

File.Name: ca02e_ca02-024.jpg
Notes:
8048936722 "Who ordered the wild bee on a slab?"

This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae seems to be gazing intently at the unfortunate little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the leaf before her. She's a mama spider, with her egg case behind her. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2012)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: ca02f_ca02-006.jpg
Notes:
8048916479 Sorry to startle you!

This is a large yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on top of non-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plant has no flowers after this very dry year. I wonder what prey the spider is waiting for? (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2012)

File.Name: ca02g_ca02-010.jpg
Notes:
8048893710 This is a different large Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae in the woods today. This one has a different face on its abdomen that looks kind of sad to me. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2012)

It's a challenge to photograph these spiders even though they hang perfectly still in their webs. The slightest breeze sets them swinging.

File.Name: ca02h_ca02-011.jpg
Notes: 8048846979 This is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae - maybe the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) or kin. You can't see a cat face in the photo, but it definitely has some sort of face on it's abdomen! (See my

File.Name: ca03a_ca03-009.jpg
Notes:
8052168386 "The King is coming!"

This is a large Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - caught in the air - about to land on a non-native Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2012)

They are wonderful large butterflies, and they are actually kind of scarce here. It's odd, because tens of thousands of them overwinter a dozen miles away in trees by the coast. Welcome home little butterfly!

It was 20∞ cooler today than yesterday. There's even a rumor of rain next week! That the way it is in our autumn - it rocks (back and forth).

(I almost made it before the Presidential debate!)

File.Name: ca03b_ca03-001.jpg
Notes:
8052102379 "Ask and ye shall receive." I mentioned Monarchs in my comments comments yesterday, and here they are. I wish it was always that easy!

This is a large Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - the King. It's perched on a non-native Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2012)

You'd think Monarchs would be common here, since tens of thousands of them overwinter a dozen miles away in trees by the coast... but they are actually rather scarce, and I only see the in the fall. We have milkweed plants in the garden, which I hope they find. There's still time for another brood!

File.Name: ca03c_ca03-016.jpg
Notes: 8052067527 I found this near-perfect Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly in the garden this morning. It's "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil on our garden path. I love its golden body and those long silky hairs. The larger sizes look better. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2012)

File.Name: ca03d_c929-017.jpg
Notes: 8052054622 I'm not sure what exact species this pretty Paper Wasp is, but I'm pretty sure it is genus Polistes in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. There are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" - all handsome. This one is working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 September 2012)
ScientificName: Polistes; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Polistes; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: ca03e_c929-003.jpg
Notes: 8052014317 This is a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working a pale non-native Aster (genus Aster, Asteraceae plant family) flower in the garden. It never seems to actually lower its proboscis to the flower. Rather, it uses it's forelegs to lift the pollen. The flies look quite black to the eye, but they are definitely brown up close. We have bigger ones and smaller ones, and I have no idea if they are the same species or many. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2012)

File.Name: ca03f_c929-009.jpg
Notes: 8051997355 This California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family still has a few white flowers, but it's mostly turning brown for autumn. If you look close, you can see a rather large spider nest (I assume) inside the flower head. It's had a good summer despite the drought conditions. I'm sorry to see it go. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2012)

File.Name: ca03g_ca01-020.jpg
Notes: 8051970776 There are still new flowers on the native composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. Despite the drought conditions, it's had a good year. I really don't understand how these natives survive the long summer months with no rain, especially after I had to water the garden every day during our recent heat wave! (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2012)

File.Name: ca03h_ca01-021.jpg
Notes: 8051951265 Here are new flowers of a native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's another of the late-season composite flowers that grows along the river and on rocky banks like this road cut in the mountains. These late-season composite flowers keep the late-season insects alive. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2012)

File.Name: ca04a_ca04-053.jpg
Notes:
8055480437 Here is papa Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) in the woods. It's a forked-horn two-point buck, and I believe it's still hunting season. (The laws are really confusing.) They are safe on our property, though I really want to keep them out of our garden! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2012)

It's surrounded by Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta), California Bay (Umbellularia californica), Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus), and Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus). I can see its ribs. This must be a very hard year for wildlife, with so little rain. Somehow, the deer survive.
Locality: Lithocarpus densiflorus). I can see its ribs. This must be a very hard year for wildlife
CommonNames: Mule Deer; Coastal Wood Fern; California Bay; Wild Blackberry; Tanbark Oak
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; Dryopteris arguta; Umbellularia californica; Rubus ursinus; Lithocarpus densiflorus
associatedTaxa: Odocoileus hemionus; Dryopteris arguta; Umbellularia californica; Rubus ursinus; Lithocarpus densiflorus

File.Name: ca04b_ca04-044.jpg
Notes:
8055440091 Here is mama Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) lying down in the woods today. She looks like a big jack rabbit with those big ears! I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens, nothing else. I watched her kick a space for herself to lie down. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2012)

This is a wild deer in the woods, not one of those National Park deer that pose for photos.

File.Name: ca04c_ca04-011.jpg
Notes:
8055371318 You have to look close so see them...

This is a papa Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) with his two youngsters in the woods this morning. It's a forked-horn two-point buck, and I believe it's still hunting season. (The laws are really confusing.) They are safe on our property, though I want to keep them out of our garden! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2012)

Mama was there too, but I couldn't catch the whole family in a photo. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: ca04d_ca04-050.jpg
Notes: 8055349045 Here is a beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on the ground, among the dead leaves and acorns in the woods today. I haven't seen these butterflies for a while, and I reckon this one is newly emerged - though it already has a bite taken from its wing. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2012)

File.Name: ca04e_ca04-027.jpg
Notes: 8055309114 This is a pretty new flower of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. You have to admire these autumn native plants that pick the harshest time of our summer-dry climate to flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 October 2012)

File.Name: ca04f_ca04-019.jpg
Notes: 8055291211 This is a new flower of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family, along with lots of faded flowers. They seem to have had a pretty good year despite the low rainfall last winter, just 1/2 normal. I had to wait for the wind to have a calm moment for this photo. It's still not quite sharp. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 October 2012)

File.Name: ca04g_ca04-022.jpg
Notes:
8055243464 These "flowers" don't look like flowers at all, but they are. They are new flowers of Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. The plants dry out and blow in the wind, piling up as tumbleweeds along fences. The non-native weeds have tumbled across our whole country, east to west, spreading seeds all the way! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 October 2012)

I can't recall seeing these plants near home. Maybe they can blow along the river but can't tumble up into the mountains?

File.Name: ca04h_ca04-017.jpg
Notes:
8055196724 Does a burl count as a gall? If so, this is the biggest gall I've ever seen!

This is a huge burl on the trunk of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family at the bottom of the Synder trail that goes up to Knapp's Castle. It sure is interesting. I reckon that "bob wire" fence is three or four feet tall, for scale. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 October 2012)

My book on galls says that burls "usually do not involve invading organisms that stimulate their development. Rather, burls are the manifestations of dormant buds or adventitious buds, and, therefore are not true galls." But it's still very cool, and I'll post it to the Plant Galls group.

File.Name: ca05a_ca05-016.jpg
Notes:
8058110546 Here's another photo of the different Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working pollen-bearing staminate flowers of still-flowering Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo. Note the "failed flower" to the right. Many - even most - of the flowers have failed in this very dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2012)

There were interesting skies today. It's been cloudy, with mares' tails cirrus clouds and even a bit of mackerel sky. It was increasingly windy today, heading south, but the upper clouds were moving north. I'm starting to think there might be something to that chance of rain early next week.

File.Name: ca05b_ca05-022.jpg
Notes: 8058099238 This is a different Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working staminate flowers of still-flowering Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2012)

File.Name: ca05c_ca05-024.jpg
Notes: 8058073878 This is another one of those furry Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera that look quite black to the eye, but are definitely brown up close. This one is perched on a leaf of still-flowering Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. We have bigger ones and smaller ones, and I have no idea if they are one species or many. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2012)

File.Name: ca05d_ca05-002.jpg
Notes: 8058058199 Here is another remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - I guess Spilomyia interrupta or kin. It has amazing patterned eyes. This one is perched on a leaf in the garden. They have a distinctive high-pitched buzz when they fly, so I usually hear them before I see them. It looks a bit like the Yellow Jacket wasp in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2012)

File.Name: ca05e_ca05-001.jpg
Notes: 8058023552 This is a perfect Yellow Jacket-type wasp (family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera) perched on a leaf in the garden this morning. We seem to have a number of different wasps around the house, but they are difficult to photograph as they don't visit flowers. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2012)

File.Name: ca05f_ca05-026.jpg
Notes:
8058003838 This handsome Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) is committing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. It's robbing a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly" in the Lamiaceae plant family) in the garden. The hole in the sepal was probably already there. It seems many insects know this trick. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2012)

"Everyone does it so it must be alright!"
There's an interesting entry about nectar theft on Wikipedia.

File.Name: ca05g_ca05-009.jpg
Notes:
8057955260 These are flowers of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our summer-dry creek. Note that the growing tip of the plant has withered, but it has managed to produce flowers from below. Many plants in our canyon have withered tips and no flowers at all. I assume the flowers are wind-pollinated, but I do find insects and even predators on them. I reckon the pollen draws them. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2012)

These plants have a local following of people who use it in a poultice for poison oak. They are astringent, so it might help. (I don't get poison oak, so I won't comment.) "Mug-wort" is the mug plant, and of course you drink beer from mugs. I've heard that mugwort is used to flavor local Porter Beers - but maybe that's a different mugwort?

File.Name: ca05h_ca05-012.jpg
Notes: 8057940449 These are flowers of a head-high composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon. Though often considered a weed, it is a native species that competes very well with naturalized weeds in disturbed areas, often along roads. Insects don't seem to like it much. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2012)

File.Name: ca06a_ca06-010.jpg
Notes: 8061362311 I found this remarkable Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) on the only flowering shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon today. Maybe it's genus Lucilia? It has the iridescent metallic colors of the common trash flies, but maybe this is something else? For one thing, it's dusted with pollen, which is not trash fly behavior. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2012)
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Lucilia
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Lucilia

File.Name: ca06b_ca06-038.jpg
Notes: 8061342502 This is a pollen-smeared Long-horned Beetle (Cerambycidae) in the order Coleoptera on flower heads of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I don't know anything about it, but here it is! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 October 2012)

File.Name: ca06c_ca06-021.jpg
Notes: 8061306063 This is a female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. It was windy today, and it was hard to get this photo. The butterflies seem to prefer these flowers - probably because there are native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrubs in the Malvaceae plant family nearby - the preferred food plant for their caterpillars. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 October 2012)
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Coastal Goldenbush; Chaparral Mallow
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae

File.Name: ca06d_ca06-030.jpg
Notes:
8061253369 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - right next to a Bagrada Bug (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera. They are on composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 October 2012)

The Bagrada Bugs have been in the local news lately. Apparently they are native to northern Africa, and are now munching kale, Brussels sprouts, and broccoli in Lompoc. See this link and this.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Bagrada Bug; Coastal Goldenbush; Bagrada Bugs
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Bagrada hilaris; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae

File.Name: ca06e_ca06-016.jpg
Notes: 8061228965 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is waiting for insects on the failed flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Good luck little spider. The shrubs are mostly not flowering in this very dry year, though they are making empty scarious husks of flowers, like little wood roses. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2012)

File.Name: ca06f_ca06-019.jpg
Notes:
8061193104 Here are new and old flower heads on Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has willow-like leaves, but composite flowers. Honey Bees (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) like the flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 October 2012)

You've got to love these October flowers in our summer-dry climate- in a drought year!
ScientificName: Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera

File.Name: ca06g_ca06-005.jpg
Notes: 8061156346 This is another surprisingly beautiful grape-sized gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. There seems to be a shriveled gall of the same kind behind it - with an old exit hole? (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2012)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: ca06h_ca06-002.jpg
Notes: 8061116316 Here is a trio of grape-sized galls of (I think) the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) looks similar, but maybe it's restricted to Blue Oaks (Quercus douglasii)? These galls are growing on twigs of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2012)
CommonNames: Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Round Gall Wasp; Blue Oaks; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Besbicus conspicuus; Quercus douglasii; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Besbicus conspicuus; Quercus douglasii; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ca07a_ca07-054.jpg
Notes:
8065314186 This is a new butterfly for me - the Dainty Sulphur (Nathalis iole)!

It's working a flower head of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. This butterfly might be quite uncommon locally. I've never noticed it before, but it's small and it flies close to the ground, so it's easy to miss. I only saw this one, and I managed to chase it away before I could get a better photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 October 2012)

File.Name: ca07b_ca07-046.jpg
Notes:
8065272152 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae looks quite red. Of course! It has its nest in a plant of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family that has turned red for fall. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)

If there was any doubt, this photo proves that the spiders are color-shifters. I've posted many photos of these spiders lately, and I think this one is my favorite. There were spiderlings present.
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae

File.Name: ca07c_ca07-005.jpg
Notes:
8065216700 D'oh!

I'm sure you've done something clumsy, and then looked around to see if anyone noticed! This large pollen-dusted Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) just fell to the ground and flopped on its back around until I turned it over. Here it is pulling itself up on the weeds along my path. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)

File.Name: ca07d_ca07-014.jpg
Notes:
8065143125 I believe this is a granite-colored Thalassica grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica) grasshopper on my path today. It's a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) - very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)

This was the only grasshopper I found today, and maybe one of the last in our woods. I reckon the heat - and now the cool - finished them off.

File.Name: ca07e_ca07-004.jpg
Notes: 8065103858 I guess from its colors and vacuum-like proboscis that this is a small Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera on a leaf in the garden. I guess it's about 1/8 inch long. Maybe it's waiting for mushrooms. I am too! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)

File.Name: ca07f_ca07-033.jpg
Notes: 8065085338 This is an elegant seed pod of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. I really like this photo - very painterly. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)

File.Name: ca07g_ca07-028.jpg
Notes:
8065026485 Scenes from a drought year...

These are new flower buds of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. They normally form their buds in the fall to get ready to flower in the spring, but I wonder if these buds will make it. This plant is not usually "summer deciduous", and the leaves look dry and crumbly. . I hope it recovers. You have to respect the plant - forming new buds even while it's maybe dying. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)

File.Name: ca07h_ca07-035.jpg
Notes: 8064992549 This is a slice of life on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. There's a leaf, and a rather gray acorn. In the upper-left, opposite the leaf, you can see a new gall just beginning to grow. I guess from it's shape that its a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. See my photo yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: ca08a_ca08-003.jpg
Notes: 8069211643 This large Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) is flying into a new morning flower of Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) in the Onagraceae plant family - or possibly a hybrid? See my [Older] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2012)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Oenothera elata; hookeri; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Oenothera elata; hookeri; Onagraceae

File.Name: ca08b_ca08-004.jpg
Notes: 8069181564 This large Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) is flying into a new morning flower of Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) in the Onagraceae plant family - or possibly a hybrid? Look at that pollen on its legs! See my [Newer] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2012)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Oenothera elata; hookeri; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Oenothera elata; hookeri; Onagraceae

File.Name: ca08c_ca08-014.jpg
Notes: 8069115106 Here is a wonderful Jumping Spider in the genus Phidippus of the family Salticidae. It's perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our summer-dry creek. They have amazing eyes - it's looking at me! This one seems to be missing its left (screen-right) pedipalp. If you look close, you can maybe see that it's chelicerae are green! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2012)
ScientificName: Phidippus; Salticidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; chelicerae
associatedTaxa: Phidippus; Salticidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; chelicerae

File.Name: ca08d_ca08-001.jpg
Notes: 8069100924 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is waiting for prey on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2012)

File.Name: ca08e_ca08-027.jpg
Notes: 8069076984 This is another Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae guarding her egg sack in a shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. She is curled up very tightly. I love that furry little "control center" of eyes on the front of its face. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2012)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
